ecology.comments: The Convict Cichlid is a highly adaptable and hardy species, making it suitable for beginner aquarists who are prepared for its territorial nature. They are known for their striking black and white vertical stripes and can exhibit significant personality. While robust, their aggression, particularly during breeding, requires careful tank mate selection.
ecology.biotope: Native to Central America, these cichlids inhabit rivers and lakes, often found in areas with rocky substrates, submerged wood, and plenty of hiding places. They prefer clear, flowing to still waters.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly territorial and aggressive, especially when breeding. Best kept as a single pair in a species-specific tank or with other robust, similarly sized, and semi-aggressive cichlids in a very large aquarium. They will defend their territory vigorously against intruders.

diet.fooditems: High-quality cichlid pellets, flakes, frozen foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp), live foods (earthworms, small insects), and some vegetable matter (spirulina flakes, blanched spinach).
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed small amounts 2-3 times daily. A varied diet is crucial for their health and coloration. Ensure food sinks or is consumed quickly to avoid water quality issues.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Cave spawners with strong biparental care. The female lays eggs in a secluded cave or crevice, and both parents guard the eggs and fry fiercely.
reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide numerous caves, overturned flowerpots, rock crevices, or PVC pipes to serve as potential spawning sites. A flat rock or smooth surface within a cave is often preferred for egg deposition. Substrate can be sand or fine gravel.
reproduction.comments: Convict Cichlids are extremely prolific breeders and can spawn frequently. The parents will aggressively defend their territory, eggs, and fry from all other tank inhabitants. Fry are relatively easy to raise on crushed flake food, brine shrimp nauplii, or specialized fry foods.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny tank tyrant that breeds at the drop of a hat.
vibeLine.pro: Amatitlania nigrofasciata. Hardy, pugnacious cichlid; breeds relentlessly, guards fiercely.

flavorText.casual: Small but absolutely fearless, they'll boss around fish triple their size. Pair two up and you'll have babies almost immediately. Hardy and fascinating, just don't expect them to share the tank politely.
flavorText.pro: Amatitlania nigrofasciata is a hardy Central American cichlid and a textbook beginner breeder, pairing off and guarding fry with relentless aggression. Keep groups of five-plus to spread tension, or a single pair in a species tank.

ecology.comments: Inhabits fast-flowing streams and rivers. Grazes on driftwood.
ecology.biotope: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated tributaries of the Amazon basin, typically with stony beds and sunken wood.
ecology.socialBehavior: Generally peaceful, but males are territorial over hiding spots and caves.

diet.fooditems: algae, biofilm
diet.feedingPlaybook: Provide sinking algae wafers, fresh zucchini, spinach, and cucumber. Driftwood MUST be present in the tank as they need to ingest cellulose for proper digestion.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide narrow breeding caves, ceramic tubes, or hollow logs.
reproduction.comments: The male attracts the female to a cave, where she lays eggs. The male then guards and fans the eggs aggressively until they hatch.

vibeLine.casual: The bristly little algae machine that stays small.
vibeLine.pro: Ancistrus cirrhosus. Compact algae-grazer; needs driftwood for proper digestion.

flavorText.casual: The pleco that won't outgrow your tank, with a face only an aquarist could love. They graze algae tirelessly and need driftwood to rasp on. Far more practical than those foot-long common plecos.
flavorText.pro: Ancistrus cirrhosus stays compact and grazes algae, but requires driftwood to ingest cellulose for digestion. A cave-brooder; males grow bristles and guard eggs in a tube. Males are territorial over caves, so provide several.

ecology.comments: Betta splendens, commonly known as the Siamese Fighting Fish, is renowned for its vibrant colors and elaborate fins. Originating from Southeast Asia, these fish are adapted to survive in oxygen-poor, shallow waters. Males are highly territorial and aggressive towards other males, necessitating individual housing or careful consideration in community tanks. They are labyrinth fish, meaning they can breathe atmospheric air.
ecology.biotope: Found in stagnant or slow-moving waters such as rice paddies, canals, and floodplains. These environments are typically shallow, densely vegetated, and can have low oxygen levels. The water is often soft and slightly acidic to neutral.
ecology.socialBehavior: Males are extremely aggressive towards other males and should never be housed together. They can be kept alone or, in larger, heavily planted tanks, with peaceful, non-fin-nipping community fish. Female bettas can sometimes be kept in 'sorority' tanks of 5+ individuals, provided ample space and hiding spots, though this requires careful monitoring.

diet.fooditems: In their natural habitat, Betta splendens primarily feed on small insects, insect larvae, and zooplankton. In captivity, a high-quality diet consists of specialized betta pellets, supplemented with frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed small amounts 1-2 times daily, only what they can consume within a few minutes. Overfeeding can lead to digestive issues and poor water quality. Varying their diet with different types of protein-rich foods is crucial for optimal health and color.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: For breeding, a separate tank with very calm water, a shallow water level (around 15-20 cm), and floating plants (e.g., Salvinia, Frogbit) or broad leaves for the male to anchor his bubble nest is essential. A heater to maintain stable temperatures (26-28°C) is also important.
reproduction.comments: The male builds a nest of bubbles at the water surface. After spawning, the male collects the eggs and places them in the nest, diligently guarding them. The female should be removed immediately after spawning to prevent aggression from the male. Fry are very small and require infusoria or specialized liquid/powder fry food for the first few days.

vibeLine.casual: The little king that demands his own palace.
vibeLine.pro: Betta splendens. Territorial and requires warm, stable water with gentle surface access.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous and full of personality. They like having their space and will flare at their own reflection sometimes.
flavorText.pro: Best kept alone or with carefully chosen tank mates. Requires warm water (24–28°C) and access to the surface for breathing. Avoid strong currents.

hiddenLore: Magikarp from Pokémon. The ultimate underdog fish. Splash around long enough with perfect water parameters and you too might evolve into something legendary ✨
evolutionLore: Congratulations! Your Magikarp has evolved into Gyarados! The most powerful and intimidating fish in the tank. Rawr! 🐲

ecology.comments: The Common Goldfish is a highly adaptable and hardy species, making it a popular choice for beginner aquarists. They are coldwater fish and do not require a heater in most indoor environments, thriving in stable temperatures. Good filtration is crucial due to their messy eating habits and high bioload. They are tolerant of a wide range of water parameters but prefer stable conditions.
ecology.biotope: Originally found in slow-moving rivers, ponds, and lakes across East Asia. They prefer areas with dense vegetation and muddy substrates.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful and social, goldfish are best kept in groups of their own kind. They are not typically aggressive towards other fish but can outcompete smaller, slower species for food. Avoid keeping them with tropical fish due to differing temperature requirements.

diet.fooditems: High-quality goldfish flakes or pellets, live or frozen brine shrimp, bloodworms, daphnia, and blanched vegetables like peas, spinach, and zucchini. They enjoy foraging.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed small amounts two to three times a day, only what they can consume within a few minutes. Overfeeding can lead to digestive issues and poor water quality. A varied diet is essential for their health and vibrant coloration.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: To encourage spawning, provide fine-leaved plants (live or artificial) or spawning mops where eggs can be deposited. A slight increase in temperature and a diet rich in live foods can also stimulate breeding.
reproduction.comments: Goldfish are seasonal spawners, typically breeding in spring. The female scatters adhesive eggs among vegetation, which are then fertilized by the male. Parents may consume eggs and fry, so a separate breeding tank or removal of adults after spawning is recommended for successful rearing. Fry are very small and require infusoria or specialized fry food.

vibeLine.casual: The original tank buster that refuses to stay small.
vibeLine.pro: Carassius auratus. Coldwater species that grows much larger than most people expect.

flavorText.casual: Cute when small. Gets massive. Needs serious space and strong filtration. Not actually a beginner fish despite what the pet store said.
flavorText.pro: Common goldfish can live for decades and grow over 12 inches. Requires large tanks or ponds with excellent filtration. Not suitable for small aquariums long-term.

ecology.comments: The Bronze Corydoras is a highly adaptable and peaceful bottom-dwelling catfish, making it an excellent choice for beginner aquarists and community tanks. They are active scavengers and thrive when kept in groups.
ecology.biotope: Native to a wide range of habitats across South America, including clear and turbid streams, rivers, and ponds. They are often found in areas with soft substrates, leaf litter, and submerged vegetation.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social schooling fish; they must be kept in groups of at least 6, preferably more, to exhibit natural behaviors and reduce stress. They are peaceful and compatible with most other non-aggressive community fish.

diet.fooditems: Small invertebrates (such as worms and insect larvae), plant matter, algae, and detritus found in their natural habitat.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Primarily a bottom feeder, constantly sifting through the substrate for food. In the aquarium, they readily accept high-quality sinking pellets, flakes, frozen foods (like bloodworms and brine shrimp), and live foods.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg layer; females deposit adhesive eggs, often on broad leaves, tank glass, or decor, after a characteristic 'T-position' embrace with the male.
reproduction.layoutRequirement: A well-planted tank with fine sand or smooth gravel substrate is beneficial. Broad-leaved plants or smooth rocks provide ideal surfaces for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Spawning is often triggered by large, cool water changes mimicking seasonal rains. Parents typically do not guard the eggs, which hatch in approximately 3-5 days depending on temperature.

vibeLine.casual: The cheerful armored catfish that scoots in groups.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras aeneus. Hardy, peaceful bottom-dweller; needs a group and sand.

flavorText.casual: Endlessly busy little bottom-dwellers that bumble around in groups looking for food. Hardy, peaceful, and full of charm, they're a brilliant first catfish. Give them soft sand and at least a half-dozen friends.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras aeneus is a hardy, peaceful schooling catfish best kept in groups of six or more over smooth sand to protect their barbels. Egg-depositing spawners triggered by cool water changes. Avoid sharp substrate at all costs.

ecology.comments: The Peppered Corydoras is a peaceful, bottom-dwelling catfish ideal for community aquariums. They are known for their active foraging behavior and distinctive 'peppered' coloration. They thrive in well-established tanks with stable water parameters and benefit greatly from being kept in groups.
ecology.biotope: Native to slow-moving rivers, streams, and floodplains in South America, particularly the lower Paraná River basin. They are typically found in areas with soft substrates, dense vegetation, and leaf litter, where they can forage for food.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social and peaceful schooling fish. They should always be kept in groups of at least six individuals of their own species to ensure their well-being and natural behavior. They are excellent tank mates for other peaceful community fish.

diet.fooditems: Sinking pellets, high-quality flakes, frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and vegetable matter. They will also graze on algae and detritus.
diet.feedingPlaybook: As bottom feeders, it is crucial to provide sinking foods that reach the substrate. Feed small amounts multiple times a day, ensuring all fish get enough food. They will actively sift through the substrate for food particles.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer with adhesive eggs. They typically lay eggs on smooth surfaces such as tank glass, broad plant leaves, or decor.
reproduction.layoutRequirement: A breeding tank should include smooth surfaces for egg deposition, such as broad-leaved plants (e.g., Anubias, Amazon Swords) or flat rocks. Fine-leaved plants or spawning mops can also be used. A soft, sandy substrate is preferred.
reproduction.comments: Spawning is often triggered by large, cool water changes mimicking seasonal rains. The male and female engage in a 'T-position' embrace during spawning. Eggs are typically guarded by the female for a short period before being left to hatch. Fry are small and require infusoria or specialized fry food.

vibeLine.casual: The hardy, peppery classic that's happy in cooler water.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras paleatus. Tough, cool-tolerant cory; one of the easiest to breed.

flavorText.casual: A mottled green-grey old-timer that's tough as nails and fine in cooler tanks. Peaceful and busy, they snuffle the bottom in a contented little gang. A brilliant, forgiving first catfish.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras paleatus tolerates cooler water than most corys and is among the easiest to breed, depositing eggs on glass and plants after cool water changes. Keep six or more over smooth sand. Remove eggs to rear fry.

ecology.comments: The Siamese Algae Eater is a highly active and generally peaceful fish, renowned for its voracious appetite for various types of algae, including troublesome brush algae. They thrive in well-oxygenated water with good flow.
ecology.biotope: Native to fast-flowing streams and rivers in Southeast Asia, particularly Thailand and the Malay Peninsula. They are often found in areas with rocky substrates and submerged vegetation.
ecology.socialBehavior: Generally peaceful and best kept in groups of 3-5 or more to spread out any potential territoriality among conspecifics, especially in smaller tanks. They are excellent community fish, compatible with most non-aggressive species.

diet.fooditems: Algae (including red brush algae, diatoms, and green algae), biofilm, high-quality sinking pellets or flakes, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach), and occasional protein-rich foods like frozen bloodworms or brine shrimp.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Provide a varied diet consisting primarily of high-quality sinking pellets or flakes. Supplement heavily with blanched vegetables and ensure ample natural algae growth in the aquarium. Offer protein-rich foods sparingly as a treat.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg layer, open water/substrate spawners. Not commonly bred in home aquariums.
reproduction.layoutRequirement: For potential breeding, a very large aquarium with strong water flow, numerous hiding spots among rocks and driftwood, and fine-leaved plants is recommended. Dim lighting may also encourage spawning behavior.
reproduction.comments: Breeding Siamese Algae Eaters in a home aquarium is extremely challenging and rare. Most commercially available specimens are wild-caught or bred in large-scale aquaculture facilities using hormone induction. If spawning occurs, eggs are typically scattered and parental care is absent.

vibeLine.casual: The hardworking cleaner that actually eats the tough algae.
vibeLine.pro: Crossocheilus oblongus. Genuine algae-grazer; one of few that eats hair algae.

flavorText.casual: One of the few fish that genuinely tackles stubborn algae, including the dreaded black beard kind. Active and mostly peaceful, they get more boisterous with age. Make sure you buy the real thing, as look-alikes abound.
flavorText.pro: Crossocheilus oblongus is among the few fish that eat hair and black beard algae, though appetite shifts toward prepared foods as they mature. Active and semi-territorial when older. Frequently mislabeled; verify the species before buying.

ecology.comments: The Zebra Danio is an extremely hardy, active, and peaceful schooling fish, making it an excellent choice for beginner aquarists. They are known for their distinctive horizontal stripes.
ecology.biotope: Native to slow-moving to moderately flowing streams, canals, ditches, and rice paddies across South Asia, particularly India and Bangladesh. They prefer areas with dense vegetation.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social and active schooling fish. They must be kept in groups of at least 6 individuals to thrive, reduce stress, and display natural behaviors. Keeping fewer can lead to shyness or nipping.

diet.fooditems: Small invertebrates, insect larvae, zooplankton, algae, and some plant matter.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Accepts a wide variety of foods including high-quality flake food, micro-pellets, and small live or frozen foods such as daphnia, brine shrimp, and bloodworms. Feed small amounts multiple times a day to match their active metabolism.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: For breeding, a separate tank with fine-leaved plants like Java moss or a spawning mop is recommended to provide a substrate for eggs to adhere to or fall into. A mesh bottom can also protect eggs from being eaten.
reproduction.comments: Zebra Danios are relatively easy to breed. They are non-parental and will readily consume their own eggs and fry. Spawning often occurs at dawn. Removing the adults after spawning is crucial for fry survival.

vibeLine.casual: The tireless little speedster that never sits still.
vibeLine.pro: Danio rerio. Bulletproof, fast-moving shoaler ideal for cycling new tanks.

flavorText.casual: Hardy, energetic, and almost impossible to discourage. They zip around the top half of the tank all day and shrug off a wide range of temperatures. A great first fish, just give them room to sprint.
flavorText.pro: Danio rerio tolerates cooler water and wide parameter swings, making it a classic starter species. Keep six or more to curb fin-nipping energy. A tight-fitting lid is recommended, as they jump.

ecology.comments: The Black Skirt Tetra is a hardy and active schooling fish, making it an excellent choice for beginner aquarists and community tanks. They are generally peaceful but should be kept in groups of at least six individuals to prevent fin-nipping behavior towards slower, long-finned tank mates. Provide ample swimming space and areas with dense planting for security.
ecology.biotope: Native to the slow-moving rivers, tributaries, and floodplains of the Paraguay and Guaporé River basins in South America, including parts of Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina. They are typically found in areas with dense vegetation and submerged roots.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social schooling fish. Must be kept in groups of 6 or more to thrive and exhibit natural behavior. Keeping them in smaller numbers can lead to stress and potential aggression towards other fish.

diet.fooditems: In the wild, their diet consists of small invertebrates, crustaceans, and some plant matter. In the aquarium, they readily accept high-quality flake food, micro-pellets, and a variety of frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Offer a varied diet to ensure optimal health and coloration. Feed small amounts 1-2 times daily, ensuring all food is consumed within a few minutes to prevent water pollution.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: For breeding, a separate breeding tank with dim lighting is recommended. Provide fine-leaved plants like Java moss, spawning mops, or a spawning grid on the bottom to protect the eggs from predation by the parents. Water should be soft and slightly acidic.
reproduction.comments: Black Skirt Tetras are relatively easy to breed in a home aquarium. Condition breeding pairs with a diet rich in live and frozen foods. Spawning typically occurs in the morning. After eggs are laid and fertilized, remove the parents immediately as they will consume the eggs. Fry are very small and require infusoria or liquid fry food for the first few days, transitioning to newly hatched brine shrimp as they grow.

vibeLine.casual: The flowy-finned classic that fades from black to silver.
vibeLine.pro: Gymnocorymbus ternetzi. Hardy mid-water tetra; occasional fin-nipper in small groups.

flavorText.casual: An old-school favorite that's tough and adaptable. Their dusky fins fade with age but they keep their charm. Keep a proper group or they may get nippy with flowing fins.
flavorText.pro: Gymnocorymbus ternetzi is a robust, long-lived tetra. Understocked groups can nip trailing fins, so keep six or more. Tolerant of a broad parameter range, making it forgiving for newer keepers.

ecology.comments: The Glowlight Tetra is a peaceful, schooling fish native to South America. They thrive in well-planted aquariums with stable water parameters and are an excellent choice for community tanks with other small, non-aggressive species. Their distinctive orange stripe makes them a popular choice for aquarists.
ecology.biotope: Found in slow-moving tributaries, clearwater, and blackwater streams of the Essequibo River basin in Guyana. Their natural habitat often features dense vegetation, submerged roots, and leaf litter, providing ample hiding spots and subdued lighting.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social and peaceful schooling fish. They should be kept in groups of at least 6-10 individuals to reduce stress and encourage natural behavior. Compatible with most other peaceful community fish.

diet.fooditems: In the wild, their diet consists of small invertebrates, insect larvae, zooplankton, and some plant matter. In the aquarium, they readily accept high-quality flake foods, micro-pellets, and small frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Offer a varied diet of high-quality flake food supplemented with frozen or live foods several times a day in small portions that can be consumed within a few minutes. This ensures complete nutrition and mimics their natural foraging behavior.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: To encourage spawning, a dedicated breeding tank with soft, acidic water (pH 6.0-6.5, GH 2-4) and a temperature of 26-28°C (79-82°F) is recommended. Provide fine-leaved plants like Java moss or a spawning mop for egg deposition. Dim lighting is preferred.
reproduction.comments: Glowlight Tetras are relatively easy to breed, though raising the fry can be challenging. The parents should be conditioned with live foods prior to spawning. After eggs are laid and fertilized, the parents should be removed promptly as they will consume the eggs. Fry hatch in about 24-36 hours and become free-swimming a few days later, requiring infusoria or liquid fry food initially, then newly hatched brine shrimp.

vibeLine.casual: The gentle one with a glowing orange racing stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Hemigrammus erythrozonus. Peaceful soft-water tetra for calm community tanks.

flavorText.casual: A quiet charmer with a neon-orange line that seems to glow under good lighting. Peaceful and easygoing, they pair well with other gentle fish. Give them a planted tank and a group to feel secure.
flavorText.pro: Hemigrammus erythrozonus is a peaceful, undemanding tetra that suits planted community setups. The luminous lateral stripe pops over dark substrate. Keep six or more for natural shoaling and reduced timidity.

ecology.comments: The Red Eye Tetra is a vibrant and active schooling fish, easily recognized by its distinctive red upper iris. They are generally peaceful and make an excellent addition to a community aquarium, provided they are kept in a sufficiently large group. They appreciate a well-planted tank with open swimming areas.
ecology.biotope: Native to slow-moving rivers, streams, and tributaries across South America, particularly in the Paraguay and Guaporé River basins. They are often found in areas with dense vegetation, submerged roots, and leaf litter.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social schooling fish; it is crucial to keep them in groups of at least 6-8 individuals to ensure their well-being and reduce stress. When kept in small numbers, they can become timid or, conversely, prone to fin-nipping other tankmates. They are generally compatible with other peaceful community fish of similar size.

diet.fooditems: High-quality flake food, micro-pellets, frozen brine shrimp, bloodworms, daphnia, and finely chopped vegetable matter.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Offer a varied diet to ensure optimal health and coloration. Feed small amounts 2-3 times daily, ensuring all food is consumed within a few minutes. Supplement dry foods with frozen or live foods several times a week.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: A separate breeding tank (10-15 gallons) with soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0-6.5, GH 4-8) and a temperature around 26-27°C. Provide fine-leaved plants like Java moss or a spawning mop for egg deposition. Dim lighting is preferred.
reproduction.comments: Condition a pair or a small group with live and frozen foods. Spawning typically occurs in the morning. The parents will scatter adhesive eggs among the plants. Remove the adults immediately after spawning to prevent them from eating the eggs. Eggs hatch in 24-36 hours, and fry become free-swimming within 3-5 days. Feed infusoria or liquid fry food initially, transitioning to newly hatched brine shrimp as they grow.

vibeLine.casual: The silver tetra with a bright red eye.
vibeLine.pro: Moenkhausia sanctaefilomenae. Hardy, active tetra; can nip in small groups.

flavorText.casual: A flash of red eye and a silver body that catches the light. Hardy and active, they're a solid community pick as long as you keep a proper group. Understocked, they can get nippy with slower fish.
flavorText.pro: Moenkhausia sanctaefilomenae is a hardy, active tetra that can nip trailing fins when kept in small numbers. Groups of eight or more diffuse the energy. Tolerant of a wide parameter range; an egg-scatterer.

ecology.comments: The Neon Tetra is a classic beginner-friendly freshwater fish, renowned for its vibrant iridescent blue and red stripe. They are peaceful and thrive in well-established aquariums. Maintaining stable water parameters is crucial for their long-term health. They are sensitive to sudden changes in water chemistry.
ecology.biotope: Native to the clearwater and blackwater streams and tributaries of the Amazon basin in southeastern Colombia, eastern Peru, and western Brazil. They typically inhabit areas with dense vegetation and leaf litter, providing ample hiding spots and subdued lighting.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social schooling fish. They must be kept in groups of at least 6 individuals, but larger schools (10+) are highly recommended to observe their natural schooling behavior and reduce stress. They are peaceful and suitable for community tanks with other small, non-aggressive species.

diet.fooditems: High-quality flake food, micro-pellets, freeze-dried bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Live or frozen foods should be offered regularly to supplement their diet and enhance their coloration.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed small amounts two to three times a day, only what they can consume within a few minutes. Overfeeding can lead to water quality issues and health problems for the fish.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Breeding Neon Tetras requires a separate spawning tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5-6.5, GH 1-4). The tank should contain fine-leaved plants like Java moss or a spawning mop for egg deposition. Dim lighting is essential as the eggs are light-sensitive.
reproduction.comments: Breeding Neon Tetras in a home aquarium can be challenging. After spawning, the parents should be removed immediately as they will consume the eggs. The eggs typically hatch within 24-36 hours, and the fry become free-swimming after 3-4 days. They require infusoria or specialized liquid fry food initially, transitioning to newly hatched brine shrimp as they grow.

vibeLine.casual: The classic little blue-and-red rocket that makes every tank feel alive.
vibeLine.pro: Paracheirodon innesi. Schooling tetra; best color in stable, soft water.

flavorText.casual: Tiny, fast, and ridiculously pretty. They look even better when they’re in a proper school. One or two just looks lonely.
flavorText.pro: Neon Tetras thrive in established, well-maintained tanks. Sensitive to ammonia and sudden parameter swings. Best kept in groups with gentle flow.

hiddenLore: Nami, the Tidecaller from League of Legends, secretly blesses this species. Keep your tank parameters perfect and she may grant you the perfect water flow ✨

ecology.comments: The Rosy Barb is a hardy and active species, making it an excellent choice for beginner aquarists. They are known for their vibrant coloration, especially the males during breeding. They thrive in well-oxygenated tanks with plenty of swimming space.
ecology.biotope: Native to slow-moving streams, rivers, ponds, and lakes across South Asia, particularly India and Bangladesh. They are often found in areas with dense vegetation and clear water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Rosy Barbs are schooling fish and should be kept in groups of at least 6 individuals to ensure their well-being and reduce stress. While generally peaceful, they can be prone to fin-nipping if kept in too small a group or with very slow-moving, long-finned tankmates. They are best kept with other active, similarly sized community fish.

diet.fooditems: They readily accept a wide variety of foods including high-quality flake food, small pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and vegetable matter like blanched spinach or spirulina flakes.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Offer a varied diet consisting of high-quality dry foods supplemented with frozen or live foods several times a day. Feed small amounts that can be consumed within a few minutes to prevent overfeeding and water quality issues.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: To encourage spawning, provide a separate breeding tank with fine-leaved plants such as Java moss or artificial spawning mops for the eggs to fall into. Dim lighting and slightly softer, acidic water can also be beneficial.
reproduction.comments: Rosy Barbs are relatively easy to breed in a home aquarium. Condition the breeding pair with a diet rich in live and frozen foods. The parents will typically eat their eggs after spawning, so it is advisable to remove the adults once eggs are laid or use a spawning grid at the bottom of the tank to protect the eggs.

vibeLine.casual: The hardy charmer happy in a cooler tank.
vibeLine.pro: Pethia conchonius. Robust, active barb tolerant of unheated coolwater setups.

flavorText.casual: Tough, active, and forgiving of beginner mistakes. Males flush rosy-pink, especially in cooler water. They're energetic swimmers, so give them space and a few friends to chase around.
flavorText.pro: Pethia conchonius is a robust, active barb comfortable in unheated tanks down to roughly 18°C. Keep six or more to spread out their energetic, occasionally nippy behavior. Males color up best in cooler water.

ecology.comments: Guppies are highly adaptable and robust fish, making them an excellent choice for beginner aquarists. They thrive in well-maintained aquariums with stable water parameters. While tolerant of a range of conditions, optimal care will result in more vibrant colors and healthier fish.
ecology.biotope: Naturally found in slow-moving streams, canals, ditches, and coastal brackish waters throughout Central and South America. They prefer areas with dense vegetation, which provides shelter and foraging opportunities.
ecology.hardnessRange: Moderately hard to hard (10-25 dGH) is preferred, though they can adapt to slightly softer water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful and social schooling fish. They should be kept in groups of at least six individuals to promote natural behavior and reduce stress. Males can be persistent in their pursuit of females, so a ratio of 2-3 females per male is recommended. Avoid housing with aggressive or fin-nipping species.

diet.fooditems: High-quality flake food, micro-pellets, live or frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, and vegetable matter such as spirulina flakes.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed small amounts 2-3 times daily, offering only what they can consume within a few minutes. A varied diet is crucial for optimal health, coloration, and to prevent digestive issues.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Densely planted tanks with plenty of floating plants provide essential shelter for newborn fry, protecting them from adult fish (including their parents) which may prey on them.
reproduction.comments: Guppies are extremely prolific breeders, often referred to as 'millionfish' due to their rapid reproduction. Females can store sperm and give birth to multiple batches of fry (typically 20-100) over several months from a single mating. Fry are born live, fully formed, and immediately free-swimming. Breeding is straightforward in a community tank, but dedicated breeding setups can maximize fry survival.

vibeLine.casual: The colorful, unstoppable little breeders of the hobby.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia reticulata. Extremely hardy and prolific livebearer.

flavorText.casual: Beautiful, easy to keep, and they multiply faster than you expect. Great for beginners, but be ready for babies.
flavorText.pro: One of the hardiest and most adaptable species. Excellent for new tanks and community setups, though selective breeding lines can be more delicate.

ecology.comments: Mollies are hardy and adaptable fish, making them excellent for beginner aquarists. They can tolerate a range of water conditions, including slightly brackish water, though they thrive in hard, alkaline freshwater.
ecology.biotope: Native to coastal fresh and brackish waters of Central and South America, often found in slow-moving rivers, estuaries, and even marine environments.
ecology.socialBehavior: Generally peaceful and social, best kept in groups of 3 or more, with a higher ratio of females to males (e.g., 2-3 females per male) to prevent male harassment. Can be prone to fin-nipping if kept in too small a tank or with very slow-moving, long-finned tankmates.

diet.fooditems: Primarily herbivorous, consuming algae and plant matter. Also accepts high-quality flake food, pellets, spirulina flakes, blanched vegetables (e.g., zucchini, spinach), and occasional live or frozen foods like brine shrimp or daphnia.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed small amounts 2-3 times daily. A varied diet rich in vegetable matter is crucial for their health and vibrant coloration. Avoid overfeeding.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense planting (e.g., Java moss, hornwort) and floating plants provide crucial hiding spots for fry. A separate breeding tank may be necessary to protect fry from adults.
reproduction.comments: Mollies are prolific livebearers, meaning they give birth to live, free-swimming fry. Breeding is relatively easy in a well-maintained aquarium. Gestation period is typically 30-40 days, producing 20-100 fry per brood.

vibeLine.casual: The easygoing livebearer that loves hard, mineral-rich water.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia sphenops. Hard-water livebearer; prolific and mildly brackish-tolerant.

flavorText.casual: Friendly, active, and quick to breed, so you'll have fry before you know it. They do best in harder, alkaline water with some mineral content. Give them space; they're bigger and busier than they look.
flavorText.pro: Poecilia sphenops thrives in hard, alkaline water and tolerates low-end brackish conditions. A prolific livebearer that drops broods every four to six weeks; provide cover or separate gravid females to save fry. Soft, acidic water invites health issues.

diet.fooditems: plants (unspecified plants); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Hardy, active, and will demolish every live plant.
vibeLine.pro: Psalidodon anisitsi. Robust subtropical tetra; notorious plant consumer, beginner-proof.

flavorText.casual: Nearly indestructible and endlessly active, but they'll shred any live plants down to stumps. Perfect for a high-energy, plastic-plant or hardscape tank. Great starter fish if you skip the planted setup.
flavorText.pro: Psalidodon anisitsi tolerates a wide temperature range (18–26°C) and variable water chemistry, making it extremely hardy. Voracious plant consumer — plastic or robust plants like Java fern only. Egg-scattering spawner; prolific in well-conditioned pairs separated then reunited. Keep in groups of six-plus.

ecology.comments: The Freshwater Angelfish is a graceful and popular cichlid known for its distinctive disc-shaped body and flowing fins. While generally peaceful, they can be territorial, especially during breeding, and may predate on very small fish. They thrive in well-maintained aquariums with stable water parameters.
ecology.biotope: Native to the slow-moving, clear waters of the Amazon basin in South America, including tributaries, floodplains, and swamps. They are typically found among dense vegetation, submerged roots, and driftwood, which provide cover and hunting grounds.
ecology.socialBehavior: Angelfish can be kept as a bonded pair or in groups of 5 or more in larger aquariums (55 gallons+). Keeping them in odd numbers or too few individuals can lead to aggression. They establish a pecking order, and a larger group helps diffuse aggression. Avoid housing with fin-nippers or extremely small, timid fish.

diet.fooditems: High-quality flake food, sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and occasional live foods like daphnia. Vegetable matter can also be offered.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed small amounts 2-3 times daily, offering a varied diet to ensure complete nutrition. Ensure food is small enough for them to consume easily. Overfeeding should be avoided to maintain water quality.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Substrate spawners. Angelfish lay their adhesive eggs on vertical surfaces such as broad plant leaves (e.g., Amazon swords), slate, PVC pipes, or even the aquarium glass. Both parents typically guard the eggs and fry.
reproduction.layoutRequirement: For breeding, provide large, broad-leafed plants (like Amazon swords), pieces of slate, or ceramic spawning cones. A separate breeding tank is often recommended to protect eggs and fry from other tank inhabitants and to allow parents to focus on care without stress.
reproduction.comments: Breeding Angelfish can be a rewarding experience. A healthy, well-conditioned pair will often initiate spawning. Water quality is paramount for successful egg hatching and fry development. Fry are free-swimming after a few days and can be fed newly hatched brine shrimp or microworms.

vibeLine.casual: The tall, graceful icon of the planted tank.
vibeLine.pro: Pterophyllum scalare. Tall cichlid; peaceful until paired, then territorial.

flavorText.casual: Elegant, tall, and full of presence, they glide through a planted tank like living sails. They're cichlids, though, so they get bossy when breeding. Don't trust them around tiny fish like neons.
flavorText.pro: Pterophyllum scalare is a tall-bodied cichlid, peaceful in groups until a pair forms and claims territory. Bonded pairs are devoted vertical-surface spawners, laying eggs on broad leaves or slate. Needs tank height; will eat fish small enough to fit its mouth.

ecology.comments: The Tiger Barb is a highly active and visually striking schooling fish, recognized by its distinctive black and orange stripes. They are known for their energetic swimming and can be somewhat boisterous, occasionally nipping at the fins of slower-moving or long-finned tankmates. Keeping them in a larger school (6 or more individuals) helps to distribute their playful aggression among themselves, making them less prone to bother other species.
ecology.biotope: Native to clear, slow-moving streams, tributaries, and swamps in Southeast Asia, particularly on the islands of Sumatra and Borneo. Their natural habitats typically feature dense aquatic vegetation, submerged roots, and a substrate of sand or fine gravel.
ecology.socialBehavior: Highly social and schooling fish. They thrive in groups of at least 6-8 individuals. When kept in insufficient numbers, they can become stressed and more aggressive towards other tank inhabitants. They are best kept with other fast-moving, robust fish that can tolerate their energetic nature.

diet.fooditems: High-quality flake food, small pellets, frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and vegetable matter such as blanched spinach or spirulina flakes.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Offer a varied diet to ensure optimal health and vibrant coloration. Feed small amounts two to three times daily, ensuring all food is consumed within a few minutes. Supplement dry foods with frozen or live options several times a week, and include vegetable matter regularly.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: A separate breeding tank of 10-15 gallons is recommended. The tank should have soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0-6.5, dGH 5-8) and a temperature of 26-27°C. Provide fine-leaved plants like Java moss, spawning mops, or a mesh bottom/marbles to protect the eggs from parental predation. Dim lighting is preferred.
reproduction.comments: Tiger Barbs are relatively easy to breed. Condition a pair or a group of two males to one female with plenty of live and frozen foods. Spawning typically occurs in the early morning, with the female scattering hundreds of adhesive eggs among the plants. Parents should be removed immediately after spawning, as they will readily consume their own eggs. Eggs usually hatch within 24-36 hours, and fry become free-swimming in 3-5 days. Feed fry infusoria or liquid fry food initially, transitioning to newly hatched brine shrimp as they grow.

vibeLine.casual: The cheeky stripe gang that nips first and asks questions never.
vibeLine.pro: Puntigrus tetrazona. Active semi-aggressive barb; keep in groups of six-plus.

flavorText.casual: Bold, fast, and full of attitude. Keep them in a proper group of six or more and they'll squabble among themselves instead of harassing your other fish. Skip the long-finned tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Puntigrus tetrazona is a notorious fin-nipper when understocked. A tight group of eight or more diffuses aggression within the shoal. Avoid pairing with bettas, angelfish, or other slow, trailing-finned species.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore in nature feeding on detritus, algae, small invertebrates and insect larvae. In aquaria accepts flakes, micro pellets, frozen daphnia, brine shrimp, and bloodworm.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The mellow barb that blushes deep cherry red.
vibeLine.pro: Puntius titteya. Peaceful, hardy barb; males redden during display.

flavorText.casual: Unlike its pushier barb cousins, this one is calm and easygoing. Males turn a rich cherry red when they're showing off. Hardy and adaptable, they're a relaxed pick for a community tank.
flavorText.pro: Puntius titteya is a peaceful exception among barbs. Males intensify to deep red during courtship and competition. Hardy and adaptable; a small group keeps them confident and colorful.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The African cichlid that turns blazing red when breeding.
vibeLine.pro: Rubricatochromis bimaculatus. Territorial African cichlid; stunning breeding color.

flavorText.casual: Normally a modest olive, they explode into fiery red when they pair up and breed. Beautiful but seriously territorial during spawning — they'll chase everything else into the corners. Plan accordingly.
flavorText.pro: Rubricatochromis bimaculatus is a West African substrate spawner that intensifies to vivid red during breeding. Extremely territorial as a pair; best in a species tank or with robust, fast tankmates. Hardy and prolific once paired.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on insects and other small invertebrates in nature. Captive fish will accept dried products but should also be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia , or bloodworm regularly to ensure development of optimal colour and condition. Small insects such as m

reproduction.comments: This species is a bubblenester which forms temporary pair bonds. Some hobbyists prefer to isolate individual pairs for spawning purposes whereas others may maintain a small group. Either way the tank to be used for spawning should be furnish it with plenty of floating and/or stem plants with leaves reaching the surface. Gentle filtration via an air-powered sponge filter is the best option, as it will not break up the bubblenest of the male or suck up fry. The tank should have the tightest-fittin

vibeLine.casual: The shy golden sweetheart that warms up to you.
vibeLine.pro: Trichogaster chuna. Peaceful labyrinth fish; far hardier than the dwarf gourami.

flavorText.casual: A gentle little golden glow of a fish. They're timid at first but grow bold and curious once settled. Calm, undemanding, and a much hardier choice than the flashier dwarf gourami.
flavorText.pro: Trichogaster chuna is a peaceful labyrinth fish that breathes surface air and prefers calm, planted water. Hardier and less disease-prone than Trichogaster lalius. Males build bubblenests and color up deep amber when breeding.

diet.fooditems: Thought to be omnivorous in nature, feeding on small invertebrates, algae and other aufwuchs . It accepts most foods offered in the aquarium , and a good quality dried food is acceptable as the staple diet. Supplement this with regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm for the f

reproduction.comments: Like many anabantoids, it’s a bubble-nesting species . Breeding is not too difficult, although the males can be somewhat unpredictable in terms of behaviour. You’ll need to set up a separate tank for spawning purposes. Something around 18″-24″ in length is fine. There’s no need to use a substrate , but a handful or two of peat fibre may be beneficial. Also add some good sized clumps of hornwort or other fine-leaved plant and some floating plants such as Riccia . A small air-driven sponge filter

vibeLine.casual: The shimmering centerpiece that breathes air from the surface.
vibeLine.pro: Trichogaster lalius. Labyrinth fish; watch for Dwarf Gourami Iridovirus in stock.

flavorText.casual: Males are living jewels in shades of red and blue, and they gulp air from the surface like little dragons. They can be a bit shy, so give them calm tankmates and gentle flow. Choose healthy stock carefully.
flavorText.pro: Trichogaster lalius is a labyrinth fish that breathes atmospheric air and prefers calm, warm water. Source carefully, as farmed stock is prone to Dwarf Gourami Iridovirus and bacterial infections. One male per tank avoids conflict.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The peaceful one wearing a little black triangle.
vibeLine.pro: Trigonostigma heteromorpha. Hardy, peaceful shoaler; signature black wedge marking.

flavorText.casual: A longtime community favorite with a coppery body and a bold black wedge. Calm, hardy, and happy in a group, they're a great match for almost any peaceful tank. They love planted setups.
flavorText.pro: Trigonostigma heteromorpha is a hardy, peaceful staple with its distinctive black triangle. Keeps best in groups of eight or more in soft to neutral water. Reliable and undemanding in planted community tanks.

ecology.comments: Prefers fast-flowing streams and rivers with vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Fast-flowing rivers and streams with rocky bottom substrates and bankside vegetation in Central America.
ecology.socialBehavior: Avoid keeping multiple males in small tanks as they establish strict hierarchies and fight.

diet.fooditems: worms, insects, plant matter
diet.feedingPlaybook: Eats flake food, veggie wafers, and frozen brine shrimp. Appreciates regular vegetable matter supplements.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense floating plant coverage or breeding box to protect newborn fry.
reproduction.comments: Internal fertilization occurs. Females give birth to large batches of live young. Very prolific breeders in well-planted tanks.

vibeLine.casual: The livebearer with a built-in sword tail.
vibeLine.pro: Xiphophorus hellerii. Active hard-water livebearer; males spar and may jump.

flavorText.casual: Named for the long sword trailing off the male's tail. They're active, hardy, and breed readily in harder water. Keep one male with several females and use a lid, as they're known jumpers.
flavorText.pro: Xiphophorus hellerii is a hardy, active livebearer for harder water. Breeds readily; keep one male to two or three females to spread harassment and protect fry with dense planting. Multiple males spar, and they are strong jumpers, so keep the tank covered.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (mayflies, unidentified, midges)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The cheerful, no-fuss splash of color for beginners.
vibeLine.pro: Xiphophorus maculatus. Hardy, peaceful livebearer in endless color strains.

flavorText.casual: One of the friendliest fish you can buy, in just about every color imaginable. Peaceful, hardy, and happy to breed, they're a classic first livebearer. Harder water keeps them at their best.
flavorText.pro: Xiphophorus maculatus is a forgiving, peaceful livebearer available in countless line-bred colors. Prefers harder, alkaline water. Breeds readily, so expect fry in any mixed-sex group.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The hardy platy that shrugs off cooler water.
vibeLine.pro: Xiphophorus variatus. Cold-tolerant, hardy platy with broad temperature range.

flavorText.casual: A close cousin of the southern platy, but even tougher and fine in cooler tanks. Just as colorful and just as easy. A great choice if your room runs cold and you'd rather skip a heater.
flavorText.pro: Xiphophorus variatus is a hardy platy tolerant of cooler temperatures, down toward 18°C. Equally colorful and prolific as its cousin. Suits unheated or temperate community setups.

ecology.comments: The Spotted Raphael Catfish is a nocturnal and shy species that thrives in a well-structured aquarium with plenty of hiding spots. They are generally peaceful and make excellent additions to community tanks with other similarly sized, non-aggressive fish. Provide caves, driftwood, and dense planting to make them feel secure.
ecology.biotope: Native to slow-moving waters, tributaries, and flooded forests of the Amazon basin in South America. They are typically found amongst submerged roots, leaf litter, and dense vegetation, where they can hide during the day.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful and can be kept singly or in groups. They are more outgoing and confident when kept in a small group of their own kind (3+ individuals). They pose no threat to other fish unless they are small enough to be considered food.

diet.fooditems: Sinking pellets, high-quality flakes, bloodworms, brine shrimp, tubifex worms, and small insect larvae. They appreciate a varied diet.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Being nocturnal, they are best fed after the aquarium lights have been turned off. Ensure sinking foods reach the bottom where they can forage. They have a good sense of smell and will locate food readily.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg layer, likely a substrate or plant scatterer. Specific details on captive breeding are scarce, suggesting it is challenging.
reproduction.layoutRequirement: For potential breeding, a heavily planted tank with plenty of caves, driftwood, and broad-leafed plants would be beneficial. A separate breeding tank with very soft, acidic water might be required.
reproduction.comments: Breeding Agamyxis pectinifrons in a home aquarium is rarely reported and considered difficult. Success often requires very specific water parameters, conditioning with live foods, and a mature, well-established tank environment. Eggs are typically scattered and receive no parental care.

vibeLine.casual: Armoured nocturnal catfish that croaks when you catch it — long-lived.
vibeLine.pro: Agamyxis pectinifrons. Doradid catfish; armoured, stridulating, nocturnal, potentially 15+ year lifespan.

flavorText.casual: A tank of armour plating that hides all day and comes out at night. Makes audible croaking sounds when caught or stressed — the spines lock and vibrate. Extremely long-lived (15+ years) and hardy once settled, but you won't see it much during daylight.
flavorText.pro: Agamyxis pectinifrons is a doradid catfish reaching 15 cm with heavily armoured lateral scutes and locking pectoral spines capable of stridulation. Strictly nocturnal; provide caves and driftwood. Long-lived (15+ years). Omnivorous; feed sinking foods after dark. Egg-scatterer; rarely bred. Pectoral spines easily tangle in nets — use containers for capture.

diet.fooditems: nekton (small fishes); plants (periphyton); zoobenthos (snails, unidentified worms, insects)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The golden bruiser that grows a massive forehead hump.
vibeLine.pro: Amphilophus citrinellus. Large, aggressive Central American cichlid; big-tank only.

flavorText.casual: Some turn solid gold, others stay barred, but all develop a commanding presence and a growing nuchal hump. They're powerful, territorial, and need a very large tank. A serious fish for serious keepers.
flavorText.pro: Amphilophus citrinellus is a large, aggressive Central American cichlid reaching over a foot. Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump. A substrate spawner with fierce parental care. Needs a very large tank; often kept as a wet pet.

diet.fooditems: nekton (small fishes); zoobenthos (snails, unidentified worms, insects larvae)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The fiery red tank boss that lives up to its name.
vibeLine.pro: Amphilophus labiatus. Large, highly aggressive cichlid; often best kept alone.

flavorText.casual: Bright red (or orange, or white) and absolutely fearless, they'll attack anything that enters their space, including your hand. A true wet pet for keepers who want one big, bold fish with personality to spare.
flavorText.pro: Amphilophus labiatus is one of the most aggressive cichlids in the hobby, often best kept alone or as a bonded pair. A substrate spawner with intense parental defense. Needs a very large tank and heavy filtration.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The mellow cichlid that's actually community-tank friendly.
vibeLine.pro: Andinoacara pulcher. Hardy, moderately peaceful cichlid; breeds readily in pairs.

flavorText.casual: One of the calmer South American cichlids, with a gentle blue shimmer and real personality. They can hold their own without terrorizing everyone else. A good first cichlid for a mid-size community.
flavorText.pro: Andinoacara pulcher is a hardy, moderately peaceful cichlid that pairs off and spawns on flat surfaces with devoted parental care. Tolerant of a broad parameter range; one of the safer cichlid choices for mixed communities.

diet.fooditems: others (unspecified insects); zoobenthos (unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The iridescent green bruiser that earns its name.
vibeLine.pro: Andinoacara rivulatus. Large, aggressive South American cichlid; needs space.

flavorText.casual: Covered in electric green-blue spangles, they're stunning but live up to the "terror" name when mature. They need a big tank, robust tankmates, and the understanding that they'll rearrange everything.
flavorText.pro: Andinoacara rivulatus is a large, territorial South American cichlid that becomes increasingly aggressive with age. A substrate spawner with devoted parental care. Needs a large tank and similarly robust company.

diet.fooditems: Surface-feeding predator in nature taking insects, small fish, and crustaceans. In aquaria: floating pellets, live/frozen foods (mosquito larvae, daphnia, bloodworm), occasional flakes.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Surface predator in gold clothing — eats small fish readily.
vibeLine.pro: Aplocheilus lineatus. Surface-hunting aplocheilid; piscivorous toward smaller tankmates, gold morph common.

flavorText.casual: That golden colour makes it look harmless, but this is a mouth optimised for grabbing things from the surface — including small fish. Anything that fits in its mouth is food. Fine with similarly-sized or larger tankmates, but neons and small tetras will disappear. Easy to breed; non-annual.
flavorText.pro: Aplocheilus lineatus is a surface-dwelling aplocheilid reaching 8–10 cm. Predatory toward smaller fish; suitable only with tankmates too large to ingest. The gold morph (xanthic form) dominates trade. Non-annual; egg-depositor on floating plants or surface mops. Soft to neutral water (pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C). Tight-fitting cover essential — strong jumper. Hardy and prolific once housed appropriately.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified); others (terrestrial insects); plants (fruits, flowers and seeds, lemna spp., echinochloa polystachya); zoobenthos (crayfish, unidentified, insects larvae)

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide a large flat rock or smooth slate; pairs clean a flat horizontal surface for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Devoted parents; both guard the eggs and fry vigorously and may move them to pits.

vibeLine.casual: The big wet puppy that rearranges its tank.
vibeLine.pro: Astronotus ocellatus. Large, intelligent cichlid; heavy bioload and big footprint.

flavorText.casual: Smart, messy, and full of personality, they learn to recognize you and beg for food like a dog. They also redecorate constantly and grow huge. A real commitment, but endlessly entertaining.
flavorText.pro: Astronotus ocellatus is an intelligent cichlid that reaches a foot long and produces a heavy bioload, demanding a large tank and serious filtration. Pairs are devoted substrate spawners. Will swallow any tankmate that fits in its mouth.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Maternal mouthbrooder — female incubates eggs and fry in mouth for 3-4 weeks
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The shimmering peacock cichlid that's calmer than most.
vibeLine.pro: Aulonocara nyassae. Peaceful Malawi peacock; sand-sifter, less aggressive than mbuna.

flavorText.casual: One of the calmer Malawi cichlids, with males that shimmer in blues and yellows. They sift sand for food and don't brawl as much as mbuna. Hard, alkaline water and a sandy bottom suit them perfectly.
flavorText.pro: Aulonocara nyassae is a relatively peaceful haplochromine that sifts sand for invertebrates. Less aggressive than mbuna, but still needs hard, alkaline water and a species-appropriate setup. A maternal mouthbrooder; keep one male to several females.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified, unidentified insects larvae); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The silver "shark" that's really a gentle giant.
vibeLine.pro: Balantiocheilos melanopterus. Large, skittish schooling cyprinid; needs serious swimming space.

flavorText.casual: They look like sleek little sharks but they're peaceful, jumpy cyprinids that grow surprisingly large. They need a big tank, a group, and a tight lid. Stunning in a spacious setup, cramped in a small one.
flavorText.pro: Balantiocheilos melanopterus is a peaceful but skittish schooling cyprinid that reaches roughly a foot and needs a long, large tank with a group. Strong jumpers, so cover the tank. Not a true shark despite the profile.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified detritus, unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Bulletproof gold barb that handles cooler temperatures gracefully.
vibeLine.pro: Barbodes semifasciolatus. Extremely hardy gold barb; tolerates subtropical temperatures down to 18°C.

flavorText.casual: Bright gold, tough as nails, and fine in cooler water. One of the hardiest barbs you can buy. They school actively and aren't particularly nippy. A reliable workhorse for almost any community setup.
flavorText.pro: Barbodes semifasciolatus is among the hardiest barbs in the hobby, tolerating 18–24°C and a wide pH range. The gold form is a selectively bred colour morph. Egg-scattering spawner; prolific. Keep in groups of six-plus. Minimal fin-nipping tendency compared to Puntigrus tetrazona.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified); nekton (unidentified); plants (filamentous algae, unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The shiny giant barb that schools and grows fast.
vibeLine.pro: Barbonymus schwanenfeldii. Large active schooling barb; needs a sizable tank.

flavorText.casual: Big, fast, and gleaming silver, they school energetically and grow far larger than the barbs you find in nano tanks. They need real swimming room and a group. Impressive in a large setup, cramped anywhere small.
flavorText.pro: Barbonymus schwanenfeldii is a large, active schooling barb that reaches over a foot and demands a big tank with strong filtration. Keep five or more to reduce skittishness. May nibble soft plants; rarely bred at home.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore feeding on insects, small invertebrates, and plant matter in the wild. In aquaria: quality flake, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, and small live foods.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The only true rainbowfish from Madagascar — unique lineage.
vibeLine.pro: Bedotia madagascariensis. Malagasy bedotiid; isolated lineage separate from Australasian melanotaeniids.

flavorText.casual: Not closely related to the Australian and New Guinean rainbows despite looking similar — Madagascar's only contribution to the rainbowfish world. Active schooler with a dark-edged tail. Prefers slightly acidic to neutral water and appreciates gentle current. Hardy once settled.
flavorText.pro: Bedotia madagascariensis represents an isolated bedotiid lineage endemic to Madagascar, phylogenetically distinct from Australasian melanotaeniids. Reaches 10–12 cm. Prefers slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.5–7.5, 22–25°C) with moderate flow. Egg-scatterer; breeds among fine vegetation. Conservation concern given Malagasy freshwater habitat degradation. Active schooler; keep in groups.

diet.fooditems: While Botia spp. appear to be chiefly carnivorous they will also eat vegetative matter if available, often including soft-leaved aquatic plants. The natural diet comprises aquatic molluscs, insects, worms, and other invertebrates. They’re largely unfussy feeders but must be offered a varied diet com

reproduction.spawningTrait: No reports of breeding in private aquaria but some species are mass-produced for the hobby via the use of hormones. Unfortunately this practice has be
reproduction.comments: No reports of breeding in private aquaria but some species are mass-produced for the hobby via the use of hormones. Unfortunately this practice has been taken to a different level in recent years with a number of hybrids appearing on the market, even including an apparent cross between a form of B. histrionica and Chromobotia macracanthus .

vibeLine.casual: The banded loach that's bold and social during daytime.
vibeLine.pro: Botia almorhae. Active, diurnal loach; social hierarchy needs a group.

flavorText.casual: Striped and surprisingly bold for a loach, often out and about during the day. They're social and need a group to show their full range of behavior. Excellent snail control and a fun bottom-layer addition.
flavorText.pro: Botia almorhae is a relatively active, diurnal loach that establishes social hierarchies in groups. Effective snail predator. Keep five or more with smooth substrate and retreats. Hardy and adaptable; rarely bred in home aquaria.

diet.fooditems: While Botia spp. appear to be chiefly carnivorous they will also eat vegetative matter if available, often including soft-leaved aquatic plants. The natural diet comprises aquatic molluscs, insects, worms, and other invertebrates. They’re largely unfussy feeders but must be offered a varied diet com

reproduction.spawningTrait: Not known to have been bred in the hobby as with the majority of botiid loaches though some, including B. striata , are being produced on a commercial
reproduction.comments: Not known to have been bred in the hobby as with the majority of botiid loaches though some, including B. striata , are being produced on a commercial basis via the use of hormones. Unfortunately this practice has been taken to a different level in recent years with a number of hybrids appearing on the market, even including an apparent cross between a form of B. histrionica and Chromobotia macracanthus .

vibeLine.casual: The patterned loach whose markings spell out Y-O-Y-O.
vibeLine.pro: Botia striata. Social, active loach; snail control expert needing a group.

flavorText.casual: Named for the Y-shaped markings that almost read "YOYO," they're social, active, and excellent snail hunters. Keep a group and they'll entertain you with constant exploration. More active than most loaches.
flavorText.pro: Botia striata is a social, active loach that effectively controls snail populations. Keep five or more to see natural hierarchy and play behavior. Needs hiding spots and sand or smooth substrate. Rarely bred at home.

diet.fooditems: Primarily aufwuchs grazer — feeds on algae, biofilm, and microorganisms on rocks. In aquaria: algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber), sinking spirulina tablets.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Cave spawner with paternal care — male guards eggs in a crevice or cave
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Effective algae eater that stays smaller and needs cooler, flowing water.
vibeLine.pro: Chaetostoma milesi. Hillstream-adapted loricariid; cooler oxygenated water, stays under 15 cm.

flavorText.casual: One of the better algae-eating plecos for medium tanks — stays under 15 cm and actually keeps grazing into adulthood. Needs cooler water (20–25°C) and good oxygenation. Won't thrive in warm, still setups. A solid alternative to plecos that outgrow everything.
flavorText.pro: Chaetostoma milesi is a rheophilic loricariid requiring cool (20–25°C), well-oxygenated water with moderate to strong flow. Effective algae grazer throughout its life, unlike many larger loricariids. Stays under 15 cm. Egg-depositor in caves; male guards clutch. Hardy when flow and oxygen requirements are met. Not suited to warm, low-flow community tanks.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The playful orange clown that needs lots of friends.
vibeLine.pro: Chromobotia macracanthus. Large, social loach; grows far beyond most expectations.

flavorText.casual: Striped orange goofballs that play, hide, and even lie on their sides like they've fainted. They're social and need a group to thrive. Just know they get big and live for decades, so plan ahead.
flavorText.pro: Chromobotia macracanthus is a social loach that needs groups of five-plus and eventually a large tank, reaching well over a foot in time. Rarely bred in home aquaria. Sensitive to ich and poor water; acclimate carefully.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified small crustaceans, unidentified, unidentified insect larvae)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The shy, peaceful cichlid that just wants to hide.
vibeLine.pro: Cleithracara maronii. Gentle, timid cichlid; one of the most peaceful.

flavorText.casual: Possibly the gentlest cichlid in the hobby. They'd rather hide than fight, and they stress easily around boisterous tankmates. Give them calm company and plenty of cover and they'll reward you with quiet charm.
flavorText.pro: Cleithracara maronii is one of the least aggressive cichlids. Timid and easily stressed, it needs peaceful company, dim lighting, and plenty of refuge. A bonded pair will spawn on flat surfaces and guard fry gently.

diet.fooditems: zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Malawi hap — red-and-blue males, peaceful for a rift-lake cichlid.
vibeLine.pro: Copadichromis borleyi. Malawi haplochromine; relatively peaceful, maternal mouthbrooder, multiple colour variants.

flavorText.casual: One of the more approachable Malawi cichlids — males develop stunning red-and-blue coloration but don't carry the extreme aggression of mbuna. Needs hard, alkaline water and a tank large enough for a group. Females are plainer but handle mouthbrooding duties.
flavorText.pro: Copadichromis borleyi is a Malawi haplochromine reaching 15–18 cm, with males exhibiting vivid red or orange body coloration with metallic blue head. Relatively peaceful for a Malawi cichlid; compatible with other non-aggressive haps and some mbuna. Maternal mouthbrooder; females incubate 20–50 eggs orally. Multiple locality colour variants — maintain population purity. Hard alkaline water (pH 7.8–8.6).

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The intricately speckled cory that's often mistaken for another.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras julii. True julii is rare; most sold are trilineatus.

flavorText.casual: A delicate maze of dark spots and lines makes this one a stunner. Fun fact: most "julii" in stores are actually the similar three-stripe cory. Either way, they're peaceful, hardy, and happiest in a group.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras julii has fine reticulated spotting and is genuinely uncommon in the trade; most labeled specimens are Corydoras trilineatus. Care is identical either way: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner.

diet.fooditems: Omnivorous and easy to feed. Use a good quality sinking pellet or tablet as the staple diet. Supplement this with live and frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia , bloodworm and similar.

reproduction.comments: Can be bred in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras species . Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bottom, sand or fine gravel substrate . Use air-powered sponge or box- type filtration as fry won’t be sucked into these and provide some clumps of vegetation such as java moss. A temperature of around 75°F and a pH of 6.5 should be fine. Filtering the water through peat is useful, as is the use of RO water. It’s always better to have a highe

vibeLine.casual: The little pandas with the black eye patches.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras panda. Peaceful bottom dweller that prefers cooler, well-oxygenated water.

flavorText.casual: Adorable little armored catfish that love to hang out in groups on the bottom. Very peaceful and fun to watch.
flavorText.pro: Best kept in groups of 5+. They appreciate cooler temperatures and sandy substrate. Sensitive to poor water quality and strong medications.

diet.fooditems: Omnivorous and easy to feed. Use a good quality sinking pellet or tablet as the staple diet. Supplement this with live and frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia , bloodworm and similar.

reproduction.comments: Generally considered one of the easiest Corydoras tp spawn and a good choice for the beginner. Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bottom, sand or fine gravel substrate . Use air-powered sponge or box- type filtration as fry won’t be sucked into these and provide some clumps of vegetation such as java moss. A temperature of around 75°F and a pH of 6.5 should be fine. Filtering the water through peat is useful, as is the use of RO water. It’s a

vibeLine.casual: The spotty armored cutie that loves a warm tank.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras sterbai. Hardy, warmth-tolerant cory; ideal discus-tank bottom companion.

flavorText.casual: Covered in cream spots with cheerful orange fins, they're one of the prettiest corys around. They handle warmer water better than most, so they pair perfectly with discus. Keep a group over soft sand.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras sterbai is a hardy cory that tolerates the higher temperatures discus need, making it a favored bottom companion. Keep six or more over smooth sand to protect barbels. An egg-depositing spawner triggered by cool water changes.

diet.fooditems: Omnivorous and easy to feed. Use a good quality sinking pellet or tablet as the staple diet. Supplement this with live and frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia , bloodworm and similar.

reproduction.comments: Generally considered one of the easiest Corydoras to spawn and a good choice for the beginner. Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bottom, sand or fine gravel substrate . Use air-powered sponge or box- type filtration as fry won’t be sucked into these and provide some clumps of vegetation such as java moss. A temperature of around 75°F and a pH of 6.5 should be fine. Filtering the water through peat is useful, as is the use of RO water. It’s a

vibeLine.casual: The "false julii" that's a charmer in its own right.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras trilineatus. Hardy, common cory; frequently sold as Corydoras julii.

flavorText.casual: Often sold under the wrong name, but a lovely, easygoing cory regardless. Patterned with bold spots and a dark lateral line, they potter around the bottom in groups. Hardy and great for beginners.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras trilineatus is the hardy, widely-available species usually sold as julii. Distinguished by a connected lateral line and bolder markings. Keep six or more over sand; an egg-depositing spawner like its relatives.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified); nekton (unidentified small fish); plants (unidentified filamentous algae, unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The slow, regal giant of Lake Tanganyika.
vibeLine.pro: Cyphotilapia frontosa. Large, slow-growing Tanganyikan; needs volume and patience.

flavorText.casual: Big, bold black-and-white stripes and a growing forehead hump give them a commanding presence. They're surprisingly calm for their size but need a very large tank and hard, alkaline water. A long-term commitment.
flavorText.pro: Cyphotilapia frontosa is a large, slow-growing Tanganyikan cichlid requiring a big tank, hard alkaline water, and a group. Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump. A maternal mouthbrooder; fry are held for weeks.

diet.fooditems: detritus (detritus); others (ants); zoobenthos (unidentified exogenous insects, unidentified water mites, nematodes); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Classic iridescent danio with a pink-gold pearl sheen all over.
vibeLine.pro: Danio albolineatus. Hardy iridescent danio; pearl sheen, cool-tolerant, prolific spawner.

flavorText.casual: An old-school danio with a subtle pearl iridescence that shifts pink-gold under light. Not flashy in a shop tank, but under good lighting at home it's a quiet stunner. Hardy, active, and easy to keep in a school.
flavorText.pro: Danio albolineatus is a robust danio reaching 5–6 cm, tolerating subtropical to tropical temperatures. Iridescent pink-gold sheen best displayed under side-lighting. Egg-scattering spawner; extremely prolific. Keep in schools of six-plus with ample swimming room.

diet.fooditems: plants (plant materials); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: A full-speed torpedo danio that needs a long tank.
vibeLine.pro: Devario aequipinnatus. Large, fast danio; requires spacious tanks with strong swimming current.

flavorText.casual: Think zebra danio energy scaled up to 10–12 cm. They're constantly on the move and will outpace anything slow in the tank. Hardy and easy but need a long aquarium — short tanks make them pace restlessly. Schoolers.
flavorText.pro: Devario aequipinnatus reaches 10–12 cm and demands spacious aquaria (120 cm+ recommended) with good flow. Extremely hardy; tolerates subtropical to tropical temperatures. Egg-scattering spawner; prolific and easy to breed. Keep in schools of six-plus. May intimidate slow-moving tankmates with its speed.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The jet-black "shark" with one brilliant red tail.
vibeLine.pro: Epalzeorhynchos bicolor. Bold, territorial cyprinid; striking but scrappy with others.

flavorText.casual: Velvet-black with a single vivid red tail, it's a real eye-catcher with a feisty streak. It guards its turf and chases intruders. Keep just one, with tankmates fast enough to stay out of trouble.
flavorText.pro: Epalzeorhynchos bicolor is a bold, territorial cyprinid, best kept one per tank with plenty of cover to break sightlines. Aggressive toward similar bottom species. A near-threatened species in the wild; not a true shark.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified); zooplankton (unidentified)
