reproduction.spawningTrait: As far as we know it’s not been bred in private aquaria but large numbers are farmed for the ornamental trade with the aid of hormones.
reproduction.comments: As far as we know it’s not been bred in private aquaria but large numbers are farmed for the ornamental trade with the aid of hormones.

vibeLine.casual: The black-bodied "shark" with bright red fins.
vibeLine.pro: Epalzeorhynchos frenatum. Territorial bottom cyprinid; aggressive toward its own kind.

flavorText.casual: A striking dark fish with fiery red fins and a bold attitude to match. They patrol the bottom and don't tolerate others of their kind. Best kept singly with fast tankmates that can shrug off the bossiness.
flavorText.pro: Epalzeorhynchos frenatum is a territorial bottom-dwelling cyprinid, aggressive to conspecifics and similar species. Keep one per tank with hiding spots and quick, mid-water company. Not a shark; the name is purely cosmetic.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Often sold as an algae helper but gets territorial once it settles.
vibeLine.pro: Epalzeorhynchos kalopterum. Algae-grazing cyprinid; increasing territoriality with maturity, often mislabelled as SAE.

flavorText.casual: Looks a lot like a Siamese algae eater and gets sold as one constantly. Young ones graze algae willingly, but adults become territorial and less interested in cleanup duty. One per tank in most setups — they won't tolerate lookalikes.
flavorText.pro: Epalzeorhynchos kalopterum is frequently confused with Crossocheilus oblongus (SAE) in trade. Juveniles graze algae effectively; adults become increasingly territorial toward conspecifics and similar-shaped fish. Reaches 12–15 cm. Best kept singly in planted tanks with defined territories. Egg-scatterer; not bred commercially in home aquaria.

diet.fooditems: Carnivorous surface feeder taking small insects and invertebrates in nature. In aquaria: live/frozen mosquito larvae, daphnia, brine shrimp, small floating pellets.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Classic beginner killifish — jewel-like colours, straightforward care.
vibeLine.pro: Fundulopanchax gardneri. Non-annual West African killi; accessible entry point to killifish keeping.

flavorText.casual: The gateway killifish for most people — colourful enough to justify the mild extra effort over standard community fish. Males are red-and-blue spotted gems. Not annual, so lifespan is normal. Breeds readily in spawning mops or fine-leaved plants. A covered tank is non-negotiable.
flavorText.pro: Fundulopanchax gardneri is a non-annual West African aplocheiloid and the standard entry species for killifish keeping. Males exhibit red and blue spotting on a golden-green base. Egg-depositor in mops or vegetation; eggs hatch in 14–21 days without diapause. Multiple geographic populations — maintain locality purity. Soft to neutral water (pH 6–7.5, 22–26°C). Tight cover essential.

diet.fooditems: Eartheater — sifts sand through gills to extract invertebrates, organic detritus, and plant matter. In aquaria: sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp. Fine sand substrate essential for feeding behavior.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Big eartheater that constantly redecorates your tank by sifting sand.
vibeLine.pro: Geophagus surinamensis. Large eartheater; obligate substrate-sifter requiring fine sand, group-oriented.

flavorText.casual: Watching a group of these methodically sift mouthfuls of sand is genuinely relaxing — but they'll rearrange every plant you put in. Large species that needs space, a sand substrate, and companions of similar size. Keep in groups for natural social behaviour.
flavorText.pro: Geophagus surinamensis reaches 25+ cm and is an obligate substrate-sifter — fine sand is non-negotiable. Biparental substrate spawner. Social species best maintained in groups of five or more in spacious tanks (400L+). pH 6–7, 24–30°C. Incompatible with planted aquascapes due to constant substrate disturbance. Tankmates should be similarly robust. Multiple geographic colour forms exist.

diet.fooditems: Easy to feed. It will readily accept just about anything offered. For the best condition and colours, offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia and brine shrimp , along with dried flakes and granules.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Quite easily bred, although you’ll need to set up a separate tank in which to do so if you want to raise any numbers of fry . Something around 18″ x 12″ x 12″ in size is fine. This should be dimly lit and contain clumps of fine-leaved plants such as java moss or spawning mops, to give the fish somewhere to deposit their adhesive eggs. You could also cover the base of the tank with some kind of mesh. This should be of a large enough grade so that the eggs can fall through it, but small enough so

vibeLine.casual: The copper-gold tetra with bright silver-tipped fins.
vibeLine.pro: Hasemania nana. Hardy, active tetra; slightly nippy, needs a group.

flavorText.casual: A warm coppery body with bright silver tips on the fins, they're lively and eye-catching. Keep a good group or they'll pester slower tankmates. Hardy and easy, they add energy to a planted community.
flavorText.pro: Hasemania nana is a hardy, active tetra with a slightly nippy streak when understocked. Keep eight or more to contain fin-nipping. Tolerant of a broad parameter range; an egg-scatterer in fine-leaved plants.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The big pink one famous for "kissing" contests.
vibeLine.pro: Helostoma temminckii. Large gourami; "kissing" is territorial sparring, not affection.

flavorText.casual: Famous for the lip-locking "kisses," which are actually little turf battles, not romance. They get surprisingly large and graze constantly. Give them room and they're a hardy, characterful centerpiece.
flavorText.pro: Helostoma temminckii grows large and uses its mouth to spar over territory rather than show affection. A constant grazer that needs volume and filtration. Unlike most gouramis, it scatters eggs at the surface and builds no nest.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The sparkly North American native with a big personality.
vibeLine.pro: Herichthys cyanoguttatus. Large, aggressive native cichlid; cold-tolerant, territorial.

flavorText.casual: The only cichlid native to the United States, covered in turquoise spangles. They're tough, cold-tolerant, and very territorial. A big tank and thick-skinned tankmates are non-negotiable.
flavorText.pro: Herichthys cyanoguttatus is a large, aggressive cichlid native to Texas and northern Mexico. Cold-tolerant down to roughly 15°C. A substrate spawner with fierce parental defense. Needs a large tank and robust company.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified parts of fish); others (terrestrial insects); plants (unidentified, unspecified aquatic plants)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The big, gentle disc that's almost friendly enough for communities.
vibeLine.pro: Heros efasciatus. Peaceful large cichlid; herbivorous tendencies, bonded-pair breeder.

flavorText.casual: Round, green-gold, and surprisingly chill for a large cichlid. They'll nibble plants and rearrange a bit, but they're far less aggressive than most fish their size. A good big-tank centerpiece with robust company.
flavorText.pro: Heros efasciatus is a large, relatively peaceful cichlid with herbivorous tendencies; will damage soft plants. Bonded pairs are devoted substrate spawners. Needs a large tank; tolerant of a broad parameter range.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified detritus); nekton (fish scales); others (terrestrial insecta); plants (fruits of unspecified plants, seeds of unspecified plants, unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The mellow disc-shaped cichlid that's almost community-safe.
vibeLine.pro: Heros severus. Relatively peaceful large cichlid; pairs bond and guard fry.

flavorText.casual: Round, colorful, and surprisingly calm for a big cichlid. They can work in a large community with robust tankmates, though they get protective when breeding. A good "first big cichlid" for keepers ready to size up.
flavorText.pro: Heros severus is one of the more peaceful large South American cichlids. Bonded pairs are substrate spawners with devoted parental care. Needs a large tank; may nip plants. Tolerant of a range of parameters.

diet.fooditems: Probably a foraging omnivore feeding primarily on worms, insects and other zooplankton, as well as smaller amounts of plant material and organic detritus in nature. In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm

reproduction.comments: An egg -scattering free spawner exhibiting no parental care . When in good condition adults will spawn often and in a mature aquarium it’s possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to maximise yield a more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but a smaller aquarium should also be set up and filled with mature water. This should be very dimly lit and the base covered with some kind of mesh of a la

vibeLine.casual: The chunky silver tetra with a flash of red.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon columbianus. Robust, slightly assertive tetra; needs a group.

flavorText.casual: Bigger and bolder than most tetras, with a silvery body and red-tipped fins. They've got a bit of attitude, so keep a proper group and avoid pairing them with timid, tiny fish. Hardy and easy otherwise.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon columbianus is a robust tetra that can be mildly assertive toward smaller species. Keep eight or more to contain fin-nipping. Hardy and adaptable; an egg-scatterer in planted tanks.

ecology.comments: Found in slow-flowing rivers and ponds with rich vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Slow-flowing tributaries, oxbows, and swampy areas in the Amazon and Paraguay river basins.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a school of 6+ to minimize fin-nipping behavior directed at slower tankmates.

diet.fooditems: crustaceans, insects
diet.feedingPlaybook: Accepts high-quality flake foods and small pellets. Supplement with frozen bloodworms and brine shrimp to maintain color vibrancy.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Java moss beds, spawning grids, or fine plants. Eggs adhere to the plant leaves.
reproduction.comments: Spawns in pairs or small groups. Parents are notorious egg-eaters and must be separated after the spawn event completes.

vibeLine.casual: The fiery red scrapper with a taste for fins.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon eques. Striking but nippy; needs large groups to settle.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous deep-red color, but they've got a feisty streak. A big group keeps their fin-nipping focused on each other rather than your other fish. Avoid slow, long-finned tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon eques is a beautiful but notorious nipper. Groups of eight or more disperse aggression internally. Best paired with fast, robust species; never with bettas or angelfish.

diet.fooditems: This species is an opportunistic omnivore by nature. The stomach contents of wild specimens from Lake Ayapuá were composed of fruit remains, aquatic insects and larvae (Trichoptera, Diptera) with the former constituting 98.5 % and latter 1 % of all items consumed. Stomachs of individuals from igarap

reproduction.comments: Egg scatterer exhibiting no parental care.

vibeLine.casual: The deep-bodied tetra with a red heart-shaped spot.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon erythrostigma. Larger tetra; needs space and soft water.

flavorText.casual: Bigger and deeper-bodied than most tetras, with a vivid red blotch on the flank that looks like a heart. They're peaceful but need room and a group to feel confident. Soft water brings out the best color.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon erythrostigma is a larger tetra needing more swimming space than typical nano species. Males develop extended dorsal fins. Best in soft, acidic water in groups of six-plus. An egg-scatterer; rarely bred at home.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified small crustaceans, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified small crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The neon's moody cousin with a sleek silver-and-black stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon herbertaxelrodi. Hardy, peaceful tetra; tougher than the true neon.

flavorText.casual: Not actually a neon, but just as easy and arguably tougher, with a crisp white-and-black racing stripe. Peaceful and hardy, they shoal beautifully against plants. A reliable, understated community staple.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon herbertaxelrodi is hardier and more forgiving than the true neon, with a bold black-and-silver lateral stripe. Keep eight or more in a planted tank. An egg-scatterer; stable soft water brings out the best contrast.

diet.fooditems: Likely to be omnivorous feeding on small invertebrates, crustacea, filamentous algae, fallen fruit and suchlike in nature. In aquaria it may survive on a diet of dried foods but like most fishes does best when offered a varied menu which in this case should also contain live and frozen chironomid la

The adult group can still be conditioned together but a smaller aquarium should also be set up and filled with mature water. This should be very dimly lit and the base covered with some kind of mesh of a large

vibeLine.casual: The smoky little tetra whose males stage mock duels.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon megalopterus. Peaceful tetra; males harmlessly spar to display.

flavorText.casual: Dusky and elegant, with a dark shoulder spot and males that face off in dramatic but harmless little fin-flaring duels. Peaceful overall and easy to keep, they add quiet character to a planted group.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon megalopterus is a peaceful tetra whose males perform harmless lateral display duels to establish rank. Keep a group with both sexes to see the behavior. An egg-scatterer; hardy and undemanding in soft, planted tanks.

diet.fooditems: plants (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care

vibeLine.casual: The pale charmer with lemon-yellow accents and red eyes.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon pulchripinnis. Hardy, peaceful tetra; color rewards a planted tank.

flavorText.casual: Subtle but lovely, with yellow-edged fins and bright red eyes that pop against greenery. Peaceful and hardy, they color up best in a planted tank with a good group. An easygoing, underrated choice.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon pulchripinnis is a hardy, peaceful tetra whose lemon and black fin accents intensify over dark substrate in planted tanks. Keep eight or more for confident shoaling. An egg-scatterer in fine-leaved plants.

diet.fooditems: Likely to be omnivorous feeding on small invertebrates, crustacea, filamentous algae, fallen fruit and suchlike in nature. In aquaria it may survive on a diet of dried foods but like most fishes does best when offered a varied menu which in this case should also contain live and frozen chironomid la

reproduction.comments: An egg -scattering free spawner exhibiting no parental care . When in good condition adults will spawn often and in a mature aquarium it is possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention, but if you want to maximise yield a more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but a smaller aquarium should also be set up and filled with mature water. This should be very dimly lit and the base covered with some kind of mesh of a large

vibeLine.casual: The warm red cousin of the black phantom tetra.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon sweglesi. Peaceful red tetra; males display with spread fins.

flavorText.casual: A soft, rosy-red tetra with a dark shoulder spot and males that flare their fins at each other in harmless little standoffs. Peaceful and easy, they look best in a planted group with subdued lighting.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon sweglesi is a peaceful tetra whose males perform lateral fin-spread displays. Best color in soft, slightly acidic water over dark substrate. Keep a group with both sexes; an egg-scatterer among fine plants.

diet.fooditems: Probably a predator feeding on small invertebrates and zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should also be offered daily meals of small live and frozen fare such as Artemia nauplii, Moina , grindal worm, etc.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer — rarely bred in captivity. May scatter eggs among plants during rainy season simulation
reproduction.comments: Unrecorded.

vibeLine.casual: The see-through ghost fish you can look right through.
vibeLine.pro: Kryptopterus vitreolus. Transparent, sensitive shoaler; needs groups and stable water.

flavorText.casual: Almost completely transparent, you can watch their spine and organs as they hover in a group. They're shy and easily stressed, so they need calm tankmates, a real school, and stable water. Magical when settled.
flavorText.pro: Kryptopterus vitreolus is a delicate, transparent shoaler that must be kept in groups of six or more or it wastes away. Sensitive to water quality and sudden change; loses transparency under stress. A midwater fish, not a bottom-dweller.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unspecified small finfishes); zoobenthos (unspecified small aquatic animals); zooplankton (unspecified small aquatic animals)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The stunning scrapper that thrives in cool water.
vibeLine.pro: Macropodus opercularis. Hardy but pugnacious labyrinth fish; tolerates cold water.

flavorText.casual: One of the oldest fish in the hobby, and gorgeous, but it has a temper. Tough enough to handle cool, unheated tanks. Beautiful kept alone or with fish too big to bully.
flavorText.pro: Macropodus opercularis is extremely hardy and cold-tolerant, but aggressive toward small or similar-shaped tankmates. Males are especially territorial and build bubblenests. Best as a centerpiece in a coolwater setup with robust company.

diet.fooditems: An easily-fed species , it will accept most dried, frozen and live foods. Feed a varied diet for the fish to show the best colouration. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: As with other related species , this is not a difficult species to breed, being an egg scatterer . That said, the fry can prove somewhat tricky to raise in the early stages. The breeding aquarium should be at least 18″ long, and contain slightly hard, alkaline water with a pH of around 7.5 and a temperature of 72-74°F. A small air-powered filter will provide sufficient oxygenation and flow. The tank should be filled with fine-leaved plants such as java moss, or nylon spawning mops. No substrate

vibeLine.casual: Delicate Sulawesi endemic with sail-like fin extensions that catch the light.
vibeLine.pro: Marosatherina ladigesi. Sulawesi endemic telmatherinid; elongated fin filaments, slightly alkaline water preferred.

flavorText.casual: The elongated rays on the dorsal and anal fins give it a sail-like silhouette that shimmers in current. Endemic to Sulawesi and somewhat delicate — prefers clean, slightly alkaline water with gentle flow. Not a beginner rainbow, but not difficult either.
flavorText.pro: Marosatherina ladigesi is a telmatherinid endemic to the Maros karst region of Sulawesi, characterised by elongated fin ray extensions in males. Requires clean, slightly alkaline water (pH 7–8) with moderate flow. Egg-scatterer among fine-leaved plants; breeds in well-maintained setups. Keep in groups of eight-plus. Conservation concern due to restricted habitat.

diet.fooditems: It will accept most foods offered, but vegetable matter in the form of spirulina flakes, blanched spinach etc. should form a good proportion of the diet. This can be supplemented with live and frozen varieties. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Possible. Maternal mouthbrooder. It should be spawned in a species tank in a harem of one male and at least 3 females. A 48″ aquarium is a good size and should be furnished as suggested above, along with some flat stones and areas of open substrate to act as potential spawning sites. The pH should be around 8.2-8.5 and the temperature 77-80°F. The fish should be conditioned with plenty of live and frozen foods. The male fish will clean and then display around his chosen spawning site, showing in

vibeLine.casual: The vivid blue mbuna that rules its rock pile.
vibeLine.pro: Maylandia callainos. Aggressive mbuna; hard alkaline water and overstocking required.

flavorText.casual: A solid, vivid blue that dominates its territory with confidence. Like all mbuna, it needs hard alkaline water, lots of rocks, and a group big enough to spread the aggression around. Not for timid tanks.
flavorText.pro: Maylandia callainos is a typical aggressive mbuna requiring hard, alkaline water (pH 7.8–8.6) and a rocky aquascape. Overstock to diffuse territorial behavior. A maternal mouthbrooder; keep one male to several females.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified algae)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Maternal mouthbrooder — female holds eggs and fry in mouth. Males highly polygamous
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The bright orange mbuna that's tougher than it looks.
vibeLine.pro: Maylandia estherae. Aggressive mbuna; overstock to spread aggression in hard water.

flavorText.casual: Don't let the cheerful orange fool you — this is a feisty mbuna that needs hard, alkaline water and a rocky tank with plenty of hiding spots. Keep a group and expect some chasing. Colorful chaos.
flavorText.pro: Maylandia estherae is an aggressive mbuna from Lake Malawi requiring hard, alkaline water and a rocky aquascape. Overstock to diffuse aggression; keep one male to multiple females. A maternal mouthbrooder.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The gold-and-black mbuna with a serious attitude problem.
vibeLine.pro: Melanochromis auratus. Extremely aggressive mbuna; dominant males are relentless.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous gold-and-black stripes as juveniles, but dominant males turn dark and become one of the most aggressive fish in the hobby. Only for keepers who know what they're getting into. Hard alkaline water, big group, lots of rock.
flavorText.pro: Melanochromis auratus is among the most aggressive mbuna. Dominant males turn dark and terrorize tankmates relentlessly. Requires hard, alkaline water, heavy overstocking, and extensive rockwork. A maternal mouthbrooder; not for mixed community tanks.

diet.fooditems: Unfussy and will accept most dried, frozen and live foods. Regular feedings of the latter will help to ensure the fish exhibit their best colours. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: As with other members of the genus , this is not a difficult species to breed, being an egg scatterer , although the fry can prove somewhat tricky to raise. The breeding aquarium should be at least 30″ long, and contain slightly hard, alkaline water with a pH of around 7.5 and a temperature of 80-84°F. A small air-powered filter will provide sufficient oxygenation and flow. The tank should be filled with fine-leaved plants such as java moss, or nylon spawning mops. No substrate is necessary. The

vibeLine.casual: The half-blue, half-gold rainbow that glows at dawn.
vibeLine.pro: Melanotaenia boesemani. Stunning dual-tone rainbowfish; needs space and a group.

flavorText.casual: Split down the middle, blue at the front and gold at the back, with colors that intensify in morning light. Active schoolers that need a roomy tank and a group to look their best. Mesmerizing in motion.
flavorText.pro: Melanotaenia boesemani is a larger rainbowfish whose blue-and-gold bicolor intensifies in morning display. Keep six or more in a long tank with moderate current. Males spar to display. An egg-scatterer among fine plants or mops.

diet.fooditems: Like most of its congeners it’s an unfussy, omnivorous species , and will accept most dried, frozen and live foods. Regular feedings of the latter will help to ensure the fish exhibit their best colours. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: As with other members of the genus , this is not a difficult species to breed, being an egg scatterer . The fry , however, can prove somewhat tricky to raise. The breeding aquarium should be at least 30″ long and contain slightly hard, alkaline water with a pH of around 7.5 and a temperature of 72-75°F. A small air-powered filter will provide sufficient oxygenation and flow. The tank should be filled with fine-leaved plants such as java moss, or nylon spawning mops. No substrate is necessary. Th

vibeLine.casual: Gold-and-blue lake endemic — another New Guinea rainbowfish jewel.
vibeLine.pro: Melanotaenia herbertaxelrodi. Lake Tebera endemic; gold-blue bicolour, conservation-relevant captive populations.

flavorText.casual: A gold-and-blue rainbowfish found only in Lake Tebera, Papua New Guinea. Males develop rich coloration over many months. Captive populations matter here — wild habitat is limited and potentially threatened. Hardy once established in neutral to alkaline water.
flavorText.pro: Melanotaenia herbertaxelrodi is restricted to Lake Tebera, Papua New Guinea, displaying gold ventral and blue dorsal coloration in mature males. Captive breeding populations have conservation significance given limited wild range. Neutral to alkaline water (pH 7–8, 22–26°C). Egg-scatterer among fine vegetation. Progressive colour development; full display by 12+ months.

diet.fooditems: Like most of its congeners, it’s an unfussy, omnivorous species and will accept most dried, frozen and live foods. Regular feedings of the latter will help to ensure the fish exhibit their best colours. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: As with other members of the genus , this is not a difficult species to breed, being an egg scatterer . The fry , however, can prove somewhat tricky to raise. The breeding aquarium should be at least 30″ long and contain slightly hard, alkaline water with a pH of around 7.5 and a temperature of 72-75°F. A small air-powered filter will provide sufficient oxygenation and flow. The tank should be filled with fine-leaved plants such as java moss, or nylon spawning mops. No substrate is necessary. Th

vibeLine.casual: Striking turquoise-blue rainbow from a single New Guinea lake.
vibeLine.pro: Melanotaenia lacustris. Lake Kutubu endemic; turquoise-blue coloration, cooler temperatures than typical melanotaeniids.

flavorText.casual: That turquoise-blue colouration is genuinely eye-catching — one of the most colourful rainbowfish available. Endemic to Lake Kutubu in Papua New Guinea, so it prefers slightly cooler, alkaline water. Colours up gradually over months. Active schooler that needs a decent-sized group.
flavorText.pro: Melanotaenia lacustris is endemic to Lake Kutubu, Papua New Guinea, exhibiting distinctive turquoise-blue body coloration. Prefers cooler water (21–25°C) and alkaline conditions (pH 7–8.5) compared to many congeners. Egg-scatterer; breeds readily. Maintain locality purity — this species has conservation relevance as a lake endemic. Males develop full colour by 8–12 months.

diet.fooditems: Like most of its congeners, it’s an unfussy, omnivorous species and will accept most dried, frozen and live foods. Regular feedings of the latter will help to ensure the fish exhibit their best colours. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: As with other members of the genus , this is not a difficult species to breed, being an egg scatterer . The fry can prove somewhat tricky to raise in the early stages. The breeding aquarium should be at least 18″ long, and contain slightly hard, alkaline water with a pH of around 7.0 and a temperature of 75-80°F. A small air-powered filter will provide sufficient oxygenation and flow. The tank should be filled with fine-leaved plants, such as java moss, or nylon spawning mops. No substrate is ne

vibeLine.casual: The compact neon-blue rainbow that flashes red fins.
vibeLine.pro: Melanotaenia praecox. Small active rainbowfish; neon-blue body with red finnage.

flavorText.casual: All the rainbow magic packed into a smaller package. Males shimmer neon blue with striking red fin edges. Active and peaceful in a group, they're a great fit for a mid-size planted tank.
flavorText.pro: Melanotaenia praecox is a smaller, active rainbowfish with intense neon-blue iridescence and red-orange fin margins. Keep six or more for best display and color; males spar harmlessly at dawn. An egg-scatterer among spawning mops.

diet.fooditems: Unfussy and will accept most dried, frozen and live foods. Regular feedings of the latter will ensure the best colouration. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: As with other members of the genus , this is not a difficult species to breed, being an egg scatterer , although the fry can prove somewhat tricky to raise. The breeding aquarium should be at least 30″ long, and contain slightly hard, alkaline water with a pH of around 7.5 and a temperature of 80-84°F. A small air-powered filter will provide sufficient oxygenation and flow. The tank should be filled with fine-leaved plants such as java moss, or nylon spawning mops. No substrate is necessary. The

vibeLine.casual: Colourful Australian rainbow with huge colour variety between wild populations.
vibeLine.pro: Melanotaenia trifasciata. Highly variable Australian melanotaeniid; locality-specific colour forms, active schooler.

flavorText.casual: One of Australia's most variable rainbowfish — wild populations range from red to gold to blue depending on their river of origin. Active, colourful, and easy to keep in groups. Males flash their brightest colours in morning sparring displays with rivals.
flavorText.pro: Melanotaenia trifasciata exhibits extreme locality-based colour polymorphism across its northern Australian range. Active schooler reaching 10–12 cm. Hard, alkaline water tolerated but neutral conditions work well. Egg-scatterer among fine vegetation; breeds readily. Maintain locality purity in breeding programs to preserve geographic colour variants.

diet.fooditems: plants (lemna spp., unspecified plant matter, paspalum spp.); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms); zooplankton (perizoon)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Peaceful medium-sized SA cichlid with a distinctive diagonal stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Mesonauta festivus. Peaceful South American cichlid; moderate size, diagonal band, community-compatible.

flavorText.casual: A mid-sized South American cichlid that's genuinely peaceful — doesn't dig, doesn't terrorise tankmates, and displays a clean diagonal black stripe from mouth to dorsal. Works in planted tanks with other non-aggressive species. One of the more community-friendly cichlids available.
flavorText.pro: Mesonauta festivus is a peaceful South American cichlid reaching 12–15 cm, characterised by a diagonal dark band from mouth to posterior dorsal. Compatible with community tankmates of similar size. Soft to neutral water (pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C). Open-substrate spawner; biparental care. Rarely aggressive outside spawning. Suitable for planted setups — generally does not uproot vegetation.

diet.fooditems: Provide a large amount of vegetable matter in the diet, including; courgette, cucumber, peas, spring greens and other green vegetables. Commercial foods such as algae wafers, spirulina and vegetable flake are also recommended. Silver dollars will also accept the majority of aquarium foods and will r

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Moderately difficult. Silver dollars are group spawners, so a large breeding tank is needed. Condition the fish well prior to introduction to the breeding setup. Water conditions should be at the softer, more acidic end of this species range and the temperature should be raised to around 82°F (28°C). Carrying out a water change with slightly warmer water may help trigger spawning . Males assume a dark colouration when ready to spawn , particularly around the anal, caudal and dorsal fins. The red

vibeLine.casual: Big, round, silver, and will eat all your plants.
vibeLine.pro: Metynnis argenteus. Classic herbivorous disc; schools, eliminates live plants entirely.

flavorText.casual: The original "silver dollar" — flat, round, and relentless on vegetation. They're peaceful toward fish but merciless on plants. A group in a large, plant-free tank with driftwood décor makes a striking display.
flavorText.pro: Metynnis argenteus is a deep-bodied herbivorous characin reaching 15 cm. Incompatible with planted tanks. School of five-plus in spacious aquaria with robust filtration. Egg-scattering spawner; condition with varied vegetable matter. Soft, warm water promotes spawning behavior.

diet.fooditems: sifts substrate for plant material and small organisms

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat stones or broad leaves as spawning sites.
reproduction.comments: Spawns on flat surfaces; females fan the eggs until hatching and both parents care for the fry.

vibeLine.casual: The mellow dwarf cichlid that's easy to please.
vibeLine.pro: Mikrogeophagus altispinosus. Hardy dwarf cichlid; far more forgiving than German rams.

flavorText.casual: All the charm of a dwarf cichlid with far less drama. They're hardier and more tolerant than German blue rams, and they sift sand and shimmy at each other adorably. A great first cichlid.
flavorText.pro: Mikrogeophagus altispinosus is a hardy dwarf cichlid that tolerates a wider parameter range than Mikrogeophagus ramirezi. Bonded pairs are gentle substrate spawners. Provide sand for sifting and flat stones for spawning sites.

diet.fooditems: Mikrogeophagus spp. are benthophagous by nature, normally taking mouthfuls of substrate which are sifted for edible items with the remaining material expelled via the gill openings and mouth, although they will also browse solid surfaces and snatch items directly from the water column. In the aquari

reproduction.comments: This species is a biparental substrate spawner and is best bred in a dedicated set-up with no other fishes present. There does not appear to be any particular trigger for the spawning process with the main requirements being good diet and stringent maintenance regime. The eggs can be tricky to raise and easily develop fungus or fail to develop unless the water is very clean and of low hardness, however. Inexperienced pairs may eat their brood but often get things right after a few attempts where

vibeLine.casual: The colorful little cichlid with main character energy.
vibeLine.pro: Mikrogeophagus ramirezi. Demands warm, soft, acidic water and pristine conditions.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous but dramatic. They like their water warm and clean, and they will let you know if they’re not happy.
flavorText.pro: Sensitive to cool temperatures and poor water quality. Best kept in pairs in well-established tanks with soft, acidic parameters around pH 6.5 and 26–28°C.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified insects, small crustaceans, unidentified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Eel-shaped coldwater loach that gets restless before storms roll in 🌩️
vibeLine.pro: Misgurnus anguillicaudatus. Coldwater cobitid; barometric activity changes, tolerates 10–24°C unheated.

flavorText.casual: A long, eel-shaped loach that can live in unheated tanks year-round. Famous for becoming hyperactive before weather changes — hence the name. They're personable, hardy, and one of the few loaches that will interact with you at the glass.
flavorText.pro: Misgurnus anguillicaudatus is a coldwater cobitid (10–24°C) exhibiting well-documented increased activity in response to barometric pressure drops. Reaches 20–25 cm. Extremely hardy; tolerates low oxygen via intestinal respiration. Egg-scatterer; breeds in outdoor ponds. Invasive in some regions — never release.

diet.fooditems: Easy to feed. It will readily accept just about anything offered. For the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia and brine shrimp , along with dried flakes and granules.

reproduction.comments: Can be spawned in a similar way to many other tetras. You’ll need to set up a separate tank in which to do so if you want to raise fry in any great numbers. Something around 18″ x 12″ x 12″ in size is fine. Use very dim lighting and add clumps of fine-leaved plants such as java moss or spawning mops, to give the fish somewhere to deposit their eggs. Alternatively you could cover the base of the tank with some kind of mesh. This should be of a large enough grade so that the eggs can fall through

vibeLine.casual: The sparkly one that glitters like scattered diamonds.
vibeLine.pro: Moenkhausia pittieri. Hardy tetra; iridescent sparkle develops with age.

flavorText.casual: Plain-looking as youngsters, they mature into glittering, diamond-dusted beauties, especially the long-finned males. Hardy and peaceful, they're worth the wait. Give them a group and good lighting to see them sparkle.
flavorText.pro: Moenkhausia pittieri is a hardy tetra whose iridescent sparkle and finnage develop with age, so juveniles look unremarkable. Keep six or more for best color and shoaling. An egg-scatterer; undemanding in planted community tanks.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: A big shiny disc that eats every plant in sight.
vibeLine.pro: Myloplus rubripinnis. Large disc-shaped herbivore; schools, destroys all vegetation.

flavorText.casual: Silver dollars with a distinctive red-tipped anal fin. They school nicely in a group but will devour any plant you add. Big, peaceful herbivores that need a roomy tank with tough décor and no live greenery.
flavorText.pro: Myloplus rubripinnis is a large-bodied characin (15–20 cm) requiring spacious, plant-free tanks. Obligate herbivore in practice — feed blanched vegetables and spirulina-based foods. Schools of five or more reduce skittishness. Egg-scattering spawner; breeding reported in soft, warm water with heavy conditioning.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The regal tetra with a dark stripe and blue eyes.
vibeLine.pro: Nematobrycon palmeri. Hardy, slightly bold tetra; males display striking finnage.

flavorText.casual: A confident, elegant tetra with a bold dark stripe and shimmering blue-green eyes. Males develop showy fins and a little attitude. Hardy and easy, they bring real presence to a planted community tank.
flavorText.pro: Nematobrycon palmeri is a hardy tetra; dominant males develop extended dorsal and caudal rays and mild territoriality. Keep a group with more females to spread display energy. An egg-scatterer among fine plants.

diet.fooditems: zooplankton (unidentified zooplankton)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The elegant Tanganyikan with flowing lyre-shaped tail fins.
vibeLine.pro: Neolamprologus brichardi. Cooperative breeder; colonies defend territory aggressively.

flavorText.casual: Graceful and pale with long, trailing fin extensions, they form family groups where older siblings help raise the young. Beautiful, but they get territorial in colonies. Hard, alkaline water is a must.
flavorText.pro: Neolamprologus brichardi is a cooperative breeder from Lake Tanganyika; established colonies defend territory fiercely. Requires hard, alkaline water (pH 8–9). Older offspring assist parents in rearing subsequent broods.

diet.fooditems: Live and frozen foods should comprise a large proportion of the diet. Dried foods can be fed, but use these less often. The diet also should include some vegetable matter, such as a good quality spirulina flake. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Easy but patience is required. Bi-parental cave spawner. By far the best way to get a pair is to buy a group of young fish, allowing them to pair off naturally. Once a pair forms (this may take a year or more), the other individuals should be removed from the tank as they will not be tolerated by the pair . Unfortunately buying an adult male and female will not guarantee a pair bond and often results in the female being harassed to death. Even when a pair does form, the bond is weak and lasts on

vibeLine.casual: Solid bright yellow Tanganyikan — territorial but manageable in rocky setups.
vibeLine.pro: Neolamprologus leleupi. Tanganyikan cave-brooder; solid yellow pigmentation, territorial, pair-bonding substrate spawner.

flavorText.casual: That uniform lemon-yellow colour makes it one of the more visually striking Tanganyikan cichlids. Territorial and aggressive toward conspecifics, but manageable in a rocky setup with defined territories. Pairs bond and breed in caves. Needs hard, alkaline water and structure to claim.
flavorText.pro: Neolamprologus leleupi is a Tanganyikan cave-brooding cichlid (8–10 cm) exhibiting solid yellow-orange pigmentation. Strongly territorial; maintain as pairs with ample rocky structure defining territory boundaries. Substrate-spawner in caves; biparental care. Requires Lake Tanganyika parameters (pH 8–9, hard water). Aggressive toward conspecifics and similar-sized cave-dwellers competing for the same refuges.

diet.fooditems: nekton (lethrinops spp.)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Plays dead on the sand, then eats whatever investigates.
vibeLine.pro: Nimbochromis livingstonii. Malawi piscivore; employs thanatosis to ambush curious prey.

flavorText.casual: One of the most remarkable hunting strategies in freshwater fish — it lies on its side on the sand, blotched pattern mimicking a decomposing corpse, and waits. When a curious small fish comes to investigate the "carcass," it strikes. Needs a large tank with sand and open space to perform this behaviour.
flavorText.pro: Nimbochromis livingstonii is a Malawi haplochromine predator exhibiting thanatosis — feigning death on sandy substrates to ambush prey attracted to the apparent carcass. Reaches 25 cm; requires spacious tanks (400L+). Maternal mouthbrooder. Maintain in Malawi predator communities with similarly robust species. pH 7.8–8.6, 23–28°C. The blotched coloration serves dual function as camouflage during hunting.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Buries in sand, waits, then ambushes passing prey fish.
vibeLine.pro: Nimbochromis venustus. Large Malawi piscivore; sand-burying ambush strategy, reaches 25 cm.

flavorText.casual: Same predatory lineage as the Livingstoni but with a different trick — partially buries itself in sand and waits for small fish to swim overhead. Large, robust, and not to be housed with anything it can swallow. Males develop striking blue-and-yellow adult coloration.
flavorText.pro: Nimbochromis venustus is a Malawi haplochromine predator (25 cm) that partially buries in sand to ambush prey from below. Maternal mouthbrooder. Males transition from juvenile blotched pattern to blue-and-yellow adult coloration. Requires spacious tanks (400L+) with open sand areas for hunting behaviour. pH 7.8–8.6, 23–28°C. House with similarly large, robust Malawi species.

diet.fooditems: Biofilm and algae specialist — grazes diatoms, soft green algae, and aufwuchs from surfaces. In aquaria: algae growth, blanched zucchini/spinach, spirulina wafers. Supplement if tank is too clean.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg depositor — lays small adhesive eggs on plant leaves and glass. Minimal parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The tiny tireless algae-grazer that needs an established tank.
vibeLine.pro: Otocinclus vittatus. Small algae specialist; only for mature, biofilm-rich tanks.

flavorText.casual: Little suckermouth catfish that work nonstop on soft algae and biofilm. They're peaceful and gentle but need a mature, established tank or they'll starve. Always buy a group, never just one.
flavorText.pro: Otocinclus vittatus is a small algae and biofilm grazer that starves in new tanks lacking established films. Keep groups of six-plus in mature, stable systems. Sensitive to poor acclimation; supplement with blanched vegetables.

diet.fooditems: Xylivore/detritivore — rasps driftwood for cellulose and grazes biofilm. In aquaria: driftwood (mandatory), sinking algae wafers, blanched courgette, occasional frozen foods.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Cave spawner with paternal care — male guards eggs in narrow crevice or coconut shell
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The tiny striped pleco that munches driftwood all day.
vibeLine.pro: Panaqolus maccus. Small wood-eating pleco; driftwood is non-negotiable.

flavorText.casual: A pint-sized pleco with bold stripes that actually eats wood, so driftwood is a must, not a decoration. They stay small and tuck into caves during the day. Great for tanks too small for big plecos.
flavorText.pro: Panaqolus maccus is a small wood-rasping pleco that requires driftwood to digest properly. Largely nocturnal and cave-dwelling; a cave-brooder where males guard eggs. Supplement with vegetables, but never omit the wood.

diet.fooditems: Nocturnal omnivore/detritivore feeding on small invertebrates, worms, and organic matter in substrate. In aquaria: sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, tubifex, brine shrimp. Feeds mainly at night.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The little eel-like noodle that loves to burrow.
vibeLine.pro: Pangio kuhlii. Peaceful nocturnal loach; needs soft sand and groups.

flavorText.casual: Tiny banded noodles that wriggle through the substrate and pop out at feeding time. Shy and peaceful, they feel safest in a group with soft sand to burrow into. A fun, quirky bottom crew.
flavorText.pro: Pangio kuhlii is a peaceful, nocturnal loach that burrows, so soft sand and hiding spots are essential. Keep five or more to draw them out. Sensitive to medications and poor water; rarely bred in captivity.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified small crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Like a neon tetra that went all-in on red.
vibeLine.pro: Paracheirodon axelrodi. Soft-water schooler with full-length red stripe.

flavorText.casual: Brighter and bolder than the neon, with red running the whole length of the body. They look stunning in a big group against dark substrate and plants. Happiest in soft, slightly acidic water.
flavorText.pro: Paracheirodon axelrodi shows its full red-and-blue brilliance in soft, acidic blackwater. Best in large shoals of ten or more over dark substrate. More sensitive to hard water than the common neon.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified detritus); plants (unspecified filamentous algae)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Males turn deep ruby-black when showing off — Sri Lankan classic.
vibeLine.pro: Pethia nigrofasciata. Sri Lankan barb; males develop intense ruby-black breeding colour.

flavorText.casual: Males in breeding colour go from mild-mannered stripy barbs to deep purplish-black with a ruby glow. One of the best barb colour transformations around. Hardy, active, and good in a group in any moderately planted tank.
flavorText.pro: Pethia nigrofasciata is a Sri Lankan endemic barb reaching 5–6 cm. Males develop dramatic melanistic-ruby breeding colouration. Hardy across a range of soft to neutral water. Egg-scattering spawner; breeds readily in soft, warm water with morning light triggers. Keep six-plus; some fin-nipping if under-schooled.

diet.fooditems: Likely to be a foraging omnivore feeding on worms, insects and other small invertebrates, as well as plant material and organic detritus . In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia , and Artemia ,

reproduction.comments: Like most small cyprinids Pethia spp. are egg -scattering free spawners exhibiting no parental care . When in good condition they will spawn often and in a mature aquarium it’s possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to maximise yield a more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but a smaller aquarium should also be set up and filled with mature water. This should be very dimly lit and the base

vibeLine.casual: Males wear a bold red band you can spot across the room.
vibeLine.pro: Pethia padamya. Males display vivid red lateral band; hardy, active schooler.

flavorText.casual: Males develop one of the most eye-catching colour displays in the barb world — a thick red stripe running the length of the body. Hardy, active, and not overly nippy in a proper school. Females are plainer but still lively.
flavorText.pro: Pethia padamya males exhibit a striking red lateral band absent in females. Hardy across soft to moderately hard water; active schooler. Egg-scattering spawner; condition pairs separately and reunite in a planted breeding tank. Keep six-plus to spread any minor fin-nipping tendency.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore feeding on small invertebrates, algae, and organic matter in the wild. In aquaria: micro pellets, flakes, frozen daphnia, brine shrimp, and bloodworm.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer in soft acidic water — eggs deposited among fine-leaved plants. No parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The red-nosed schooler that moves like one fish.
vibeLine.pro: Petitella rhodostoma. Tight-schooling water-quality barometer; red nose tracks health.

flavorText.casual: Famous for schooling so tightly they look choreographed. Their bright red noses fade when water quality drops, so they double as a health gauge. They like clean, stable, soft water.
flavorText.pro: Petitella rhodostoma schools more tightly than almost any tetra and signals stress by losing nose color. Demands stable, clean, soft water, hence its intermediate rating. Stunning in groups of ten or more.

diet.fooditems: plants (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The shimmering rainbow tetra with flowing, feathery fins.
vibeLine.pro: Phenacogrammus interruptus. Larger schooling tetra; needs space and a calm tank.

flavorText.casual: Big for a tetra, with iridescent scales and long, hazy fins that catch the light beautifully. They're a bit shy, so they need a roomy tank, a good group, and calm tankmates to really shine.
flavorText.pro: Phenacogrammus interruptus is a larger schooling tetra needing more swimming room than most. Males grow flowing fin extensions; best color in groups of six-plus under subdued lighting. Skittish, so avoid boisterous tankmates and cover the tank.

diet.fooditems: detritus (detritus); others (terrestrial invertebrates); plants (unidentified fruits, vegetative parts of higher plants); zoobenthos (aquatic invertebrates)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Sold at 5 cm, grows past 60 cm — most tanks cannot.
vibeLine.pro: Piaractus brachypomus. Reaches 60 cm-plus; pond-scale housing required long-term.

flavorText.casual: That cute little "piranha look-alike" in the shop will outgrow almost every home aquarium. They hit 60 cm and need hundreds of gallons. Vegetarian and docile, but sheer size makes them unsuitable unless you have a pond or public-aquarium-scale setup. Research hard before buying.
flavorText.pro: Piaractus brachypomus routinely exceeds 60 cm TL and requires 1,000+ litre housing long-term. Herbivorous — feed vegetables, pellets, and fruit. Often impulse-purchased as juveniles and subsequently surrendered. Rarely bred in home aquaria; commercial production is pond-based. Not recommended without dedicated large-scale facilities.

diet.fooditems: Very easy to feed. It mainly preys on aquatic invertebrates in nature but in the aquarium will greedily accept just about anything offered. Meaty items such as live or frozen bloodworm , Tubifex or small earthworms are particularly relished, but dried sinking foods will also be taken. Take care not

reproduction.spawningTrait: Not thought to have been achieved in the hobby.
reproduction.comments: Not thought to have been achieved in the hobby.

vibeLine.casual: The silver speedster with whiskers longer than itself.
vibeLine.pro: Pimelodus pictus. Fast, active catfish; will eat small tankmates.

flavorText.casual: Sleek, silver, and always on the move, with trailing whiskers and endless energy. They need open swimming room and fast company. Just remember anything small enough to swallow is fair game.
flavorText.pro: Pimelodus pictus is an active, fast-swimming catfish needing open space and a group. It predates small fish, so choose tankmates carefully. Sharp pectoral spines tangle in nets; handle with a container, not mesh.

diet.fooditems: Omnivorous and easy to feed. Use a good quality sinking pellet or tablet as the staple diet. Supplement this with live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , small earthworms and similar. It’s best to add food after lights out to ensure it gets its share.

reproduction.spawningTrait: There are no reports of breeding in the hobby. Apparently in nature it spawns amongst surface vegetation.
reproduction.comments: There are no reports of breeding in the hobby. Apparently in nature it spawns amongst surface vegetation.

vibeLine.casual: Armoured, croaking, nocturnal — same deal as spotted Raphael, with stripes.
vibeLine.pro: Platydoras armatulus. Doradid; parallel care to Agamyxis, striped morph, locking spines.

flavorText.casual: Essentially the striped version of the spotted Raphael. Same croaking sounds, same tank-of-armour build, same "hide all day, emerge at night" lifestyle. Hardy and long-lived, but don't expect daytime entertainment. The locking spines make netting a bad idea — scoop with a container.
flavorText.pro: Platydoras armatulus is a doradid catfish with black-and-white lateral striping, reaching 20 cm. Care mirrors Agamyxis pectinifrons — nocturnal, armoured, long-lived, stridulating pectoral spines. Omnivorous scavenger. Frequently sold as P. costatus (a separate species). Egg-scatterer; rarely bred in captivity. Use rigid containers rather than nets to avoid spine entanglement.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The molly that shows off with a giant sail.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia velifera. Large hard-water molly; males display towering dorsal fin.

flavorText.casual: The showstopper of the molly world, where mature males raise a huge sail-like fin to impress. They need room, hard alkaline water, and warmth to look their best. Not a fish for a small tank.
flavorText.pro: Poecilia velifera is a large molly whose males develop a spectacular sailfin in spacious, hard, alkaline water. Often benefits from added marine salt. Needs warmth and volume to fully develop finnage.

diet.fooditems: Herbivorous mbuna — primarily aufwuchs grazer in the wild. In aquaria: spirulina-based foods, algae wafers, blanched vegetables. Avoid high-protein foods to prevent bloat.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Males turn ink-blue, females stay orange — same aggressive species.
vibeLine.pro: Pseudotropheus johannii. Sexually dichromatic mbuna; males melanistic blue, females orange-yellow polymorphism.

flavorText.casual: One of those mbuna where males and females look like completely different species. Males go deep dark blue while females stay bright orange-yellow. Aggression is high even by mbuna standards — overstocking and rockwork are mandatory to spread the heat. Not a beginner cichlid.
flavorText.pro: Pseudotropheus johannii is a highly aggressive mbuna exhibiting extreme sexual dichromatism — males develop melanistic blue coloration while females retain orange-yellow pigmentation. Requires overcrowded Malawi mbuna communities (ratio 1M:3-4F minimum) with extensive rockwork to diffuse territorial aggression. pH 7.8–8.6, 23–28°C. One of the more belligerent mbuna species; subordinate males suppressed to female coloration. Not suitable for mixed community tanks.

diet.fooditems: plants (unspecified algae)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Burrow spawner — digs tunnels in river banks. Paternal care. Rarely bred in home aquaria due to size requirements
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The huge-finned algae tank that quickly outgrows small setups.
vibeLine.pro: Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps. Large pleco; heavy bioload, big-tank commitment only.

flavorText.casual: Stunning leopard pattern and a tall sail fin, but make no mistake, this one gets big. It produces a lot of waste and outgrows most community tanks fast. A centerpiece for large setups only.
flavorText.pro: Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps reaches well over a foot and produces a heavy bioload, demanding a large tank and strong filtration. Provide driftwood and supplement algae with vegetables. Frequently sold too small for the tank it will need.

diet.fooditems: insects, worms, crustaceans

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat, hard surfaces (slate, rocks, or broad leaves) for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Attentive parents; both guard the eggs and fry vigorously.

vibeLine.casual: The heavyweight brawler that glitters with blue spangles.
vibeLine.pro: Rocio octofasciata. Large, aggressive cichlid; stunning spangling, serious territorial streak.

flavorText.casual: Named after a famous boxer for good reason. Mature fish are covered in electric blue-green spangles that flash under good light. Tough and territorial, they're a bold pick that needs space and careful tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Rocio octofasciata develops dazzling blue-green spangling and an equally strong territorial temper, especially when breeding. A substrate spawner that guards fry hard. Needs a large tank and robust, similarly-sized company.

diet.fooditems: nekton (fish)

reproduction.comments: Not particularly difficult. Maternal mouthbrooder. Ideally, it should be spawned in a species tank in a harem of one male and at least 3 females. However, it will often spawn in the community aquarium . A 60″ aquarium is a good size (although larger is preferable) and this should be furnished as suggested above, along with some flat, sloping rocks and areas of open substrate to act as potential spawning sites. The pH should be around 8.2-8.5 and the temperature 77-80°F. Condition the fish with a

vibeLine.casual: The electric blue predator of the Malawi rock pile.
vibeLine.pro: Sciaenochromis fryeri. Piscivorous Malawi hap; stunning blue, moderately aggressive.

flavorText.casual: Males are an intense, almost unreal electric blue. They're predators in the wild, so don't trust them with anything small enough to eat. Hard, alkaline water and a spacious tank bring out their best.
flavorText.pro: Sciaenochromis fryeri is a piscivorous haplochromine from Lake Malawi. Males develop intense blue; females are silver. Requires hard, alkaline water and a large tank. A maternal mouthbrooder; keep one male to several females.

ecology.comments: Found in quiet acidic blackwater areas of the Amazon basin.
ecology.biotope: Slow-moving Amazonian tributaries and flooded forests under dense canopy cover.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a conspecific group of 6+ individuals to prevent submissive stress and establish a natural social hierarchy.

diet.fooditems: small crustaceans, worms, organic debris
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed premium beefheart mixes, freeze-dried tubifex, and frozen bloodworms. Enjoys grazing on high-protein pellets throughout the day to support body growth.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide vertical slate, breeding cones, or broad-leafed plants (e.g., Amazon Swords) for vertical egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Lays eggs on vertical surfaces. Parents produce a specialized skin mucus that serves as the primary food source for newly hatched fry.

vibeLine.casual: The round, regal "king" of the planted aquarium.
vibeLine.pro: Symphysodon aequifasciatus. Demanding warmwater cichlid; pristine, stable parameters essential.

flavorText.casual: Big, round, and stunning, they really do look like living dinner plates of color. They're also divas about water quality and warmth. A rewarding fish once you've got the basics down, not a starter pick.
flavorText.pro: Symphysodon aequifasciatus demands warm (28-30°C), soft, spotless water and a group of six or more to settle. Bonded pairs guard eggs and feed fry on body slime. Sensitive to nitrate and parameter swings; an advanced keeper's fish.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Tall feathery dorsal fin on a peaceful, medium-large African catfish.
vibeLine.pro: Synodontis eupterus. West African synodontid; elaborate dorsal finnage, peaceful, reaches 20 cm.

flavorText.casual: That dramatic, sail-like dorsal fin is the draw — it unfurls impressively as the fish matures. Grows to around 20 cm but stays peaceful for a synodontis. Hardy, adaptable, and works in most community tanks large enough to house it. Nocturnal but visible at feeding time.
flavorText.pro: Synodontis eupterus is a West African synodontid reaching ~20 cm, distinguished by its elongated, feather-like dorsal fin rays. Peaceful for the genus; compatible with a range of medium-large community fish. Omnivorous; sinking foods after dark. Egg-scatterer; not commonly bred in home aquaria. Hardy across neutral to moderately alkaline water.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified vegetable degree); others (ants); plants (unidentified filamentous algae); zoobenthos (beetles, unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The quirky catfish that swims belly-up on purpose.
vibeLine.pro: Synodontis nigriventris. Peaceful nocturnal Synodontis; genuinely swims inverted to feed.

flavorText.casual: Yes, it really does swim upside down, an adaptation for feeding at the surface from below. Peaceful, social, and most active in the evening. A fun, unusual catfish for a calm community tank.
flavorText.pro: Synodontis nigriventris is a peaceful, nocturnal catfish that genuinely swims inverted to graze the underside of the surface and leaves. Keep a small group with hiding spots. Its belly is darker than its back, reversed countershading.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The tough little gem that's happy in cooler water.
vibeLine.pro: Tanichthys albonubes. Subtropical shoaler; thrives unheated in cooler rooms.

flavorText.casual: Underrated and incredibly easy. They show their best colors in cooler water and don't even need a heater in a warm room. Peaceful, pretty, and perfect for a first tank.
flavorText.pro: Tanichthys albonubes is a hardy subtropical species best kept between 15 and 22°C. Colors intensify in groups and cooler conditions. An excellent unheated nano or coldwater community fish.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified small insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The tetra that swims at a permanent jaunty angle.
vibeLine.pro: Thayeria boehlkei. Hardy, peaceful tetra; distinctive oblique resting posture.

flavorText.casual: Famous for hovering at a head-up angle, like they're always looking at something interesting above them. Peaceful, hardy, and easy to keep in a group. A fun, quirky addition to a community tank.
flavorText.pro: Thayeria boehlkei is a hardy, peaceful tetra known for its oblique resting posture. Keep six or more for natural shoaling. Tolerant of a wide parameter range and undemanding in planted community setups. An egg-scatterer.

diet.fooditems: opportunistic omnivore — insects, plant matter, small invertebrates

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat stones or slate for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Highly developed brood care; both parents tend eggs and fry.

vibeLine.casual: The bluffer that flares a fiery red throat.
vibeLine.pro: Thorichthys meeki. Moderately peaceful cichlid; threat display over real aggression.

flavorText.casual: When they want to look tough, they puff out a brilliant red throat to seem bigger. Mostly bluff rather than brawl, they're one of the calmer cichlids. Great if you want personality without constant warfare.
flavorText.pro: Thorichthys meeki intimidates rivals by flaring its red throat and gill covers, relying on display more than damage. A relatively peaceful substrate-spawning cichlid that guards fry diligently. Tolerant tankmates and some cover keep things calm.

diet.fooditems: Unfussy and will accept most foods offered. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Easy. Bubblenester. The breeding tank should contain water of no more than 6-8″ in depth with lots of floating plants. Filtration should be gentle and air-powered filters are ideal. Condition the fish with live food. When the female becomes plump with eggs the male will construct a large bubblenest of up to 10″ diameter amongst the floating plants. He will then begin to display to the female. As the female approaches the nest the fish can be seen touching each other with their modified ventral f

vibeLine.casual: The lace-patterned beauty that glows when it's happy.
vibeLine.pro: Trichopodus leerii. Peaceful, elegant gourami; needs warmth and height.

flavorText.casual: Covered in a delicate pearly net pattern, with breeding males flushing fiery orange on the throat. Peaceful and graceful, they're one of the easier large gouramis. Give them a calm tank with some height.
flavorText.pro: Trichopodus leerii is a peaceful, hardy gourami whose males develop a bright orange breast when spawning. A bubblenest builder that values warm, calm water and surface plants. One of the most forgiving mid-size labyrinth fish.

diet.fooditems: Probably a micropredator feeding on small insects, worms, crustaceans, and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia , and Artemia , alongside good quality dried flake

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: This species exhibits no parental care although it does utilise a different spawning method to most small cyprinids as the eggs are attached to the underside of broad plant leaves or other objects rather than scattered randomly. When the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it is possible that small numbers of young may start to appear without human intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled appr

vibeLine.casual: Coppery little rasbora with a narrow lambchop-shaped side mark.
vibeLine.pro: Trigonostigma espei. Copper-orange body with narrow lambchop mark; classic easy cyprinid.

flavorText.casual: Warm copper-orange body with a thin dark wedge that narrows to a lambchop shape. Easy, peaceful, and looks incredible in a planted tank with dark substrate. Often confused with Hengel's — this one is more uniformly orange.
flavorText.pro: Trigonostigma espei displays a narrower, more linear wedge than T. heteromorpha, on a copper-orange body. Care mirrors other Trigonostigma — soft, slightly acidic water, groups of eight-plus. Egg-depositing spawner adhering eggs beneath broad leaves. Commonly mislabelled as T. hengeli in trade.

diet.fooditems: Probably a micropredator feeding on small insects, worms, crustaceans, and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia , and Artemia , alongside good quality dried flake

reproduction.comments: This species exhibits no parental care although it does utilise a different spawning method to most small cyprinids as the eggs are attached to the underside of broad plant leaves or other objects rather than scattered randomly. When the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it is possible that small numbers of young may start to appear without human intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled appr

vibeLine.casual: The harlequin's slimmer cousin with a bright orange-edged wedge.
vibeLine.pro: Trigonostigma hengeli. Slimmer wedge mark than T. heteromorpha; orange-bordered lambchop.

flavorText.casual: Similar to the classic harlequin rasbora but with a narrower wedge mark edged in glowing orange. Equally easy and peaceful. If you like the harlequin look but want something a touch more refined, this is your fish.
flavorText.pro: Trigonostigma hengeli is distinguished from T. heteromorpha by a narrower, more defined wedge mark with prominent orange edging. Care is identical — soft, slightly acidic water, groups of eight-plus. Egg-depositing spawner; adheres eggs to the underside of broad leaves. Often confused with T. espei in trade.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified small animals)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer — buries into substrate. Rarely bred in captivity
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Buries in sand with only its eyes sticking out — seriously.
vibeLine.pro: Acantopsis dialuzona. Sand-burying loach; elongated snout, peaceful, requires deep fine substrate.

flavorText.casual: One of the weirdest loach behaviours in the hobby — it dives headfirst into sand and disappears completely, leaving only its tiny eyes poking out. Peaceful and easy to keep as long as you give it deep, fine sand to vanish into.
flavorText.pro: Acantopsis dialuzona is a fossorial loach requiring a deep bed (5+ cm) of fine sand to exhibit natural burying behaviour. Elongated snout and streamlined body facilitate rapid substrate entry. Peaceful; reaches 15–20 cm. Egg-scatterer; breeding rarely reported. Nocturnal feeder; sinking foods after lights-out.

diet.fooditems: Most meaty live and frozen foods are readily accepted. It won’t usually take dried varieties. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Possible. Substrate spawner. It may breed in the community aquarium but if you want to raise a full brood of these, a separate aquarium should be used. Set this up as any other Tanganyikan tank , using piles of rocks to form caves and crevices. Flowerpots or shells can also be used to create the desired effect. Water should be hard and alkaline with a pH of around 8.0-8.5 and a temperature of 77-80°F. The best way of obtaining a pair is to buy a group of juveniles and let them pair off naturally

vibeLine.casual: Paper-thin predator that slides into rock cracks to hunt fry.
vibeLine.pro: Altolamprologus calvus. Laterally compressed Tanganyikan; pearlescent scaling, crevice-hunting micro-predator strategy.

flavorText.casual: Built like a knife blade — so laterally compressed it can slip into rock crevices where other fish can't follow, picking off fry and invertebrates hiding inside. Pearlescent scales give it a shimmering look that photographs don't capture well. Slow grower, patient hunter. Needs rocky Tanganyikan setup.
flavorText.pro: Altolamprologus calvus is a laterally compressed Tanganyikan cichlid adapted for crevice predation — its narrow body profile allows access to rock interstices to prey on fry and small invertebrates. Pearlescent scale iridescence distinguishes it from A. compressiceps. Extremely slow growth rate. pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Provide stacked rockwork with varied crevice widths. Passive feeder; avoid housing with aggressive competitors for food.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified shrimps)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Laterally compressed Tanganyikan predator — built to squeeze into rock crevices.
vibeLine.pro: Altolamprologus compressiceps. Tanganyikan predator; extreme lateral compression for crevice-hunting, slow-growing, cave-brooding.

flavorText.casual: That impossibly thin body isn't decorative — it lets this fish slide into rock crevices to ambush shrimp and fry hiding inside. A slow, deliberate predator from Lake Tanganyika. Needs hard, alkaline water and a rockscape with plenty of structure. Grows slowly but reaches 12–15 cm eventually.
flavorText.pro: Altolamprologus compressiceps exhibits extreme lateral compression adapted for predation within rock interstices. Slow-growing; males reach 12–15 cm over several years. Cave-brooder; female deposits eggs deep in shells or crevices. Requires Lake Tanganyika parameters (pH 8–9, hard water). Feed meaty foods (frozen, live). Compatible with other Tanganyikan cichlids that don't occupy the same microhabitat.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Classic orange lyretail — the friendliest killi to start with.
vibeLine.pro: Aphyosemion australe. Non-annual aplocheilid; orange lyretail morph widely available, beginner-accessible.

flavorText.casual: If you want to try killifish without anxiety, start here. The orange lyretail form is hardy, forgiving, and gorgeous — deep orange with a lyre-shaped tail. Not annual, breeds in mops without special egg storage, and tolerates a range of conditions. Just keep a lid on it.
flavorText.pro: Aphyosemion australe is a non-annual aplocheilid from coastal West Africa, with the orange lyretail morph being the most common in trade. Eggs deposited on mops or fine vegetation hatch in 14–21 days submerged — no diapause required. Hardy across pH 6–7 and 21–24°C. Widely regarded as the most beginner-friendly Aphyosemion species. Tight cover essential.

diet.fooditems: Carnivorous surface feeder — small insects, mosquito larvae, micro crustaceans. In aquaria: live/frozen daphnia, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, micro worms.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Colourful West African killi with two dark lateral stripes 🐟
vibeLine.pro: Aphyosemion bivittatum. Non-annual West African aplocheilid; bivittate pattern, readily bred.

flavorText.casual: Two dark stripes along the body give it the name, but the real draw is the colour males develop — reds, blues, and yellows depending on the population. Non-annual with straightforward mop-breeding and a normal lifespan. Soft, slightly acidic water and a secure lid.
flavorText.pro: Aphyosemion bivittatum is a non-annual aplocheilid from West Africa, characterised by two lateral dark bands. Males develop polychromatic coloration varying by population. Egg-depositor on mops or vegetation; eggs hatch in 14–21 days submerged. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6–7, 20–24°C). Multiple geographic variants — maintain locality records. Tight cover required.

diet.fooditems: Aphyosemion species feed largely on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates in the wild. In captivity small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia or bloodworm are preferred although the fish will also accept good quality flake in most cases.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: A number of different methods have proven successful for spawning Aphyosemion species in aquaria, and much is down to personal preference. It is often recommended that a trio comprising a single male and two females should be used, but brood sizes tend to be lower under these circumstances, and it can also be bred using a single pair. Many breeders do not use filtration, but the use of a small, air-driven sponge filter to prevent stagnation is advisable. Water should be around neutral in terms o

vibeLine.casual: Vivid red horizontal stripes on males — looks hand-painted.
vibeLine.pro: Aphyosemion striatum. Non-annual aplocheilid; males exhibit bold red longitudinal striping.

flavorText.casual: Males look like someone painted precise red lines along their body — one of the most graphic patterns in freshwater fish. Non-annual, so normal lifespan and straightforward breeding in mops or fine-leaved plants. Prefers cooler, soft water and a covered tank.
flavorText.pro: Aphyosemion striatum is a non-annual aplocheilid from Gabon, males distinguished by bold red longitudinal striping on a green-gold body. Prefers cooler conditions (20–24°C) and soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6–7). Egg-depositor; eggs hatch in 14–18 days without diapause. Tight cover essential.
