Multiple collection points exist — maintain locality data where possible.

diet.fooditems: Primarily carnivorous and apparently feeds mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia and chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most specimens will also learn to accept dried alternatives with pelleted pro

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry . The female is responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: The sharp-tailed showstopper of the dwarf cichlid world.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma agassizii. Classic soft-water Apisto; dramatic tail, cave-brooder.

flavorText.casual: Males have a striking spade-shaped tail and vivid markings that vary wildly between localities. They prefer soft, acidic water and a harem setup. More demanding than the cockatoo but stunning when thriving.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma agassizii is a classic soft-water apisto with numerous color morphs. Requires soft, acidic water (pH 5–7) for best health and breeding. A cave-brooder; males maintain territories with multiple females.

diet.fooditems: Primarily carnivorous and feeds mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia and chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most specimens will also learn to accept dried alternatives with pelleted products gener

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: Dramatic fin extensions on males — long-finned and soft-water dependent.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma bitaeniata. Elongate finnage in males; soft acidic water required, Peruvian origin.

flavorText.casual: Males develop impressively long, trailing fin extensions that make them one of the more elegant-looking apistos. Needs genuinely soft, acidic water to thrive — not a species that tolerates neutral or hard conditions well. The finnage is the draw, but it demands the right environment.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma bitaeniata is a Peruvian species distinguished by elongated unpaired fin extensions in males. Requires soft, acidic water (pH 5–6.5, low hardness) for long-term health and full fin development. Cave-brooder; females guard fry. Multiple colour morphs exist across its range. Sensitive to elevated hardness — RO water recommended in most tap water areas.

ecology.comments: Inhabits slow-moving streams and flooded areas.
ecology.biotope: Slow-moving streams, pools, and flooded grasslands in the Paraguay river basin.
ecology.socialBehavior: Best kept in pairs or harems (1 male to multiple females) to manage territorial aggression.

diet.fooditems: insects, larvae
diet.feedingPlaybook: Provide small frozen food like daphnia, cyclops, and baby brine shrimp. Flakes are accepted but should not be the sole diet.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide half coconut shells, small clay caves, or dense leaf litter.
reproduction.comments: Maternal mouthbrooder / cave breeder. The female guards the eggs inside the cave while the male defends the outer territory.

vibeLine.casual: The shy little dwarf cichlid with a subtle shine.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma borellii. Cool-tolerant dwarf Apisto; cave-brooder, female guards fry.

flavorText.casual: More subtle than the flashy cockatoo, with a soft blue sheen and a gentle temperament. They tolerate cooler water than most apistos and are happy in a well-planted tank. Peaceful with anything not cave-sized.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma borellii is one of the more cold-tolerant apistos, handling temps down to 20°C. A cave-brooder where the female assumes primary fry defense. Keep one male to two-plus females in a well-planted tank.

diet.fooditems: Primarily carnivorous and apparently feeds mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia and chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most specimens will also learn to accept dried alternatives with pelleted pro

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: The colorful little show-off with wild spiked dorsal fins.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma cacatuoides. Hardy dwarf Apisto; spectacular finnage, cave-brooder.

flavorText.casual: Males are living fireworks, with dramatic spiked dorsal fins and bold reds and oranges. They strut around their territory and let everyone know it's theirs. One of the hardier Apistogramma species and a great entry point.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma cacatuoides is one of the hardiest apistos, tolerating wider parameters than many congeners. Males display spectacular extended dorsal rays. A cave-brooder; females guard fry aggressively. Keep one male to two or three females.

diet.fooditems: Apistogramma spp. are chiefly carnivorous and feed mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia , Moina and chironomid larvae (bloodworm) should be offered regularly although most species will also learn to accept dried alternatives with

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is mostly responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive. While spawning this species is not difficult under the correct conditions, raising the fry during the early weeks of life may present difficulties since they’re highly sensitive to changes in water chemistry. Successful breeders tend to recommend f

vibeLine.casual: Demands extreme soft acidic water — stunning but not for casual keepers.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma elizabethae. Obligate blackwater apisto; pH 4.5–6, near-zero hardness, advanced husbandry required.

flavorText.casual: One of the most demanding apistos in the hobby. Needs water so soft and acidic that standard tap water is useless — think pH 4.5 to 6 and essentially zero hardness. Males are genuinely spectacular, but maintaining those conditions long-term separates committed keepers from window shoppers.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma elizabethae requires extreme blackwater conditions — pH 4.5–6.0, GH <1, KH 0. RO water with botanical acidification (leaves, peat) essential. Cave-brooding; females develop intense yellow and guard fry aggressively. Wild-caught specimens predominate. High sensitivity to dissolved organics; frequent small water changes with matched parameters. Among the most challenging Apistogramma in maintenance.

diet.fooditems: Primarily carnivorous and feeds mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia and chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most specimens will also learn to accept dried alternatives with pelleted products gener

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: Red-and-yellow Venezuelan apisto — colourful and moderately demanding.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma hongsloi. Venezuelan apisto; red-yellow coloration, moderately acidic water sufficient.

flavorText.casual: One of the more colourful apistos — males develop bold red and yellow with maturity. From Venezuela's llanos, so it doesn't need the extreme blackwater conditions some congeners demand. Still prefers soft, slightly acidic water, but more forgiving than the pH-4.5 crowd.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma hongsloi originates from Venezuelan llanos habitats. Males develop prominent red and yellow pigmentation. Less demanding than extreme blackwater congeners — pH 5.5–6.8, soft to moderately soft water sufficient. Cave-brooder; females guard fry with typical apistogrammine maternal care. Multiple commercial colour variants available. Breeds well in dedicated pairs with adequate territory.

diet.fooditems: Primarily? carnivorous ? and apparently feeds mostly on? benthic ? invertebrates in nature. In the? aquarium ? live and frozen foods such as? Artemia ,? Daphnia ? and? chironomid ? larvae ? ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most specimens will also learn to accept dried alternatives

reproduction.comments: Substrate ? spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is responsible for post- spawning ? care of eggs and? fry ? and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: One of the more commonly available apistos — colourful and adaptable.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma macmasteri. Widely available Colombian apisto; tolerates broader parameters.

flavorText.casual: Among the apistos you'll actually find in shops. Males show strong reds in the tail and dorsal, and it's somewhat more tolerant of varied water conditions than many of its relatives. Still does best in soft, slightly acidic water, but won't collapse if your pH drifts above 7 briefly.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma macmasteri is a Colombian species and one of the more readily available Apistogramma in commercial trade. Tolerates pH 5.5–6.8 and moderate hardness better than strict blackwater congeners. Cave-brooder with female brood care. Several colour variants and locality forms exist — often confused with A. viejita in trade. Males reach 7–8 cm.

diet.fooditems: Primarily carnivorous and apparently feeds mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia and chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most specimens will also learn to accept dried alternatives with pelleted pro

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: The "panda apisto" — females turn bright yellow when guarding fry.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma nijsseni. Panda apisto; females turn vivid yellow during brood care.

flavorText.casual: Called the panda apisto for the dark markings on a pale body, but the real surprise is the females — they turn bright yellow during brood care and become the showpiece of the tank. Needs soft, acidic water (pH 5–6.5) and a well-structured tank with caves.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma nijsseni is distinguished by female nuptial coloration — vivid yellow with contrasting dark markings during brood care. Males smaller and less colourful than many congeners. Requires soft acidic water (pH 5–6.5, low hardness). Cave-brooder; females aggressively defend territories around fry. Peruvian origin. Breeds in dedicated setups with multiple caves and visual barriers.

diet.fooditems: Apistogramma spp. are chiefly carnivorous and feed mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia and chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most species will also learn to accept dried alternatives with pellet

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: Sky-blue males with orange highlights — compact and moderately demanding.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma panduro. Peruvian apisto; sky-blue males, soft acidic water preferred.

flavorText.casual: Males develop a sky-blue body with orange accents on the ventral area — one of the more visually clean colour combinations in apistos. Peruvian species needing soft, acidic water but not as extreme as the pH-4.5 specialists. Compact at around 6 cm.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma panduro is a Peruvian species, males reaching ~6 cm with sky-blue coloration and orange ventral accents. Requires soft, acidic water (pH 5–6.5) but not as extreme as obligate blackwater congeners. Cave-brooder; females guard fry with yellow maternal dress. Breeds in dedicated pairs with multiple hiding spots. Sometimes confused with A. nijsseni in trade.

diet.fooditems: Apistogramma spp. are chiefly carnivorous and feed mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia , Moina and chironomid larvae (bloodworm) should be offered regularly although most species will also learn to accept dried alternatives with

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. Sexually-mature males establish territories and react aggressively to other males in the vicinity, though the presence of several females is normally tolerated. Post-spawning the male usually returns to protecting his larger territory and courting other females leaving the female to guard and tend the eggs and fry. Depending on temperature the eggs hatch in 36-72 hours with the fry free-swimming after a furth

vibeLine.casual: Widespread South American apisto with three lateral bands — widely available.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma trifasciata. Widespread SA apisto; multiple locality forms, moderate demands.

flavorText.casual: One of the more geographically widespread apistos, found across a large range in South America. That means varied colour forms depending on collection point. Not as water-chemistry-sensitive as the blackwater specialists — does fine in soft to neutral conditions. A solid mid-tier apisto.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma trifasciata has one of the broadest distributions in the genus, spanning southern Brazil, Paraguay, and Argentina. Multiple locality colour forms exist. Tolerates pH 5.5–7 and soft to moderately soft water — less demanding than blackwater-obligate congeners. Cave-brooder; standard apistogrammine female brood care. Cool-tolerant (down to 22°C).

diet.fooditems: Apistogramma spp. are chiefly carnivorous and feed mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia , Moina and chironomid larvae (bloodworm) should be offered regularly although most species will also learn to accept dried alternatives with

reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. Sexually-mature males establish territories and react aggressively to other males in the vicinity, though the presence of several females is normally tolerated. Post-spawning the male usually returns to protecting his larger territory and courting other females leaving the female to guard and tend the eggs and fry, although when maintained as a single pair he may help guard the fry once mobile. Depending on t

vibeLine.casual: Fire-red spotted cheeks on males — often confused with macmasteri.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma viejita. Colombian apisto; red-spotted opercular markings, frequently misidentified as A. macmasteri.

flavorText.casual: The red spotting on the cheeks and gill covers makes mature males immediately recognisable — when you can actually get the right species. Often confused with or sold as A. macmasteri in shops. Needs soft, acidic water and structured territory with caves and sight breaks.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma viejita is a Colombian species frequently confused with A. macmasteri in commercial trade. Distinguished by pronounced red opercular spotting in males. Requires soft acidic water (pH 5.5–6.8); less tolerant of elevated hardness than macmasteri. Cave-brooder with female brood care. Verify sourcing carefully — mislabelling is common. Males reach 6–7 cm.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore taking small invertebrates and zooplankton in the wild. In aquaria: micro pellets, flakes, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworm.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer — deposits eggs among fine-leaved plants. No parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: A zippy little blue tetra that never sits still.
vibeLine.pro: Boehlkea fredcochui. Active small tetra; iridescent blue, straightforward care.

flavorText.casual: Flashes of electric blue that streak around the tank in a tight group. They're small but fast and always on the move. Easy to keep and pairs well with other active community fish of similar size.
flavorText.pro: Boehlkea fredcochui is a small, active tetra displaying strong iridescent blue under side-lighting. Undemanding in soft to moderately hard water. Egg-scattering spawner; conditioning with live foods improves results. Keep in groups of eight-plus for best schooling display.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: A dot-sized micro-fish for calm nano worlds.
vibeLine.pro: Boraras maculatus. Micro-cyprinid; nano shoaler comparable to B. brigittae in care.

flavorText.casual: Barely bigger than a grain of rice when young. They hover together in little clusters among plants and look their best in a small, dedicated setup with dark substrate. Keep them away from anything that could eat them.
flavorText.pro: Boraras maculatus is a micro-cyprinid reaching approximately 2.5 cm. Similar care to B. brigittae — soft, slightly acidic water and micro-foods (infusoria for fry). Egg-scattering spawner among fine vegetation. Best in species-focused nanos or with equally diminutive tankmates.

diet.fooditems: As with other Boraras species it’s likely to be a micropredator feeding on small insects, worms, crustaceans and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should not be fed these exclusively. Daily meals of small live and frozen fare such as Daphn

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg -scattering, continuous spawner that exhibits zero parental care . That is to say when in good condition and in the presence of both males and females relatively small numbers of eggs will be laid daily. In a well-furnished, mature aquarium it is therefore possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without human intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult gro

vibeLine.casual: A red-orange spark in a very, very small package.
vibeLine.pro: Boraras merah. Red-orange micro-cyprinid; soft acidic water, nano groups.

flavorText.casual: Another miniature Boraras, this one in warm reds and oranges. They're shy at first but colour up well once settled in a quiet, tannin-stained nano. Keep them in a big group away from anything large enough to eat them.
flavorText.pro: Boraras merah reaches approximately 2 cm and requires soft, acidic water for optimal coloration. Similar husbandry to B. brigittae and B. maculatus. Egg-scattering spawner among fine vegetation; fry require infusoria. Best in dedicated species or Boraras-community nano setups.

diet.fooditems: zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Micro-fish with a spot-and-line marking that literally looks like "!"
vibeLine.pro: Boraras urophthalmoides. Micro-cyprinid; distinctive exclamation-mark lateral marking pattern.

flavorText.casual: Squint and you can actually see an exclamation mark on its side — a dark lateral line ending in a round spot near the tail. One of the slightly hardier Boraras, still best in a dedicated nano with soft water and tiny tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Boraras urophthalmoides is a micro-cyprinid (~3 cm) distinguished by a dark lateral stripe terminating in a caudal spot, forming an exclamation-mark pattern. Somewhat hardier than other Boraras species. Egg-scatterer; soft, acidic water preferred for breeding. Keep in groups of ten-plus.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Named for the green glint in its eye — subtle charm.
vibeLine.pro: Brevibora dorsiocellata. Peaceful small rasbora; distinctive dorsal ocellus and green iridescent eye.

flavorText.casual: A small, understated rasbora whose calling card is a vivid green flash in the eye when the light hits right. Peaceful, easy, and works in any calm community. Not a show-stopper, but a quiet looker in a planted tank.
flavorText.pro: Brevibora dorsiocellata is a small cyprinid (3–4 cm) with a prominent dorsal ocellus and iridescent green eye reflections. Undemanding in soft to moderately hard water. Egg-scattering spawner; relatively easy to breed in soft, acidic conditions. Keep eight-plus for natural shoaling.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified fish); others (unidentified terrestrial insects); zoobenthos (unidentified insect larvae)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg depositor — lays eggs on flat surfaces or in depressions. Minimal parental care after spawning
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Flat, leaf-shaped catfish that sits motionless until food drifts past.
vibeLine.pro: Bunocephalus coracoideus. Fossorial ambush feeder; extremely cryptic, leaf-mimicking aspredinid catfish.

flavorText.casual: Looks like a dead leaf on the bottom of the tank — and acts like one too. Barely moves during the day, then ambushes small sinking foods at night. Harmless, weird, and oddly satisfying to keep if you enjoy the "is it alive?" game.
flavorText.pro: Bunocephalus coracoideus is an aspredinid catfish exhibiting extreme crypsis and minimal locomotion. Ambush-feeds nocturnally on sinking invertebrate foods. Reaches ~15 cm. Fine sand or leaf litter substrate preferred. Egg-scatterer; males reported to guard eggs in some accounts. Hardy but easily outcompeted for food — target-feed after lights-out.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The only fish that actually flies — keep the lid tight.
vibeLine.pro: Carnegiella strigata. True aerial glider; obligate surface dweller, escape-prone.

flavorText.casual: Hatchetfish are the only fish that use powered flight — their pectoral muscles let them glide above the surface. They live exclusively in the top inch of water. A tight-fitting lid with no gaps is non-negotiable or you'll find them on the floor.
flavorText.pro: Carnegiella strigata possesses enlarged pectoral musculature enabling true powered gliding above the water surface. Obligate surface feeder; offer floating foods and small insects. Requires a completely sealed lid. Sensitive to water quality fluctuations. Egg-scatterer; rarely bred in captivity. Soft, acidic blackwater conditions preferred.

diet.fooditems: Herbivorous mbuna — aufwuchs grazer feeding on algae and biofilm from rocks. In aquaria: spirulina flakes/pellets, algae wafers, blanched spinach. Strictly avoid high-protein foods.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Maternal mouthbrooder — female incubates eggs in mouth for 3-4 weeks
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The tiny striped mbuna with outsized aggression.
vibeLine.pro: Chindongo demasoni. Small but extremely aggressive mbuna; large groups essential.

flavorText.casual: Beautiful dark blue and black stripes on a small body, but don't let the size fool you — they're one of the most aggressive mbuna. You need a big group (twelve-plus) to keep any one fish from being singled out.
flavorText.pro: Chindongo demasoni is notoriously aggressive for its size. Keep in large groups of twelve or more to prevent targeted bullying. Requires hard, alkaline water and a heavily rocked tank. A maternal mouthbrooder.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as bloodworm , Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to survive on ‘left-overs’

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Can be bred in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras species . Use a ratio of 2 males per female if possible, and when the females are visibly full of eggs perform a large (50-70%) water change with cooler water, and increase oxygenation and flow in the tank . Repeat this daily until the fish spawn . Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, but it’s recommended to provide alternatives in the form of fine-leaved vegetation or fine spawning mops. Once spawning is complete either adul

vibeLine.casual: The sleek cory with a bold orange head patch.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras adolfoi. Premium cory; orange crown, sensitive to poor acclimation.

flavorText.casual: Highly prized for the vivid orange blaze behind its head. They're a bit more sensitive than the common corys, so stable water and gentle acclimation matter. Beautiful reward for careful keeping.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras adolfoi commands a premium price and is more sensitive than hardy species like aeneus or paleatus. Requires stable, soft water and careful acclimation. An egg-depositing spawner; keep groups of six-plus over smooth sand.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as bloodworm , Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to survive on ‘left-overs’

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The spotted cory with a distinctive dark lateral band.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras agassizii. Hardy community cory; classic care, groups over sand.

flavorText.casual: A handsome spotted cory with a prominent dark band down each side. Hardy and easy like most corys — give them sand, friends, and sinking food and they'll bumble happily along the bottom.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras agassizii is a hardy, peaceful bottom-dweller with classic cory care requirements. Keep six or more over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner triggered by cool water changes and increased feeding.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as bloodworm , Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to survive on ‘left-overs’

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The pale cory with a bold black arched stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras arcuatus. Distinctive arched-stripe cory; peaceful and standard care.

flavorText.casual: A clean pale body with a bold dark stripe arching from snout over the back to the tail. Elegant and easy to spot in a group. Standard cory care: sand, sinking food, friends.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras arcuatus is distinguished by a continuous dark arch from snout to caudal peduncle. Standard cory husbandry: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; peaceful and hardy.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as bloodworm , Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to survive on ‘left-overs’

reproduction.spawningTrait: Can be bred in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras species . Use a ratio of 2 males per female if possible, and when the females are visibly ful

Use a ratio of 2 males per female if possible, and when the females are visibly full of eggs perform a large (50-70%) water change with cooler water, and increase oxygenation and flow in the tank . Repeat this daily until the fish spawn . Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, but it’s recommended to provide alternatives in the form of fine-leaved vegetation or fine spawning mops. Once spawning is complete either adul

vibeLine.casual: The delicate little cory with ink-dot shoulder spots.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras atropersonatus. Small, peaceful cory; distinctive dark shoulder blotch.

flavorText.casual: Named for its delicate build and prominent dark spots near the gills. Peaceful and easy like all corys, just a little smaller and more subtle. Group them over sand and enjoy their quiet charm.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras atropersonatus is a small, peaceful cory distinguished by bold dark shoulder spots. Standard care: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. Less commonly available; an egg-depositing spawner.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as bloodworm , Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to survive on ‘left-overs’

reproduction.comments: Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, or among fine-leaved vegetation. Once spawning is complete the eggs can be left alone or removed. Under the latter cicrcumstances the new container should contain the same water as the spawning tank and be similarly well-oxygenated. Most breeders add a few drops of methylene blue, or an alder cone or two at this point in order to prevent the eggs developing fungus. Incubation is normally 3-4 days and once the fry have fully-absorbed their yolk s

vibeLine.casual: The uniform slate-grey cory with a moody minimalist look.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras concolor. Monochrome cory; uniform slate color, standard care.

flavorText.casual: No spots, no stripes — just a smooth, uniform slate grey that gives them a sleek, minimalist look. Different from the usual busy patterns but just as easy to keep. Sand, group, done.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras concolor is unusual among corys for its uniform dark coloration without distinct markings. Standard care: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; occasionally available in the trade.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as chironomid larvae (bloodworm), Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to surv

reproduction.comments: Reported to deposit eggs either on the substrate or around the water line.

vibeLine.casual: The Adolfo look-alike that's a little hardier and cheaper.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras duplicareus. Hardy Adolfo mimic; orange crown, more accessible price.

flavorText.casual: Nearly identical to Adolfo's Corydoras, with the same orange crown, but a touch hardier and more available. A great alternative if you want the look without the sensitivity premium. Same care: sand and a group.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras duplicareus closely resembles adolfoi but is generally hardier and more affordable. Standard cory care applies: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner. Distinguish by slightly broader dorsal band.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as chironomid larvae (bloodworm), Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to surv

reproduction.comments: Can be bred in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras species. Use a ratio of 2 males per female if possible, and when the females are visibly full of eggs perform a large (50-70%) water change with cooler water, and increase oxygenation and flow in the tank. Repeat this daily until the fish spawn. Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, but it’s recommended to provide alternatives in the form of fine-leaved vegetation or fine spawning mops. Once spawning is complete either adults

vibeLine.casual: The patterned cory that swims midwater more than expected.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras elegans. Semi-midwater cory; active swimmer for the genus.

flavorText.casual: More active and more likely to leave the bottom than typical corys, with an attractive spotted pattern. They mix bottom foraging with midwater cruising. Hardy and easy in a standard community group.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras elegans is a relatively active cory that spends more time in midwater than most congeners. Standard care: groups of six-plus over smooth sand, soft to neutral water. An egg-depositing spawner.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores, and most will accept sinking dried foods as well as small live and frozen varieties such as chironomid larvae (bloodworm), Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to surv

Use a ratio of two or more males per female if possible, and when the females are visibly full of eggs perform a large (50-70%) water change with soft, cool water, and increase oxygenation and flow in the tank. Repeat this daily until the fish spawn. Eggs may be deposited on the aquarium glass, among fine-leaved vegetation or within sunken spawning mops, with the latter particularly recommended since they facilitate easy removal o

vibeLine.casual: The tiny cory that hovers midwater like a miniature.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras habrosus. Nano cory; one of three true dwarf species.

flavorText.casual: One of the tiniest corys, barely bigger than a thumbnail. Like the pygmy, it often hovers off the bottom in midwater. Peaceful and adorable in a group in a nano tank with gentle tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras habrosus is one of three recognized dwarf corys (with pygmaeus and hastatus). Often midwater-hovering rather than strictly benthic. Keep ten or more in a planted nano tank; too small for standard community fish.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores, and most will accept sinking dried foods as well as small live and frozen varieties such as chironomid larvae (bloodworm), Tubifex , etc. C. hastatus is slightly different in that it is partially adapted to forage on pelagic zooplankton (see ‘Notes’) meaning a

Use a ratio of two or more males per female if possible, and when the females are visibly full of eggs perform a large (50-70%) water change with soft, cool water, and increase oxygenation and flow in the tank. Repeat this daily until the fish spawn. Eggs may be deposited on the aquarium glass, among fine-leaved vegetation or within sunken spawning mops, with the latter particularly recommended since they facilitate easy removal o

vibeLine.casual: The tiniest cory that schools like a tetra.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras hastatus. True nano cory; midwater schooler, needs large groups.

flavorText.casual: The smallest of all corys, it spends most of its time schooling in midwater rather than sitting on the bottom. Adorable in a big group in a nano setup. Keep them with equally peaceful, tiny fish.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras hastatus is the smallest cory species and a predominantly midwater schooler, unlike most benthic corydoras. Keep fifteen or more for proper schooling confidence. Ideal nano species with dwarf shrimp.

diet.fooditems: Will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as bloodworm , brine shrimp and chopped earthworm. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in the best condition.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Can be bred in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras species . Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a
reproduction.comments: Can be bred in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras species . Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bottom, sand or fine gravel substrate . Use air-powered sponge or box- type filtration as fry won’t be sucked into these and provide some clumps of vegetation such as java moss. A temperature of around 75°F and a pH of 7 should be fine. It’s always better to have a higher ratio of males to females when breeding corys and 2 males per female is

vibeLine.casual: The rare little cory from Peru's Loreto region.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras loretoensis. Uncommon Peruvian cory; standard care, limited availability.

flavorText.casual: A less common cory with subtle markings from the Peruvian Amazon. Care is straightforward — sand, a group, sinking food — but finding them in the trade can be the real challenge.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras loretoensis is an uncommon species from the Loreto region of Peru. Standard cory husbandry applies: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; limited captive-bred availability.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as chironomid larvae (bloodworm), Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to surv

Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, but it is recommended to provide alternatives in the form of fine-leaved vegetation or fine spawning mops. Once spawning is complete either adults o

vibeLine.casual: The pale cory with a bold diagonal black stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras melini. Distinctive diagonal-striped cory; standard care, peaceful.

flavorText.casual: A pale body with a striking dark stripe running diagonally from the dorsal down to the tail. It's a distinctive look that makes them stand out in a cory group. Hardy and peaceful as always.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras melini is a peaceful cory distinguished by a bold diagonal band from dorsal to caudal. Standard care: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner in soft to neutral water.

diet.fooditems: Corydoras spp. are foraging omnivores and will accept most sinking dried foods, as well as small live and frozen varieties such as chironomid larvae (bloodworm), Tubifex , etc. Feeding a varied diet will ensure the fish are in optimum condition. Under no circumstances should they be expected to surv

When the females are visibly full of eggs perform a large (50-70%) water change with cooler water, and increase oxygenation and flow in the tank. Repeat this daily until the fish spawn. Eggs are normally deposited on the aquarium glass, but it is recommended to provide alternatives in the form of fine-leaved vegetation or fine spawning mops. Once spawning is complete either adults o

vibeLine.casual: The cory with a raccoon mask across its eyes.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras metae. Peaceful cory; named for the dark eye band.

flavorText.casual: Aptly named for the dark band running across its eyes like a little bandit mask. Hardy, peaceful, and easy to keep in a group. One of the more distinctive-looking corys thanks to that bold face marking.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras metae is a hardy, peaceful cory distinguished by a prominent dark eye-band. Standard cory care: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; triggered by cool water changes.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The warm-toned cory with a coppery orange glow.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras rabauti. Hardy cory; distinctive rusty-orange coloration over dark base.

flavorText.casual: Named for its warm orange-brown tones, like polished copper armor. Hardy, peaceful, and easy to keep in a standard cory group. One of the more colorful species without needing anything special.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras rabauti has a distinctive rusty-orange hue over a dark base, unlike most silver-brown congeners. Standard cory care: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; hardy and undemanding.

diet.fooditems: Omnivorous and easy to feed. Use a good quality sinking pellet or tablet as the staple diet. Supplement this with live and frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia , bloodworm and similar.

reproduction.comments: Can be spawned in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras , although it’s apparently tricky to induce in this species . Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bottom, sand or fine gravel substrate . Use air-powered sponge or box- type filtration as fry won’t be sucked into these and provide some clumps of vegetation such as java moss. A temperature of around 75°F and a pH of 6.5 should be fine. Filtering the water through peat is useful, as is

vibeLine.casual: The boldly spotted cory with a strong dorsal stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras schwartzi. Hardy community cory; bold markings, standard group care.

flavorText.casual: A handsome cory with prominent dark spots and a strong stripe along the back. Hardy and easy, they fit right into any peaceful bottom crew. Sand, friends, sinking food — the usual cory recipe.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras schwartzi is a hardy, attractively marked cory with bold dorsal striping and lateral spots. Standard care: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; occasionally available as wild-caught.

diet.fooditems: Omnivorous and easy to feed. Use a good quality sinking pellet or tablet as the staple diet. Supplement this with live and frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia , bloodworm and similar.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Can be spawned in a similar fashion to other Corydoras . Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bott
reproduction.comments: Can be spawned in a similar fashion to other Corydoras . Set up the breeding tank (18″ x 12″ x 12″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bottom, sand or fine gravel substrate . Use air-powered sponge or box- type filtration as fry won’t be sucked into these and provide some clumps of vegetation such as java moss. A temperature of around 75°F and a pH of 6.5 should be fine. Filtering the water through peat is useful, as is the use of RO water. It’s always better to have a higher ratio of

vibeLine.casual: The understated cory that looks a bit like paleatus.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras similis. Subtle, hardy cory; resembles paleatus, standard group care.

flavorText.casual: Similar in build and pattern to the common peppered cory, with slight differences in spotting. Hardy and peaceful — keep a group on sand and they'll do what corys do best: bumble and forage.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras similis is a hardy, unassuming cory superficially similar to paleatus. Standard care applies: groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; undemanding and peaceful.

diet.fooditems: An obligate predator which probably feeds on smaller fishes and invertebrates in nature, but in most cases adapts well to dead alternatives in captivity. Some specimens may accept dried foods though these should never form the staple diet. Young fish can be offered chironomid larvae (bloodworm), sma

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: There are very few reports of this fish spawning in aquaria. The chances of obtaining a suitable pair should be higher if you buy a group of young fish, allowing these to pair off naturally. The spawning tank should be large and contain lots of floating plants. The tank should have the tightest-fitting cover you can find, as the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air. Without this, the development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. Apparently the pair embrace in the usual anabantoid

vibeLine.casual: Spotted ambush predator disguised as a dead leaf among plants.
vibeLine.pro: Ctenopoma acutirostre. African anabantoid; leaf-mimicking ambush predator, consumes fish under 4cm.

flavorText.casual: Hangs motionless among plants and driftwood, looking exactly like a spotted dead leaf — then strikes at anything small enough to fit in that surprisingly large mouth. Eats small fish, shrimp, and anything bite-sized. Keep only with tankmates too large to swallow. Often refuses dry food initially.
flavorText.pro: Ctenopoma acutirostre is an African climbing perch employing leaf-mimicry ambush predation — remains motionless among vegetation until prey (fish <4 cm, invertebrates) approaches within strike range. Possesses labyrinth organ for atmospheric respiration. 23–28°C, pH 6–7.5. Feed live/frozen foods; dry food acceptance variable. House only with species exceeding its gape size. Nocturnal activity peak; provide dim lighting and dense cover.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified shellfish); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Open-water schooling cichlid from Tanganyika — needs depth, not width.
vibeLine.pro: Cyprichromis leptosoma. Pelagic Tanganyikan cichlid; mouthbrooder, schools in open water column.

flavorText.casual: Unlike most Tanganyikan cichlids that hug rocks, these school in open water like sardines — midwater to surface. Needs a tall tank more than a long one. Maternal mouthbrooder with spectacular male colour variation between populations. Keep a group of twelve or more for natural behaviour.
flavorText.pro: Cyprichromis leptosoma is a pelagic Tanganyikan cichlid that schools in the open water column, atypical for the lake's rock-dominated fauna. Maternal mouthbrooder. Requires tall tanks (50+ cm height) and groups of 12+. pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Numerous geographic colour morphs exist — maintain population purity. Males display in open water rather than from territory. Minimal aggression compared to substrate-holding Tanganyikans.

diet.fooditems: Almost certain to prey chiefly on insects and their larvae in nature. In the aquarium it’s a largely unfussy feeder and will accept most foods. A good quality dried product can be used as the staple diet but this should be supplemented with regular meals of small live and frozen fare such as bloodwo

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg-scattering spawner that exhibits no parental care. That is to say when the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it is possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without human intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but one or more smaller, say 10-15 litre, contai

vibeLine.casual: Tiny danio packed with orange, green, and gold bars.
vibeLine.pro: Danio choprae. Compact, colourful danio; orange-green barring, easy care.

flavorText.casual: One of the most colourful danios for its size — alternating orange and greenish bars along the body. Active, hardy, and easy to keep in a group. A strong pick for anyone who wants danio energy with more colour punch.
flavorText.pro: Danio choprae is a small danio (3 cm) displaying vivid orange and iridescent green-blue barring. Hardy and undemanding across a range of soft to neutral water. Egg-scattering spawner; breeds readily in conditioned groups. Keep eight-plus for natural behaviour.

diet.fooditems: Likely to feed on small invertebrates, algae and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should not be fed these exclusively, and as it rarely rises to the surface there’s little point in offering floating products. Daily meals of small live and

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids it’s an egg -scattering spawner that does not exhibit parental care . If the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it’s possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but one or more smaller, perhaps 10-15 litre, containers should also be set

vibeLine.casual: Tiny banded danio from Lake Inle that prefers cooler water.
vibeLine.pro: Danio erythromicron. Lake Inle endemic; cooler, harder water than typical danios.

flavorText.casual: A miniature danio with vertical bands and subtle orange fins, native to Myanmar's Lake Inle. Prefers cooler temps (20–24°C) and slightly harder, more alkaline water than your typical tropical nano fish. Shy but rewarding in a dedicated setup.
flavorText.pro: Danio erythromicron is a Lake Inle endemic requiring cooler water (20–24°C) and moderately hard, neutral to slightly alkaline conditions. Keep in groups of ten-plus with cover. Egg-scattering spawner; small clutches deposited among plants. Not suited to warm, soft-water community tanks.

diet.fooditems: Almost certain to prey chiefly on insects and their larvae in nature. In the aquarium it’s a largely unfussy feeder and will accept most foods. A good quality dried product can be used as the staple diet but this should be supplemented with regular meals of small live and frozen fare such as bloodwo

reproduction.comments: Like most small cyprinids this species is an egg-scattering spawner that exhibits no parental care. That is to say when the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it is possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but one or more small, say 30-40 litre, containers sho

vibeLine.casual: Fast little danio with bright orange edges on every fin.
vibeLine.pro: Danio kyathit. Active small danio; orange finnage, standard danio husbandry.

flavorText.casual: A compact danio whose orange-trimmed fins give it a warmth most danios lack. Same bullet-speed energy as its relatives but in a colourful little package. Hardy, easy, and fun to watch in a school.
flavorText.pro: Danio kyathit is a small danio (3–4 cm) distinguished by orange fin margins. Spotted and striped morphs exist in trade. Standard danio care — tolerates a range of conditions, egg-scattering spawner, keep in schools of eight-plus. Active swimmer requiring adequate tank length.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified utaka)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Named for biting eyes off other fish — not a metaphor.
vibeLine.pro: Dimidiochromis compressiceps. Malawi piscivore; ocular predation strategy targeting eyes of heterospecifics.

flavorText.casual: The name is literal. This fish targets and bites the eyes of other fish to disable them before consuming them. Laterally compressed for speed, grows large, and absolutely cannot be housed with anything you value. Large Malawi predator community only — with fish robust enough to avoid becoming victims.
flavorText.pro: Dimidiochromis compressiceps is a large Malawi haplochromine (25+ cm) documented to employ ocular predation — targeting eyes of other fish to disable prey before consumption. Laterally compressed body enables rapid acceleration. Maternal mouthbrooder. pH 7.8–8.6, 23–28°C. Requires 400L+ with robust Malawi predator tankmates. Do not house with small-eyed or slow species. One of the lake's most specialized predation strategies.

diet.fooditems: Aufwuchs grazer and detritivore — rasps algae and biofilm from surfaces. In aquaria: algae wafers, blanched vegetables, sinking spirulina. Driftwood presence beneficial.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Thin as a stick, fragile as one too — pristine water only.
vibeLine.pro: Farlowella acus. Extremely elongate loricariid; fragile, demands pristine stable water quality.

flavorText.casual: Looks exactly like a thin twig clinging to driftwood — remarkable camouflage. But that delicate build means delicate care. Needs pristine, stable water with gentle flow and constant biofilm availability. Not forgiving of neglected maintenance or parameter swings.
flavorText.pro: Farlowella acus is an extremely elongate loricariid (15–20 cm) requiring stable, high-quality water with gentle flow. Feeds exclusively on biofilm and soft algae — supplement in clean tanks. Sensitive to ammonia, nitrite, and parameter fluctuations. Male guards eggs on flat surfaces. Starvation is the primary cause of captive mortality; ensure constant grazing surfaces.

diet.fooditems: others (unidentified terrestrial insects); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Bigger hatchetfish, same surface life, same escape artist tendencies.
vibeLine.pro: Gasteropelecus sternicla. Larger hatchetfish; surface-obligate, requires sealed tank tops.

flavorText.casual: A larger relative of the marbled hatchetfish with the same deep keel-shaped body and surface-only lifestyle. They will jump through any gap in a lid, so seal everything. Feed floating foods — they rarely venture below the top centimetre.
flavorText.pro: Gasteropelecus sternicla is a larger hatchetfish (6–7 cm) with the characteristic deep-keeled thorax for aerial locomotion. Obligate surface dweller; feed floating insects and flake. Tightly sealed lids essential. Egg-scattering spawner; breeding uncommon in aquaria. Tolerates slightly harder water than Carnegiella but still prefers soft, acidic conditions.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified); plants (unidentified algae); zoobenthos (unidentified dipterans, unidentified plecopterans)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Subtropical sand-sifter that actually handles cooler water just fine.
vibeLine.pro: Geophagus brasiliensis. Subtropical eartheater; tolerates 20–26°C, sifts substrate, southernmost Geophagus species.

flavorText.casual: One of the few eartheaters that doesn't need tropical temperatures — it comes from southern Brazil and handles down to 20°C. Sifts sand constantly, so give it fine substrate and skip the gravel. Hardy, not overly aggressive, and pairs up reliably for breeding.
flavorText.pro: Geophagus brasiliensis is the southernmost representative of the genus, tolerating subtropical temperatures (20–26°C). Substrate-sifter requiring fine sand; gravel damages buccal structures. Biparental substrate spawner. Hardy across pH 6.5–7.5. Reaches 20+ cm — needs adequate space despite moderate temperament. Suitable for temperate community tanks with robust tankmates. Sand-sifting behaviour disrupts planted layouts.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified detritus)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Mouthbrooding eartheater — sifts sand, then carries fry in its mouth.
vibeLine.pro: Geophagus steindachneri. Mouthbrooding eartheater; unusual within genus for maternal oral incubation.

flavorText.casual: Most eartheaters are substrate spawners, but this one picks up the eggs and broods them in its mouth — unusual for the group. Males develop a red nuchal hump. Constant sand-sifting behaviour means fine substrate is mandatory. Social fish that does best in groups of five or more.
flavorText.pro: Geophagus steindachneri is notable within the genus for maternal mouthbrooding rather than substrate spawning. Males develop a red nuchal hump with maturity. Obligate sand-sifter requiring fine substrate to prevent buccal damage. Maintain in groups (5+) in spacious tanks. pH 6.5–7.5, 24–30°C. Social hierarchy establishes within groups; adequate space reduces aggression. Reaches 15–18 cm.

diet.fooditems: plants (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: One of Earth's smallest vertebrates — and it's actually a livebearer.
vibeLine.pro: Heterandria formosa. Among the smallest vertebrates; poeciliid livebearer despite the common name.

flavorText.casual: Despite the name, this isn't a killifish at all — it's a livebearer in the guppy family. And one of the tiniest vertebrates on the planet. Females produce a few fry at a time over days rather than dropping a whole brood at once. Needs calm, planted tanks where it won't be outcompeted for food.
flavorText.pro: Heterandria formosa is a poeciliid livebearer (not a cyprinodont) and among the smallest known vertebrates — females reach ~3.5 cm, males ~2 cm. Exhibits superfetation: multiple broods at different developmental stages, delivering 1–3 fry over consecutive days. Requires calm, densely planted setups with micro-foods. Neutral to slightly alkaline water (pH 7–8, 20–26°C). Easily lost with boisterous tankmates.

diet.fooditems: No special requirements. It will accept most prepared foods, but should also be offered regular meals of small live and frozen stuff such as brine shrimp , Daphnia and similar. A varied diet will help to ensure the best colour and devlopment.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Has been spawned regularly in the hobby. You’ll need to set up a separate tank if you want to raise decent numbers of fry . Something around 18″ x 10″ x 10″ in size is fine, and you could even get away with something a little smaller. This should be very dimly lit and contain clumps of fine-leaved plants such as java moss or spawning mops, to give the fish somewhere to deposit their eggs. Alternatively, you could cover the base of the tank with some kind of mesh. This should be of a large enough

vibeLine.casual: The compact purple-blue tetra that punches above its size.
vibeLine.pro: Inpaichthys kerri. Hardy, peaceful tetra; males show intense purple-blue.

flavorText.casual: Small but vivid, with males showing a deep purple-blue that's hard to beat for the size. Hardy, peaceful, and easy in a planted group. An underrated gem that deserves more attention in the hobby.
flavorText.pro: Inpaichthys kerri is a hardy, peaceful tetra whose males develop intense purple-blue coloration. Keep eight or more for confident shoaling. An egg-scatterer among fine-leaved plants in soft, slightly acidic water.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms, unspecified insects); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: North American native that actually eats hair algae — subtropical keeper.
vibeLine.pro: Jordanella floridae. Subtropical North American cyprinodont; grazes filamentous algae, tolerates cooler temperatures.

flavorText.casual: One of very few fish that genuinely eats hair algae — and it's a cold-tolerant North American native that doesn't need a heater in most homes. Males are colourful with an iridescent spot pattern. Can be nippy with long-finned tankmates, so pick companions carefully.
flavorText.pro: Jordanella floridae is a subtropical cyprinodont from Florida, notable for consuming filamentous algae — uncommon among ornamental fish. Tolerates 18–24°C; no heater required in temperate homes. Males territorial during spawning; egg-depositor with paternal guarding. Can nip slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. Hardy across a wide pH range (6.5–7.8). Useful biological algae control.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified algae); zoobenthos (unspecified sponges)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Larger checkerboard Julie — same rock loyalty, more presence.
vibeLine.pro: Julidochromis marlieri. Larger Julidochromis; bold checkerboard pattern, monogamous, rock-dwelling cave-spawner.

flavorText.casual: The biggest commonly available Julie, with a bold black-and-white checkerboard pattern that makes it easy to identify. Same lifestyle — permanent pair bond, cave spawning, rock-hugging orientation. More territorial than smaller Julies due to size, so give pairs adequate rockwork separation.
flavorText.pro: Julidochromis marlieri is the largest commonly available Julidochromis (13–15 cm), distinguished by a bold checkerboard pattern. Monogamous cave-spawner with biparental care. More aggressively territorial than smaller congeners due to body size. Requires substantial rockwork with multiple caves and crevices. pH 8–9, 23–27°C. House only one pair per territory; multiple pairs need large tanks with visual barriers.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Smaller Julie with golden bars — same rock-hugging loyalty.
vibeLine.pro: Julidochromis ornatus. Smaller Tanganyikan Julidochromis; golden-barred pattern, monogamous cave-spawner.

flavorText.casual: Same deal as the other Julies — permanent pair bond, lives in rock crevices, defends a small territory, ignores everyone else. Smaller than J. regani with a distinctive golden-barred pattern. Good for Tanganyikan community tanks where rockwork provides enough separate territories.
flavorText.pro: Julidochromis ornatus is a smaller Julidochromis species (7–8 cm) distinguished by golden-yellow longitudinal barring. Monogamous cave-spawner with biparental care. Inhabits rocky littoral zones of Lake Tanganyika. pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Provide stacked rockwork with narrow interstices. Less aggressive than larger congeners but still territorial around the breeding cave. Compatible with shell-dwellers and open-water Cyprichromis.

diet.fooditems: Live and frozen foods should comprise a large proportion of the diet. Dried foods can be fed, but less often. Some vegetable matter, such as spirulina or blanched spinach should also be fed. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Easy but patience is required. It’s best to buy a group of young fish and allow them to pair off naturally. Once a pair forms (this may take a year or more) the other fish should be removed as the pair will remain together for life. Unfortunately buying an adult male and female will not guarantee a pair and often results in the female being harassed to death. The aquarium itself should be at least 48″ in length and set up as suggested above. The pH should be around 8.2-9.0 and the temperature 77

vibeLine.casual: Rock-dwelling pair that bonds for life in Tanganyika crevices.
vibeLine.pro: Julidochromis regani. Tanganyikan rock-dweller; permanent pair bonds, cave-spawning biparental care.

flavorText.casual: Finds a crevice, finds a mate, and that's it — pair bond locked in for life. Hugs rocks upside down, sideways, any orientation. Territorial around the chosen cave but ignores fish that stay out of its immediate space. Needs stacked rockwork with tight gaps.
flavorText.pro: Julidochromis regani is a Tanganyikan rock-dwelling cichlid forming permanent monogamous pair bonds. Cave-spawner with biparental care; both parents defend the breeding site. Inhabits rocky habitats, often orienting body along rock surfaces regardless of angle. pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Provide complex rockwork with narrow crevices. Territorial only within immediate cave vicinity. Multiple geographic colour forms exist.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Smallest Julie with a black mask — perfect for nano Tanganyika.
vibeLine.pro: Julidochromis transcriptus. Smallest Julidochromis; black facial mask, suited to nano Tanganyikan setups.

flavorText.casual: The runt of the Julie family — barely seven centimetres and easily identified by the black mask across its face. Same rock-dwelling, pair-bonding behaviour as its larger relatives but in a smaller package. Works in Tanganyikan nano setups where bigger Julies would overwhelm the space.
flavorText.pro: Julidochromis transcriptus is the smallest Julidochromis species (6–7 cm), identified by a distinctive black facial mask pattern. Monogamous cave-spawner with biparental care. Suitable for smaller Tanganyikan biotope tanks (60L+). pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Less territorially aggressive than larger congeners. Compatible with shell-dwellers and other small Tanganyikans. Provide rockwork with appropriately narrow crevices for its body size.

diet.fooditems: Wild fish are probably foragers feeding on algae , organic detritus , small insects, worms, crustaceans, and other zooplankton. In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia , and Artemia , alongside

reproduction.comments: Biparental substrate-spawner.

vibeLine.casual: Small, peaceful dwarf cichlid that works in planted community tanks.
vibeLine.pro: Laetacara curviceps. Peaceful South American dwarf cichlid; community-compatible, biparental open-substrate spawner.

flavorText.casual: One of the most peaceful dwarf cichlids you can find — small, doesn't dig, doesn't wreck plants, and doesn't bully tankmates. Pairs form bonds and share parenting duties. A good choice when you want cichlid behaviour without cichlid aggression in a community setup.
flavorText.pro: Laetacara curviceps is a peaceful dwarf cichlid (6–8 cm) suited to planted community tanks. Biparental open-substrate spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry without excessive territorial aggression. Soft to slightly acidic water preferred (pH 6–7.2, 22–28°C). Does not uproot plants or rearrange substrate. Compatible with tetras, corydoras, and other non-aggressive species.

diet.fooditems: Wild fish are probably foragers feeding on algae, organic detritus, small insects, worms, crustaceans, and other zooplankton. In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia , and Artemia , alongside goo

reproduction.comments: Biparental substrate spawner.

vibeLine.casual: Peaceful dwarf cichlid with a warm red-orange belly patch.
vibeLine.pro: Laetacara dorsigera. Peaceful dwarf cichlid; soft water, compatible with community tankmates.

flavorText.casual: About as mellow as dwarf cichlids get — pairs hold a small territory but don't terrorise the neighbourhood. Males show a red-orange flush across the belly and lower body. Works well in planted community tanks with tetras and corydoras. Soft, slightly acidic water brings out the best colour.
flavorText.pro: Laetacara dorsigera is a peaceful dwarf cichlid (5–6 cm) from South American soft-water habitats. Biparental substrate spawner; both parents guard fry with minimal disruption to tankmates. Prefers pH 6–7 and 22–28°C. Red-orange ventral coloration intensifies during breeding. Compatible with small tetras, corydoras, and other non-aggressive species. One of the more community-safe cichlid options.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified micro-invertebrates); zooplankton (unspecified micro-invertebrates)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Tiny but furious — guards its shell against fish ten times bigger.
vibeLine.pro: Lamprologus ocellatus. Tanganyikan shell-dweller; males aggressively territorial, disproportionate defensive behaviour for size.

flavorText.casual: Don't let the size fool you — males will charge at fish many times their size if they approach the shell. Buries shells in sand until only the opening shows, then defends that entrance with absurd intensity. One of the most entertaining fish to watch in a small Tanganyikan setup.
flavorText.pro: Lamprologus ocellatus is a Tanganyikan shell-dweller exhibiting disproportionately aggressive territorial defense — males attack intruders far exceeding their own body size. Buries shells in sand with only the aperture exposed. Harem-forming; one male maintains multiple shells with individual females. pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Provide sand deep enough for shell burial (5+ cm). Males reach 5–6 cm; females smaller. Gold and blue morphs available in trade.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified detritus)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Presumably a seasonal spawner in nature but hasn’t been bred in captivity as far as we know although purported courtship behaviour has been observed.
reproduction.comments: Presumably a seasonal spawner in nature but hasn’t been bred in captivity as far as we know although purported courtship behaviour has been observed.

vibeLine.casual: Tiny burrowing loach with actual spines tucked below its eyes.
vibeLine.pro: Lepidocephalichthys thermalis. Small burrowing cobitid; suborbital spine, sand substrate essential for welfare.

flavorText.casual: A small, sand-diving loach that spends most of its time half-buried. The "spiny" name comes from a small spine beneath each eye — it's a defence thing, not a danger to you. Keep in groups with fine sand and they'll sift happily.
flavorText.pro: Lepidocephalichthys thermalis is a small cobitid (5–6 cm) requiring fine sand substrate for its burrowing behaviour. The suborbital spine is a diagnostic feature. Tolerates a broad temperature range (20–26°C). Egg-scatterer; rarely bred in captivity. Keep in groups with gentle flow and abundant cover.

diet.fooditems: Omnivorous and will accept most foods offered but the diet should contain some vegetable matter. This can either be fresh form or some kind of vegetable flake. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Very easy if soft, acidic water conditions are avoided. It reproduces in standard livebearer fashion. As with related species , the males can be somewhat incessant in their pursuit of females. Several females should be kept to every male in order to dissipate this. Areas of dense planting for the females to seek refuge in will also help. Gestation takes around 6-8 weeks and broods of up to 50 fry are not uncommon. The adult fish will predate on the young, so the best method of raising them is to

vibeLine.casual: Males develop a distinctive arched back — a livebearer with character.
vibeLine.pro: Limia nigrofasciata. Haitian livebearer; males develop dorsal hump with maturity, hardy.

flavorText.casual: The standout feature: mature males develop a pronounced arched back that makes them look nothing like typical livebearers. From Haiti, easy to keep in hard, alkaline water, and breeds without much encouragement. Under-appreciated in the hobby.
flavorText.pro: Limia nigrofasciata is a Haitian poeciliid in which mature males develop a distinctive dorsal kyphosis. Hardy in hard, alkaline conditions (pH 7–8). Prolific livebearer; female-biased groups reduce male-driven stress. Omnivorous with algae-grazing tendencies. An interesting species for livebearer enthusiasts seeking unusual morphology beyond standard poeciliids.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore feeding on insects, small invertebrates, algae, and plant matter. In aquaria: quality flake food, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, live foods.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Big, colourful New Guinea rainbow — fills a large planted tank.
vibeLine.pro: Melanotaenia goldiei. Large melanotaeniid from New Guinea; robust, active, group-oriented.

flavorText.casual: One of the bigger rainbowfish — reaching 12–15 cm with deep body shape and plenty of colour in mature males. Needs space to school properly and shows best in groups of six-plus. Hardy and undemanding once you give it room to move.
flavorText.pro: Melanotaenia goldiei is a large melanotaeniid (12–15 cm) from New Guinea's Goldie River drainage. Develops coloration progressively over months. Egg-scatterer among fine-leaved plants; breeds readily in conditioned groups. Requires neutral to slightly alkaline water (pH 7–8) and ample swimming space. Males intensify colour during morning sparring.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore — insects, small invertebrates, algae in the wild. In aquaria: varied diet of flakes, frozen foods (brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworm), and live foods.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Orange-and-black New Guinea rainbow that deepens with age.
vibeLine.pro: Melanotaenia parkinsoni. New Guinean melanotaeniid; orange-black patterning intensifies progressively with maturity.

flavorText.casual: Males develop bold orange patches breaking up a dark body — one of the more contrasty rainbowfish. Colour intensifies steadily as the fish matures over months. Hardy, active, and straightforward to keep in neutral to alkaline water with a good-sized group.
flavorText.pro: Melanotaenia parkinsoni is a New Guinean melanotaeniid reaching 10–12 cm, males developing distinctive orange-on-black patterning that intensifies with maturity. Egg-scatterer among fine vegetation; breeds readily when conditioned. Neutral to slightly alkaline water (pH 7–8, 24–28°C). Multiple locality colour forms exist; maintain purity where provenance is known.

diet.fooditems: others (unspecified ants)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg depositor — lays eggs in concealed locations. Nocturnal breeder. Rarely bred in captivity
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Small nocturnal catfish in yellow-black stripes — eats tiny tankmates.
vibeLine.pro: Microglanis iheringi. Small nocturnal predator; yellow-black banding, will consume nano fish.

flavorText.casual: Those cute yellow-and-black stripes hide a little predator. Anything small enough to fit in its surprisingly wide mouth is food — so don't mix with nano fish or fry. Otherwise peaceful and easy, just nocturnal and secretive.
flavorText.pro: Microglanis iheringi is a small (6–8 cm) pseudopimelodid catfish with bold yellow-black banding. Nocturnal predator; will consume fish and invertebrates small enough to ingest. Provide caves and driftwood retreats. Hardy across a range of neutral water conditions. Egg-scatterer; breeding uncommon in captivity. Feed meaty sinking foods after dark.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified, unidentified insect larvae)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Hardy little dwarf cichlid — one of the easier SA options.
vibeLine.pro: Nannacara anomala. Hardy nannacarine cichlid; forgiving of varied water chemistry, readily breeds.

flavorText.casual: If you want a South American dwarf cichlid without the soft-water anxiety, this is a solid pick. Hardy across a range of conditions, males develop nice green-gold colouration, and females turn into fierce little guardians when brooding. A genuinely approachable dwarf cichlid.
flavorText.pro: Nannacara anomala is a robust nannacarine dwarf cichlid tolerant of pH 6–7.5 and moderate hardness — more forgiving than most Apistogramma. Cave-brooder; females exhibit dramatic melanistic pattern shift during brood care and defend fry aggressively. Males reach 6–8 cm. Breeds readily in pairs with adequate cave sites. Good entry species for SA dwarf cichlid keeping.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The slim, horizontal hover-fish with a golden stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Nannostomus beckfordi. Hardy pencilfish; surface-oriented, needs a calm group.

flavorText.casual: Pencilfish float horizontally in the upper water like living lines of gold and red. They're peaceful and undemanding but prefer calm conditions and a group. A lovely, underappreciated nano-community fish.
flavorText.pro: Nannostomus beckfordi is one of the hardiest pencilfish, hovering in the upper water column. Keep six or more in a calm, planted tank. Males display to each other with intensified color bands. An egg-scatterer among fine plants.

diet.fooditems: zooplankton (unspecified plankton)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Lives inside an empty snail shell — that's literally home.
vibeLine.pro: Neolamprologus brevis. Tanganyikan shell-dweller; inhabits empty Neothauma shells, pair-forming species.

flavorText.casual: Its entire existence revolves around a single empty snail shell on the lake floor — that's the nest, the shelter, and the territory. Pairs bond and defend their shell fiercely despite being tiny. Perfect for nano Tanganyikan setups with sand and scattered shells.
flavorText.pro: Neolamprologus brevis is a Tanganyikan shell-dwelling cichlid that occupies empty Neothauma tanganyicense shells on sandy substrates. Pair-forming; both sexes defend the shell territory. Provide multiple shells per pair on fine sand. pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Suitable for species-specific nano setups (40L+). Minimal aggression outside immediate shell vicinity. Breeding occurs within the shell; fry remain near parental territory.

diet.fooditems: zooplankton (unspecified plankton)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Quite easy. Shell brooder. It may breed in the community tank but if you want to raise a full brood of these, a separate tank should be used. Set it u
reproduction.comments: Quite easy. Shell brooder. It may breed in the community tank but if you want to raise a full brood of these, a separate tank should be used. Set it up as suggested above. Provide plenty of snail shells, as the females will lay their eggs in these. Escargot shells are a good choice and can be obtained from most decent delicatessens. Water should be hard and alkaline with a pH of around 8.0-8.5 and a temperature of 77-80°F. Keep several females per male and spacing the shells out a little. This h

vibeLine.casual: World's smallest cichlid — builds whole colonies around piles of shells.
vibeLine.pro: Neolamprologus multifasciatus. World's smallest cichlid; colonial shell-dweller forming dense breeding aggregations.

flavorText.casual: The smallest cichlid on Earth — males barely reach 4 cm, females even less. Unlike pair-forming shell dwellers, these form colonies where dozens of individuals share a shell bed. Watching them bury and rearrange shells, defend micro-territories, and raise fry communally is endlessly entertaining. Sand and shells — that's all you need.
flavorText.pro: Neolamprologus multifasciatus is the world's smallest cichlid (males ~4 cm, females ~2.5 cm), forming colonial aggregations in Neothauma shell beds. Unlike pair-forming Neolamprologus, colonies consist of multiple breeding units sharing a shell field. Provide deep sand and abundant shells (2–3 per individual minimum). pH 8–9, 23–27°C. Self-sustaining colonies establish readily; population management may become necessary. Fascinating model for cichlid social behaviour.

diet.fooditems: Carnivore feeding on small invertebrates, insect larvae, and crustaceans. In aquaria: live/frozen foods essential — brine shrimp, daphnia, mosquito larvae, grindal worms.
