reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Another annual killifish — spectacular colours, measured in months not years.
vibeLine.pro: Nothobranchius guentheri. Annual killifish; ephemeral lifecycle, eggs require dry-incubation diapause period.

flavorText.casual: Same deal as the Bluefin Notho — seasonal pools, adults that live fast and die when things dry up, and eggs that wait in the mud for rain. Males are spectacularly coloured blue-and-red. You get months of peak display, then it's over. Keep breeding stock going to maintain the line.
flavorText.pro: Nothobranchius guentheri is an annual killifish from East African seasonal pools. Adult lifespan 6–12 months; eggs undergo obligate diapause in peat or coco fibre substrate. Harvest eggs, store semi-dry at room temperature for 3–6 months before wetting to trigger hatching. Males develop vivid blue body with red caudal. Multiple populations available — maintain locality integrity.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Another small oto — same algae work, same fragile start.
vibeLine.pro: Otocinclus affinis. Standard otocinclus husbandry; biofilm-dependent, sensitive post-import period applies.

flavorText.casual: Very similar in care and behaviour to other otos. Tiny, constantly grazing, and sensitive during the first weeks home. Once past that adjustment period they're hardy little algae workers. Groups of six-plus in a mature planted tank.
flavorText.pro: Otocinclus affinis follows standard genus husbandry — small loricariid dependent on biofilm, high post-import mortality risk from starvation during supply chain. Establish in mature tanks with visible algae and biofilm. Groups of six-plus. Egg-depositor; breeding possible in well-maintained planted tanks with soft, slightly acidic water.

diet.fooditems: It feeds primarily on algae and other microrganisms in nature. In captivity the bulk of the diet should be composed of vegetable matter in both fresh (courgette and cucumber slices, blanched spinach etc.) and dried ( algae wafers, spirulina tablets etc.) forms. Although it will also accept small liv

reproduction.comments: Has been achieved, but unfortunately very few details are available. Spawning occurs in a similar style to Corydoras species , with the fish forming the classic ‘T’ position, in which the male grasps the female between his pectoral fin and body and eggs and sperm are deposited. Unlike in cories the eggs are scattered randomly around the tank , usually among plants in the upper areas, or on the tank glass. Various factors appear to be involved in triggering a spawn . Large weekly water changes wi

vibeLine.casual: Tiny algae-grazing catfish that works nonstop on your glass.
vibeLine.pro: Otocinclus macrospilus. Small loricariid algae grazer; fragile at import, stable once established.

flavorText.casual: Another oto species — same small size, same relentless biofilm grazing, same sensitivity in the first few weeks after purchase. Once settled, hardy and effective. Keep in groups of six-plus in a mature, planted tank with algae to graze.
flavorText.pro: Otocinclus macrospilus shares husbandry requirements with O. vittatus — small loricariid (3–4 cm) feeding primarily on biofilm and soft algae. High initial mortality common due to starvation during import chain. Acclimate to established tanks with visible biofilm growth. Egg-depositor on plant leaves; breeding reported in well-maintained setups.

diet.fooditems: Xylivore — primary diet is wood (mandatory driftwood). Also grazes algae and biofilm. In aquaria: multiple pieces of driftwood, supplemented with blanched vegetables, algae wafers.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Cave spawner with paternal care — male guards eggs in large cave or hollow log. Rarely bred due to size
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The big blue-eyed wood-eater with a regal name.
vibeLine.pro: Panaque nigrolineatus. Large wood-eating pleco; driftwood essential, grows slowly.

flavorText.casual: A handsome grey pleco with striking blue eyes and a taste for actual wood. They grow large and slowly, and driftwood is essential to their digestion. A characterful centerpiece for a big tank.
flavorText.pro: Panaque nigrolineatus is one of the true wood-eating plecos and must have driftwood to digest cellulose. Reaches over a foot with a heavy bioload; needs a large tank and strong filtration. Slow-growing and long-lived.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: There exists at least one documented account of this species being bred in aquaria, though not under controlled conditions. A number of partially-grow
reproduction.comments: There exists at least one documented account of this species being bred in aquaria, though not under controlled conditions. A number of partially-grown fry were discovered living among a substrate of large-grade gravel and no spawning or courtship behaviour was observed. The aquarium was around 200 litres in volume with pH 7.8 – 8.0, gH 18 – 22, kH above 20 and temperature 78 – 80 °F/25.6 – 26.7 °C.

vibeLine.casual: All the kuhli wiggles, now in solid dark chocolate.
vibeLine.pro: Pangio oblonga. Uniform dark morph kuhli; identical husbandry to P. semicincta.

flavorText.casual: Same noodle-shaped burrower as the banded kuhli, just in solid dark brown-black instead of stripes. They hide all day and emerge at night to sift through sand. Keep a group and give them plenty of hiding spots to feel safe.
flavorText.pro: Pangio oblonga is the uniform dark morph relative of the banded kuhli loach. Identical care — fine sand substrate, numerous hiding spots, groups of six-plus. Nocturnal benthic forager. Egg-scatterer; breeding uncommon in captivity. Sensitive to medications containing copper.

diet.fooditems: Chiefly a micropredator sifting mouthfuls of substrate through the mouth and gills from which insect larvae , small crustaceans and suchlike are extracted with a proportion of the natural diet also likely to comprise organic detritus and plant material from the gut contents of prey. In the aquarium

reproduction.spawningTrait: Several vague reports exist but none are supported by solid information or photographs. Presumably a seasonal spawner in nature.
reproduction.comments: Several vague reports exist but none are supported by solid information or photographs. Presumably a seasonal spawner in nature.

vibeLine.casual: The banded kuhli noodle most people actually have — classic sand wiggler.
vibeLine.pro: Pangio semicincta. The commonly traded "kuhli loach"; bands don't fully encircle body.

flavorText.casual: This is almost certainly the species you have if you bought "kuhli loaches" from a shop. Bands wrap partway around rather than fully encircling the body. Same care as all kuhlis — sand, hides, groups, patience at feeding time.
flavorText.pro: Pangio semicincta is the species most frequently sold as "kuhli loach," distinguished from P. kuhlii by incomplete ventral banding. Care is standard for the genus — fine sand, groups of six-plus, nocturnal feeding, and numerous retreats. Egg-scatterer; rarely intentionally bred. Copper-sensitive.

diet.fooditems: It will accept most foods offered but live and frozen varieties should form the bulk of the diet. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Relatively easy. Cave spawner. The fish form monogamous pairs and the best way to obtain such a pair is to buy a group of six or more young fish and grow them on, allowing pairing to occur naturally. There are no guarantees that simply buying a male and female fish will result in a compatible pair , and it may result in the death of an unwilling partner. If you do choose to buy a single pair , select the largest male and most colourful female from the dealer’s tank . The breeding tank should be

vibeLine.casual: The colorful West African cave-dweller that breeds beautifully.
vibeLine.pro: Pelvicachromis taeniatus. Colorful dwarf cichlid; cave-brooder with devoted biparental care.

flavorText.casual: More colorful and varied than the common krib, with regional forms showing different patterns. Pairs bond and breed in caves, and both parents fiercely guard the fry. A small tank showpiece with real behavior.
flavorText.pro: Pelvicachromis taeniatus has numerous locality forms with distinct coloration. A cave-brooder with strong biparental care; both parents escort free-swimming fry. Requires soft to neutral water and a well-structured tank.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified crustaceans); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Tiny translucent gold barb — peaceful and nano-sized throughout life.
vibeLine.pro: Pethia gelius. Nano-scale barb; translucent gold, peaceful, stays under 4 cm.

flavorText.casual: A barb you can keep in a nano tank without worry — maxes out around 3 cm and never bothers anyone. Translucent gold with dark blotches. Subtle rather than showy, but charming in a planted nano group.
flavorText.pro: Pethia gelius is a true nano barb (3–4 cm) suitable for small, planted aquaria. Peaceful; lacks the fin-nipping tendency of larger barbs. Egg-scattering spawner; soft, slightly acidic water and fine-leaved plants encourage breeding. Keep eight-plus for natural shoaling.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: A solid, easy-going mid-sized rasbora for any community.
vibeLine.pro: Rasbora einthovenii. Robust medium rasbora; undemanding schooler for general community tanks.

flavorText.casual: Not flashy, but reliable and active. A medium-sized rasbora that schools well, stays peaceful, and doesn't ask for much beyond clean water and a group. A good background fish that fills the mid-level nicely.
flavorText.pro: Rasbora einthovenii reaches approximately 9 cm, making it a medium-bodied schooling cyprinid suitable for general community setups. Tolerates a range of water conditions. Egg-scattering spawner; prolific when conditioned with live foods in soft water. Keep six or more.

diet.fooditems: Probably feeds mostly on invertebrates both aquatic and terrestrial in nature as with similar members of the genus . In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but for it to develop its best colours and condition offer daily meals of live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia and Artemia along with good

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg -scattering, continuous spawner that exhibits no parental care . That is to say when the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it is possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without human intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but one or more long, shallow, say

vibeLine.casual: A larger rasbora with bold dark spots and copper warmth.
vibeLine.pro: Rasbora kalochroma. Larger soft-water rasbora; bold markings, requires acidic conditions.

flavorText.casual: Bigger than most rasboras and more boldly marked, with large dark spots on a pinkish-copper body. Needs softer, more acidic water than the average rasbora — not difficult, just specific. Schools peacefully in a well-sized tank.
flavorText.pro: Rasbora kalochroma is a medium-large rasbora (up to 10 cm) requiring soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5) for health and colour development. Bold dorsal and lateral blotches distinguish it. Egg-scattering spawner in soft water over fine-leaved plants. Keep six-plus in a spacious tank.

diet.fooditems: Like similar species such as R. borapetensis it is probably a micropredator feeding on small insects, worms, crustaceans and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should not be fed these exclusively. Daily meals of small live and frozen fare s

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg -scattering, continuous spawner that exhibits no parental care . That is to say when the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it is possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without human intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but one or more small, say 12″ x 8

vibeLine.casual: A modest rasbora with a vivid red dorsal fin stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Rasbora rubrodorsalis. Small peaceful schooler; red dorsal marking distinguishes from congeners.

flavorText.casual: The red stripe running through the dorsal fin gives it away — otherwise a quiet, mid-tank schooler that doesn't make a fuss. Easy to keep and works in most community setups as long as tankmates aren't too rowdy.
flavorText.pro: Rasbora rubrodorsalis is a small cyprinid (3–4 cm) identified by its red-pigmented dorsal fin. Undemanding; tolerates a range of soft to moderately hard water. Egg-scattering spawner. Keep in groups of eight-plus. Peaceful schooler suitable for general planted community tanks.

diet.fooditems: Feeds on aufwuchs and small aquatic crustaceans in nature. In the aquarium it proves omnivorous and will accept both sinking dried foods, vegetable matter (including blanched spinach, courgette, kale etc.) and live and frozen fare such as bloodworm or daphnia .

reproduction.spawningTrait: If kept in suitable conditions and fed well, the fish will breed quite easily, although the fry can be a little tricky to raise. Try starting with a g
reproduction.comments: If kept in suitable conditions and fed well, the fish will breed quite easily, although the fry can be a little tricky to raise. Try starting with a group of 6 or more juvenile fish to give yourself the best chance of obtaining a pair . Set up the tank as suggested above, using lengths of pvc piping of less than 2″ diameter to provide spawning caves. The water should be just on the acidic side of neutral and soft. Condition the fish on a diet of nutrient-rich greenstuffs, such as kale, spinach e

vibeLine.casual: Slender bottom-dweller — males fan eggs like devoted little housekeepers.
vibeLine.pro: Rineloricaria lanceolata. Slender loricariid; paternal egg-fanning on flat substrate, readily breeds.

flavorText.casual: A thin, flat loricariid that hugs the bottom and grazes on surfaces. Males develop lip bristles and guard egg clutches, fanning them constantly until they hatch. One of the easier loricariids to breed at home. Peaceful and undemanding.
flavorText.pro: Rineloricaria lanceolata is a slender loricariid (10–12 cm) exhibiting paternal care — males fan and guard eggs deposited on flat surfaces (glass, slate, driftwood). Breeds readily in captivity with conditioned pairs. Biofilm and aufwuchs grazer; supplement with sinking wafers. Hardy across soft to neutral water. Peaceful; suitable for planted communities.

diet.fooditems: It will accept dried pellets or flakes, but these should be supplemented with regular feedings of live and frozen foods such as bloodworm and brineshrimp.

reproduction.comments: Not too difficult. Monogamous cave spawner. The pair bond formed by this species is so strong that if one partner in a pair dies, the other may not seek another partner, confining itself to a life of sexual unfulfilment! The fish may breed in a community setup, but is best spawned in a species tank , with only a shoal of Congo tetras or similar to act as dither fish , as if kept alone the fish are somewhat skittish. The aquarium should be set up as described above, with a temperature of 75-80°F

vibeLine.casual: Bottom-hopping river cichlid that barely swims — hops instead 🪨
vibeLine.pro: Steatocranus casuarius. Rheophilic cichlid; reduced swim bladder, benthic hopping locomotion.

flavorText.casual: This fish doesn't swim so much as hop along the bottom — its swim bladder is reduced so it stays planted on rocks in fast-flowing rivers. Males grow a chunky forehead hump that gets bigger with age. Needs strong flow, rocks, and a sand base. Pair-bonding is tight and long-term.
flavorText.pro: Steatocranus casuarius is a rheophilic cichlid from the Congo River rapids with a functionally reduced swim bladder, resulting in characteristic benthic hopping locomotion. Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump with maturity. Cave-spawner forming monogamous pairs. Requires strong flow, rocky substrate, and oxygen-rich water (23–27°C, pH 6.5–7.5). Not a community species — territorial toward conspecifics and similar-sized fish.

diet.fooditems: Aufwuchs grazer and detritivore feeding on algae, biofilm, and decaying plant matter. In aquaria: algae wafers, blanched courgette/spinach, sinking vegetable tabs.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Long, slender algae-grazer — the male guards eggs on flat surfaces.
vibeLine.pro: Sturisomatichthys aureum. Elongate loricariid; biofilm grazer, paternal egg guarding on substrate.

flavorText.casual: Looks like a twig with a sucker mouth — long, thin, and elegant in a planted tank. Males develop bristles and guard egg clutches on flat surfaces like driftwood or glass. A dedicated algae grazer that needs supplemental feeding in clean tanks.
flavorText.pro: Sturisomatichthys aureum is an elongate loricariid reaching 20+ cm, feeding on biofilm and soft algae. Males exhibit paternal care, fanning and guarding eggs deposited on flat surfaces. Requires well-oxygenated water with moderate flow. Supplement with algae wafers and blanched vegetables in tanks with insufficient biofilm. Breeds readily when conditioned.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Large, aggressive loach that demolishes snails and dominates its tankmates.
vibeLine.pro: Syncrossus hymenophysa. Large aggressive botid; effective snail predator, requires robust tankmates.

flavorText.casual: Looks striking with bold tiger-like bars, but this is a big, assertive loach that can reach 25+ cm and bully anything it decides to push around. Great at eliminating snails, terrible at sharing space politely. Plan tankmates carefully.
flavorText.pro: Syncrossus hymenophysa is a large botid (25+ cm) with pronounced interspecific and intraspecific aggression. Highly effective biological snail control. Requires spacious tanks (150 cm+) with numerous line-of-sight breaks. Keep in groups of five-plus to distribute aggression. Egg-scatterer; not bred in home aquaria. Suborbital spine can snag nets.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified, neothauma)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Tricks mouthbrooding cichlids into raising its young — actual fish parasitism.
vibeLine.pro: Synodontis multipunctatus. Obligate brood parasite; sneaks eggs into mouthbrooding cichlid host clutches.

flavorText.casual: One of the most remarkable behaviours in freshwater fish. This catfish spawns alongside mouthbrooding cichlids, sneaking its eggs into the brooding parent's mouth. The cichlid raises cuckoo catfish fry alongside (or instead of) its own. Needs hard, alkaline Tanganyikan water.
flavorText.pro: Synodontis multipunctatus is an obligate brood parasite — the only well-documented example among freshwater fish. Spawns synchronously with mouthbrooding cichlids (Ctenochromis, Tropheus, etc.), inserting eggs that are incubated by the host. Requires Lake Tanganyika parameters (pH 7.8–8.5, hard water). Keep with compatible mouthbrooders to observe natural reproductive behaviour.

diet.fooditems: Likely to be a micropredator feeding on small insects, worms, crustaceans, and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it’s easily-fed but the best condition and colours offer regular meals of small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , Daphnia , and Artemia alongside good quality dried fla

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg -scattering, continuous spawner that exhibits no parental care . That is to say when the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and when a group is maintained alone in a densely-planted, mature aquarium or outdoor container small numbers of fry usually start to appear without further intervention. Should a more controlled approach be required a separate, smaller aquarium can be set up. This should be dimly-lit and the base covered with s

vibeLine.casual: A tiny subtropical minnow — the white cloud's lesser-known cousin.
vibeLine.pro: Tanichthys micagemmae. Subtropical micro-minnow; closely related to T. albonubes, cooler water.

flavorText.casual: Closely related to the white cloud mountain minnow but smaller and with more red in the fins. Prefers cooler water (18–23°C), so skip the heater in most homes. A gentle little nano fish that does well in a group.
flavorText.pro: Tanichthys micagemmae is a subtropical micro-cyprinid (2–3 cm) from Vietnam, requiring cooler temperatures (18–23°C) than typical tropical fish. Care parallels T. albonubes. Egg-scattering spawner; breeds readily in conditioned groups. Keep ten-plus in planted, unheated tanks.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The tiny jewel gourami that also crackles and pops.
vibeLine.pro: Trichopsis pumila. Nano labyrinth fish; audible crackling, bubblenest builder.

flavorText.casual: Like a miniature gem with iridescent blue-green speckles and a personality far bigger than its body. They click and crackle audibly at each other. Perfect for a planted nano tank with gentle company.
flavorText.pro: Trichopsis pumila is a true nano labyrinth fish that crackles audibly during interactions. A bubblenest builder for mature, planted nano tanks. Keep in pairs or small groups with calm, equally small tankmates.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified, unidentified larvae); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The gourami that literally makes audible croaking sounds.
vibeLine.pro: Trichopsis vittata. Small labyrinth fish; makes audible sounds during display.

flavorText.casual: Put your ear close to the tank and you'll actually hear them croak, a surprisingly loud sound for such a small fish. They produce it by vibrating their pectoral fins. Peaceful and easy in a calm, planted tank.
flavorText.pro: Trichopsis vittata produces audible sounds via pectoral-fin vibration during courtship and territorial display. A peaceful bubblenest builder for calm, planted setups. Hardy and undemanding; keep with quiet tankmates.

diet.fooditems: It will accept most foods offered but vegetable matter in the form of spirulina flakes, blanched spinach, nori etc. should form the basis of the diet. This can be supplemented with small feeds of live and frozen varieties. Never feed beefheart or any other animal meat as it interferes with the diges

reproduction.comments: Possible. Maternal mouthbrooder. The tank should be set up as decribed above. We recommend the purchase of 20-30 young fish and allowing them to grow on together, as adults are costly. If you can, try and obtain tank -bred fish, as wild specimens tend to be far more delicate, aggressive and expensive. It is difficult to obtain the perfect group, and some fish (particularly subdominant males) may be lost. The other method is to remove dominant males as they develop until you have the desired sex

vibeLine.casual: Juveniles covered in white spots that vanish as adults — dramatic shift.
vibeLine.pro: Tropheus duboisi. Tanganyikan aufwuchs grazer; white-spotted juveniles, aggressive colonies required.

flavorText.casual: Young fish are jet-black covered in brilliant white polka dots — then as adults, they lose all spots and develop a vertical white or yellow band instead. Dramatic colour shift. Same aggressive colony dynamics as T. moorii — keep in crowded groups to spread aggression or expect losses.
flavorText.pro: Tropheus duboisi is a Tanganyikan epilithic aufwuchs grazer exhibiting dramatic ontogenetic colour change — juveniles display white spots on a black body, transitioning to a banded adult pattern. Highly aggressive; requires colony management (12+ individuals) to diffuse intraspecific aggression. Herbivorous diet mandatory — protein-rich foods cause bloat. pH 8–9, 24–28°C. Multiple geographic variants; maintain population integrity.

diet.fooditems: It will accept most foods offered but vegetable matter in the form of spirulina flakes, blanched spinach, nori etc. should form the basis of the diet. This can be supplemented with small feeds of live and frozen varieties. Never feed beefheart or any other animal meat as it interferes with the diges

reproduction.comments: Possible. Maternal mouthbrooder. The tank should be set up as decribed above. We recommend the purchase of 20-30 young fish and allowing them to grow on together, as adults are costly. If you can, try and obtain tank -bred fish, as wild specimens tend to be far more delicate, aggressive and expensive. It is difficult to obtain the perfect group, and some fish (particularly subdominant males) may be lost. The other method is to remove dominant males as they develop until you have the desired sex

vibeLine.casual: Aggressive Tanganyikan algae-grazer — needs groups of twelve-plus to spread aggression.
vibeLine.pro: Tropheus moorii. Aggressive Tanganyikan mouthbrooder; colony of 12-plus mandatory.

flavorText.casual: Not a cichlid you keep in pairs or trios — they'll murder each other. Needs large groups (12+) so aggression disperses among many targets. Dozens of colour morphs exist from different lake localities. Dedicated Tanganyikan setup with rocks, alkaline water, and an algae-based diet. Demanding but rewarding for committed keepers.
flavorText.pro: Tropheus moorii is a highly aggressive Tanganyikan maternal mouthbrooder requiring colonies of 12+ individuals to disperse intraspecific aggression. Numerous locality-specific colour morphs exist — never mix morphs. Obligate aufwuchs grazer; protein-rich foods cause bloat. Alkaline water (pH 8–9), high hardness, strong filtration. Species-specific tank essential. Breeding occurs within established colonies; females brood 5–15 eggs orally.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Larger loach with attitude — snail assassin, not always friendly.
vibeLine.pro: Yasuhikotakia modesta. Medium-large botid loach; territorial, effective snail control species.

flavorText.casual: A bigger, meaner cousin of the clown loach. Bluish-grey body, effective snail eater, but can bully smaller loaches and fish. Give them space, hiding spots, and think carefully about tankmates. Not a gentle community addition.
flavorText.pro: Yasuhikotakia modesta reaches 20+ cm and can be territorially aggressive, particularly toward conspecifics and similar-shaped fish. Effective biological snail control. Requires spacious tanks with numerous hiding spots and moderate to strong flow. Egg-scattering spawner; not bred in home aquaria. Keep in groups to diffuse aggression.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The playful little loach that schools in broad daylight.
vibeLine.pro: Ambastaia sidthimunki. Social, diurnal loach; needs a group and open swimming.

flavorText.casual: Unlike most loaches that hide all day, these ones school openly and actively. They're social, playful, and surprisingly bold. Keep a good group and they'll zip around the middle and bottom layers all day.
flavorText.pro: Ambastaia sidthimunki is a diurnal, social loach that schools openly when kept in groups of six or more. Active and peaceful; needs open swimming room as well as hiding spots. Rarely bred in captivity.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Colour-changing micro-predator — the chameleon of freshwater fish 🎨
vibeLine.pro: Badis badis. Chameleonic badid; rapid chromatophore shifts, micro-predator, cave-spawning male guarder.

flavorText.casual: Changes colour depending on mood, dominance, and surroundings — sometimes dark, sometimes pale, sometimes showing blues and reds. A tiny ambush predator that picks off micro-invertebrates from surfaces. Males guard eggs in small caves. Shy, so keep with calm tankmates only.
flavorText.pro: Badis badis is a badid micro-predator exhibiting rapid chromatophore-driven colour change in response to social status and environment. Males are cave-spawners, guarding eggs until hatching. Feeds on small live and frozen invertebrates — often refuses dry food initially. Shy; maintain with non-aggressive, similarly-sized species. Soft to neutral water (pH 6–7.5, 20–26°C). Easily outcompeted at feeding time.

diet.fooditems: As with other Boraras species it is likely to be a micropredator feeding on small insects, worms, crustaceans and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should not be fed these exclusively. Daily meals of small live and frozen fare such as Daph

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg -scattering, continuous spawner that exhibits zero parental care . That is to say when in good condition and in the presence of both males and females relatively small numbers of eggs will be laid daily. In a well-furnished, mature aquarium it is therefore possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without human intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult gro

vibeLine.casual: A speck of bright red that's somehow a whole fish.
vibeLine.pro: Boraras brigittae. Micro-shoaler for soft-water nano and shrimp tanks.

flavorText.casual: Tiny but unforgettable, with males lighting up deep red in a well-planted nano tank. They're shy at first and shine once settled in a confident group. Soft water and gentle tankmates suit them best.
flavorText.pro: Boraras brigittae is a true micro-fish for mature, soft-water nano tanks. Color deepens in large groups over dark substrate. Too small for boisterous tankmates; pairs ideally with dwarf shrimp.

diet.fooditems: Small live foods such as Artemia , Daphnia , etc., should be considered essential although some specimens will learn to accept frozen alternatives. Dried products are normally ignored.

reproduction.comments: This species spawns in small caves with small flower pots, ceramic tubes, shells, or plastic camera film canisters all suitable (the latter may need to be weighed down somehow). An individual male will select a site and display to female in the vicinity until he finds a receptive partner. Around 100-200 eggs are deposited in the cave after which the female departs, leaving the male to guard and tend the clutch. Once a male is guarding eggs you may wish to remove any other fish present, or if pos

vibeLine.casual: Tiny striped goby that refuses dry food — needs live prey.
vibeLine.pro: Brachygobius doriae. Brackish micro-goby; obligate live food preference, minimal dry acceptance.

flavorText.casual: Adorable yellow-and-black striped gobies that will cheerfully starve rather than eat flake food. Need live or frozen food — baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro-worms. Do best in slightly brackish conditions with other small, peaceful species. Bottom-dwellers that claim tiny territories around caves and shells.
flavorText.pro: Brachygobius doriae is a micro-goby (2–3 cm) from brackish coastal habitats. Notoriously reluctant to accept prepared foods — live Artemia nauplii and frozen foods often required long-term. Optimal in low-end brackish conditions (SG 1.002–1.005), pH 7–8.5, 22–28°C. Territorial between males at substrate level. Provide small caves or shells as micro-territories. Poor competitor; avoid housing with active, aggressive feeders.

diet.fooditems: As is the case with with most puffers, this species relishes all kinds of shellfish, as well as worms and other live and frozen foods. It should be fed small snails (shell on) regularly, in order to maintain its sharp teeth. As with other puffers, these grow continuously and become a problem for the

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Unlike many of its relatives, C. travancoricus has been successfully bred in aquaria on numerous occasions. Most experts recommend spawning it in pairs or in a harem situation with a single male and multiple females, as rival males have been known to fight to the death. Keeping more females than males also reduces the risk of a single female being excessively harassed by an amorous male. Success has also been achieved using a bigger group of fish containing several specimens of each sex , or wit

vibeLine.casual: World's smallest puffer — freshwater only, enormous attitude, nips fins.
vibeLine.pro: Carinotetraodon travancoricus. World's smallest pufferfish; freshwater obligate, pronounced fin-nipping aggression.

flavorText.casual: Barely 2.5 cm and acts like it owns the entire tank. Curious, intelligent, tracks your movements, and absolutely will shred the fins of slower tankmates. Fully freshwater — no salt needed despite puffer stereotypes. Needs snails and frozen foods to wear down constantly growing teeth. Species-only setups work best.
flavorText.pro: Carinotetraodon travancoricus is the world's smallest pufferfish (2–2.5 cm), entirely freshwater with no brackish requirement. Despite diminutive size, exhibits pronounced fin-nipping and territorial aggression. Teeth grow continuously — provide hard-shelled prey (snails, crustaceans) for dental maintenance. pH 6.5–7.5, 22–26°C. Best maintained species-only or with fast, short-finned tankmates. Highly intelligent; displays individual recognition of keeper.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unspecified detritus); plants (unspecified filamentous algae); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Survives Australian desert springs — handles temperature and salt extremes.
vibeLine.pro: Chlamydogobius eremius. Australian desert endemic; eurythermal, euryhaline, extreme environmental tolerance range.

flavorText.casual: Evolved in isolated desert springs where conditions swing wildly — handles cold nights, scorching days, fresh or brackish water without complaint. One of the hardiest fish available, thriving where most species would perish. Males colour up with neon-blue fins when breeding. Low-maintenance and fascinating for its backstory alone.
flavorText.pro: Chlamydogobius eremius is endemic to Australian desert springs (Artesian basin), exhibiting exceptional eurythermal (18–28°C+) and euryhaline tolerance. Survives conditions lethal to most aquarium species. Males display iridescent blue finnage during breeding — cave-spawner with paternal care. pH 7–8.5. Accepts most foods readily. Suitable for beginners seeking unusual species with minimal environmental sensitivity. Small (5–6 cm), peaceful.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified); plants (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified); zooplankton (perizoon)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Unrecorded in aquaria. This species exhibits a spawning strategy comparable to that of marine puffers and in contrast to the majority of freshwater te
reproduction.comments: Unrecorded in aquaria. This species exhibits a spawning strategy comparable to that of marine puffers and in contrast to the majority of freshwater tetraodontids, with high fecundity, relatively small eggs, and no parental care. Limited studies in the central Amazon basin suggest that spawning occurs in main river channels or close to banks at the mouths of floodplain lakes and tributaries during periods of high water. The pelagic larvae are washed into nursery zones in floodplain lakes where th

vibeLine.casual: South American puffer whose teeth never stop growing — needs crunchy food.
vibeLine.pro: Colomesus asellus. South American freshwater puffer; continuous dental growth requires shell-based diet.

flavorText.casual: Unlike most puffers, this one is South American and fully freshwater. The catch — teeth grow nonstop. Without hard-shelled food (snails, mussels, crustaceans) the beak overgrows and the fish starves. Active swimmer that needs space and current. Less aggressive than many puffers but still nippy. Advanced commitment.
flavorText.pro: Colomesus asellus is a South American freshwater puffer requiring dedicated dental maintenance — beak grows continuously and must be worn through hard-shelled prey (gastropods, bivalves, crustaceans). Without appropriate diet, overgrowth leads to starvation. Active pelagic swimmer; needs spacious tanks with moderate flow. pH 5.5–7, 22–28°C. Less territorial than Carinotetraodon spp. but still fin-nips. Advanced husbandry due to dietary specialization.

diet.fooditems: Any food offered must be small enough for its tiny mouth. It will take dried foods, but these should be of a small grade or ground down before being added to the tank . For the fish to develop the best colour and condition a good proportion of the diet should also consist of small live and frozen fo

reproduction.comments: Can be bred in a similar fashion to many other Corydoras species , although the fry require a little extra care due to their size. Set up the breeding tank (12″ x 8″ x 8″ or similar is a good size), with either a bare bottom, sand or fine gravel substrate . Use air-powered sponge or box- type filtration as fry won’t be sucked into these and provide some clumps of vegetation such as java moss. A temperature of around 75°F and a pH of 6.5 should be fine. Filtering the water through peat is useful,

vibeLine.casual: The teeny cory that schools in midwater like a tetra.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras pygmaeus. Nano shoaling cory; hovers midwater, needs large groups.

flavorText.casual: Barely an inch long, these tiny corys break the rules by shoaling up in midwater instead of just the bottom. Adorable in a big group in a nano tank. Keep them with peaceful, equally tiny tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras pygmaeus is a true nano cory that shoals in midwater when kept in large groups of ten-plus. Ideal for planted nano tanks with gentle tankmates and dwarf shrimp. Too small to compete with boisterous fish.

diet.fooditems: Benthic omnivore sifting substrate for worms, insect larvae, and organic detritus. In aquaria: sinking pellets/wafers, frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, live tubifex.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg depositor with T-position embrace — adhesive eggs placed on glass and plant leaves
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The rare beauty with a golden sheen and dark mask.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras weitzmani. Premium, less common cory; cooler water, sensitive.

flavorText.casual: A striking cory with golden tones and a bold dark facial mask. Less common and a bit more sensitive than the everyday corys. Stable, slightly cooler water and careful acclimation keep them happy.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras weitzmani is a premium cory preferring cooler (20–25°C), stable water. More sensitive to parameter swings than hardy species. Keep groups of six-plus over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; limited availability.

diet.fooditems: Likely to feed on small invertebrates, algae and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should not be fed these exclusively, and as it rarely rises to the surface there’s little point in offering floating products. Daily meals of small live and

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg-scattering spawner that shows no parental care. It was first bred by SF members Pete Liptrot and Paul Dixon of the Bolton Museum Aquarium, U.K., only a week or two after entering the country in 2006. If the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it’s possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more contro

vibeLine.casual: Tiny galaxies swimming in your tank.
vibeLine.pro: Danio margaritatus. Peaceful schooling fish that prefers cooler, well-oxygenated water.

flavorText.casual: These little guys look like they have stars on their bodies. Super peaceful and love swimming in groups.
flavorText.pro: Best kept in schools of 6+. They prefer slightly cooler temperatures (22–24°C) and appreciate gentle flow with plenty of swimming space.

diet.fooditems: Almost certain to prey chiefly on insects and their larvae in nature as with most danionins. In the aquarium it’s a largely unfussy feeder and will accept most foods. A good quality dried product can be used as the staple diet but this should be supplemented with regular meals of small live and froz

reproduction.comments: Like many small cyprinids this species is an egg-scattering spawner that exhibits no parental care. When the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it’s possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but one or more smaller, say 10-15 litre, containers should also be se

vibeLine.casual: Spotted danio with a chain-mail pattern and calm energy.
vibeLine.pro: Danio tinwini. Spotted micro-danio; peaceful, less frenetic than D. rerio.

flavorText.casual: Instead of stripes, this danio wears a pattern of gold-ringed dark spots that look like chainmail. Calmer than most danios — still active, but won't bounce off the walls. A good nano-sized danio for planted setups.
flavorText.pro: Danio tinwini is a small (2.5–3 cm) spotted danio from Myanmar, less hyperactive than D. rerio. Tolerant of a range of soft to moderately hard water. Egg-scattering spawner; breeds readily. Keep in groups of eight-plus. Well-suited to planted nano communities.

diet.fooditems: Dario species are micropredators feeding on small aquatic crustaceans, worms, insect larvae and other zooplankton. In captivity they should be offered small live or frozen fare such as Artemia nauplii, Daphnia , grindal, micro-, and banana worm. Badids tend to develop issues with obesity and become

reproduction.spawningTrait: Substrate-spawner forming temporary pair bonds. Other fishes are best omitted if you want to raise good numbers of fry, although in a mature, well-fur
reproduction.comments: Substrate-spawner forming temporary pair bonds. Other fishes are best omitted if you want to raise good numbers of fry, although in a mature, well-furnished community a few may survive to adulthood. Either a single pair or a group of adults can be used but if using multiple males be sure to provide each with space to form a territory with around 30 cm² per individual adequate. One male will usually become dominant meaning the others will not be involved in breeding. Water parameters should be wi

vibeLine.casual: The impossibly tiny jewel that only eats live food.
vibeLine.pro: Dario dario. Micro-predator; requires live foods and calm nano conditions.

flavorText.casual: Barely bigger than a grain of rice, males are an intense red-blue mosaic. The catch: most refuse anything that isn't live food. Worth the effort for keepers willing to culture daphnia or grindal worms.
flavorText.pro: Dario dario is a micro-predator that typically refuses dry foods; live or frozen micro-foods are essential. Males are territorial but tiny. Best in a mature, planted nano tank. Females are cryptic and often absent from shipments.

diet.fooditems: Probably a micropredator feeding on terrestrial and aquatic invertebrates and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should also be offered daily meals of small live and frozen fare such as Artemia nauplii, Daphnia , Moina , grindal worm, etc.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Relatively easy to breed and in a well-decorated, mature species set-up fry may begin to appear without further intervention. If the adults are being maintained in a community tank, however, it’s best to organise a separate aquarium for breeding purposes. This can be very simple provided it contains a suitable medium for the fish to deposit their eggs in with Riccia , Taxiphylum spp. mosses, the roots of floating plants or woollen spawning mops all equally suitable, and if filtration is desired

vibeLine.casual: Tiny surface-dweller with a rocket-shaped tail — nano favourite.
vibeLine.pro: Epiplatys annulatus. Miniature surface-dwelling aplocheilid; non-annual, rocket-shaped caudal fin pattern.

flavorText.casual: Barely four centimetres long and lives right at the water's surface — where that flame-coloured, rocket-shaped tail fin is always on display. Not an annual species, so you get a normal lifespan. Needs a tight-fitting lid, calm surface, and small floating foods.
flavorText.pro: Epiplatys annulatus is a non-annual aplocheilid (3.5–4 cm) occupying the upper water column. Distinguished by a lyrate caudal with red-orange banding. Soft, slightly acidic water preferred (pH 6–7, 23–27°C). Egg-depositor on floating plants or surface mops; eggs hatch in 10–14 days without diapause. Tight cover essential — prone to jumping. Micro-foods required due to small gape.

diet.fooditems: Much of the natural diet is likely to be composed of benthic algae plus associated micro-organisms which are rasped from solid surfaces. In captivity it will accept good-quality dried foods and meatier items like live or frozen bloodworm but may suffer internal problems if the diet contains excessiv

reproduction.spawningTrait: Has not been bred in aquaria and the only observations we know of for any member of the genus were made by German aquarist Philipp Dickmann and publis
reproduction.comments: Has not been bred in aquaria and the only observations we know of for any member of the genus were made by German aquarist Philipp Dickmann and published in a hobbyist magazine during 2001. He collected wild specimens of G. scitulus (identified as G. punctulatus at the time) and G. monticola , and attempted to spawn both using different methods. Initially a pair of G. scitulus were placed in a 30 litre tank without substrate or filtration but heavily-aerated and containing some broken flower pot

vibeLine.casual: Flat as a pancake, clings to rocks in fast-flowing streams.
vibeLine.pro: Gastromyzon ctenocephalus. Hillstream loach; obligate high-flow, cool, oxygen-rich water.

flavorText.casual: A flat, saucer-shaped loach built to cling to rocks in rapid currents. Needs a hillstream setup — strong flow, cool water (20–24°C), and rocky surfaces covered in biofilm. Not for standard tropical tanks; they overheat and suffocate in still, warm water.
flavorText.pro: Gastromyzon ctenocephalus is a rheophilic hillstream loach requiring high-flow, oxygen-saturated, cool water (20–24°C). Feeds on biofilm and aufwuchs grazed from rock surfaces. Supplement with algae wafers and blanched vegetables. Not compatible with warm, low-flow community tanks. Breeding reported in high-flow setups with stone substrates; egg-depositor.

diet.fooditems: Probably a micropredator feeding on tiny invertebrates and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should also be offered daily meals of small live and frozen fare such as Artemia nauplii, Moina , grindal worm, etc.

reproduction.comments: An egg -scattering free spawner exhibiting no parental care . When in good condition adults will spawn often and in a mature aquarium it’s possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to maximise yield a more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but a smaller aquarium should also be set up and filled with mature water. This should be very dimly lit and the base covered with some kind of mesh of a la

vibeLine.casual: A tiny glowing ember drifting through your plants.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon amandae. Peaceful nano tetra; warm orange best in groups.

flavorText.casual: Little flecks of warm orange that look amazing in a planted nano tank. They're peaceful and shoal tightly when they feel safe. Perfect with shrimp and other small, gentle tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon amandae is an ideal nano species, glowing orange against greenery. Keep ten or more for confident shoaling and best color. Peaceful enough to cohabit with dwarf shrimp.

diet.fooditems: Micro predator feeding on tiny invertebrates and zooplankton. In aquaria: crushed flake, micro pellets, live/frozen baby brine shrimp, daphnia, vinegar eels.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer among fine plants — very small eggs. No parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: A whisper-thin nano tetra you might almost miss.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon elachys. Diminutive tetra suited to planted nano setups.

flavorText.casual: Extremely small and subtle — more of a delicate shimmer than a bold statement. Best in a quiet planted nano where you can actually appreciate them up close. Keep a good-sized group so they feel secure.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon elachys is a diminutive tetra (under 2 cm) best kept in species-focused or nano community setups with equally small fish. Soft, slightly acidic water preferred. Egg-scattering spawner; challenging due to tiny fry size. Feed micro-foods and freshly hatched artemia.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified); plants (unidentified algae); zoobenthos (unspecified microcrustaceans, unidentified chironomid larva, unidentified ephemeropteran larva)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Colourful Australian native gudgeon — peaceful, bottom-dwelling, and surprisingly vivid.
vibeLine.pro: Hypseleotris compressa. Australian eleotrid; males develop vivid nuptial colouration, peaceful bottom-dweller.

flavorText.casual: Males in breeding condition turn impressively colourful — red, orange, and blue depending on the population. A peaceful bottom-dwelling gudgeon from Australian rivers. Undemanding in most neutral water, and small enough for a medium community tank. Under-appreciated outside Australia.
flavorText.pro: Hypseleotris compressa is an Australian eleotrid (8–10 cm) in which males develop striking nuptial pigmentation. Peaceful bottom-dweller suitable for community tanks with non-aggressive tankmates. Tolerates a broad range of conditions (pH 6.5–8, 20–26°C). Egg-depositor; male guards eggs on flat surfaces. Multiple geographic colour forms exist in trade.

diet.fooditems: plants (unspecified plant matter); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Underrated Jamaican livebearer with a dark belly and constant activity.
vibeLine.pro: Limia melanogaster. Jamaican livebearer; hardy, active, tolerates hard alkaline water well.

flavorText.casual: A livebearer you've probably never seen in shops — native to Jamaica and more active than most guppies or platies. Males show off with dark ventral colouring and busy display behaviour. Hardy in harder, alkaline water. Breeds readily without intervention.
flavorText.pro: Limia melanogaster is a Jamaican poeciliid characterised by melanistic ventral pigmentation in both sexes (more pronounced in males). Thrives in hard, alkaline water (pH 7–8). Active and prolific livebearer. Keep in groups with a female-biased ratio to reduce male harassment. Underrepresented in the hobby despite straightforward husbandry.

diet.fooditems: Likely to feed on small invertebrates, algae and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should mostly be offered small live and frozen fare such as Daphnia , Artemia , etc.

reproduction.comments: Has been bred successfully in aquaria. Like many small cyprinids Microdevario spp. scatter their eggs randomly, typically among aquatic vegetation, and do not exhibit parental care. If the fish are in good condition they will spawn often and in a mature planted aquarium it is possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to increase the yield a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but o

vibeLine.casual: Electric green nano fish that glow under good lighting.
vibeLine.pro: Microdevario kubotai. Neon-green micro-cyprinid; straightforward planted nano candidate.

flavorText.casual: Genuinely neon green under decent lighting — not a marketing stretch. Tiny and peaceful, they look best in a dark-substrate planted nano in a big shoal. Easy to feed and keep, just give them calm tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Microdevario kubotai is a micro-cyprinid reaching ~2 cm, exhibiting intense green fluorescence under appropriate lighting. Undemanding in soft to neutral water. Egg-scattering spawner; fry are minute. Keep ten-plus in planted nanos with gentle flow. Sometimes placed in Danio in older literature.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The pencilfish that hovers at a permanent head-up angle.
vibeLine.pro: Nannostomus eques. Oblique-hovering pencilfish; surface specialist, needs calm conditions.

flavorText.casual: This one rests at a permanent head-up angle, hovering near the surface like a tiny helicopter. Peaceful and delicate, they need very calm water and gentle tankmates. A specialist pick for a serene planted setup.
flavorText.pro: Nannostomus eques is an obligate surface-oriented pencilfish that rests at an oblique angle. Requires calm, soft, acidic water and very gentle tankmates. Keep a group of six-plus. An egg-scatterer; rarely bred at home.

diet.fooditems: A micropredator feeding on tiny invertebrates and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should also be offered daily meals of small live and frozen fare such as Artemia nauplii, Moina , grindal worm, etc.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Has been achieved in aquaria, albeit rarely, and in a densely-planted, mature aquarium it’s possible that small numbers of fry may start to appear without intervention. However if you want to increase the yield of fry a slightly more controlled approach is required. The adult group can still be conditioned together but one or more smaller containers should also be set up and filled with aged water. Fill much of the available space with fine wool mops, Taxiphyllum or other fine-leaved aquatic pla

vibeLine.casual: Tiny striped fish that hover in place like hummingbirds.
vibeLine.pro: Nannostomus marginatus. Micro pencilfish; peaceful hover-feeder for planted nanos.

flavorText.casual: They hang motionless in the water column, barely moving except to snag a micro-morsel. Gorgeous little striped fish for a calm, planted nano. Keep them with equally gentle tankmates — anything boisterous will stress them out.
flavorText.pro: Nannostomus marginatus is among the smallest pencilfish, ideal for densely planted nano tanks. Feeds on micro-foods; supplement with freshly hatched brine shrimp. Males establish small territories. Egg-scatterer among fine-leaved plants; eggs and fry are tiny. Soft, acidic water preferred.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified flying insects)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Livebearer with a jutting lower jaw — skims the surface.
vibeLine.pro: Nomorhamphus liemi. Sulawesi halfbeak; livebearer, surface-dwelling, elongated lower jaw morphology.

flavorText.casual: That extended lower jaw isn't damage — it's the defining feature. Skims the water surface picking off insects and floating food. One of the few livebearers that isn't a guppy or molly, giving birth to fully formed fry. Males spar with each other using those jaw extensions. Keep a lid on tight — jumpers.
flavorText.pro: Nomorhamphus liemi is a Sulawesian halfbeak with pronounced lower jaw extension used in surface feeding and male-male sparring. Viviparous — females deliver fully developed fry. pH 6.5–7.5, 22–26°C. Surface-obligate; provide floating plants for security and open surface area for feeding. Strong jumpers — secure lids essential. Males territorial among themselves; maintain one male with multiple females. Fry predation occurs; separate for maximal yield.

diet.fooditems: A natural micropredator meaning small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia and chopped bloodworm should form the basis of the diet.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: When its natural habitats become dessicated during the dry season the adult fish die, leaving fertilised eggs encased within the substrate . These are resistant to dessication and remain there until the rains return some 5-6 months later at which point the fry hatch and grow very quickly with sexual maturity reached at around 3 weeks of age. Typical lifespan in the aquarium is 6-12 months and eggs should be stored at a temperature of 70-77°F/21.1-25°C for 5-7 months before being wetted.

vibeLine.casual: Annual killifish — lives fast, dies young, looks impossibly colourful.
vibeLine.pro: Nothobranchius rachovii. Annual killifish; eggs diapause in substrate through dry season.

flavorText.casual: This fish's entire adult life plays out in months, not years. In the wild, pools dry up and adults die — but their eggs survive in the mud until rains return. Males are among the most colourful freshwater fish alive. You're keeping a countdown clock, but what a display while it lasts.
flavorText.pro: Nothobranchius rachovii is an annual killifish from seasonal pools in Mozambique. Adults complete their lifecycle in months; eggs undergo diapause in peat substrate, surviving desiccation for months to years. Males exhibit extreme polychromatic coloration. Breed on peat or coco fibre substrate, harvest and store eggs semi-dry for 3–6 months before wetting. Short-lived adults (6–18 months in captivity).

diet.fooditems: Likely to be omnivorous feeding on small invertebrates, crustacea, filamentous algae, fallen fruit and suchlike in nature. In aquaria it may survive on a diet of dried foods but like most fishes does best when offered a varied menu which in this case should also contain live and frozen chironomid la

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The delicate neon relative that glows green in blackwater.
vibeLine.pro: Paracheirodon simulans. Soft-water nano tetra; needs acidic, tannin-rich conditions.

flavorText.casual: Smaller and more delicate than the common neon, with a green-blue stripe that glows in tannin-stained water. They need soft, acidic conditions to thrive and look their best. A blackwater specialist.
flavorText.pro: Paracheirodon simulans is a true blackwater species requiring very soft, acidic water (pH 5–6.5) to show its best color and remain healthy. Keep large groups in mature, tannin-rich tanks. More sensitive than the common neon.

diet.fooditems: An omnivorous species , feeding on a variety of zoobenthos and detritus in the wild. Will eat most foods offered but should have some vegetation in the diet. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Very easy. Endler’s will breed in the community tank and are incredibly prolific. To be honest breeding this species is more of a case of ‘when’ rather then ‘how’. If you’re not interested inbreeding them, buy only male fish. Otherwise breeding is much the same as for many other livebearers. We recommend that several females should be kept to every male in order to dissipate vigorous harassment by males. Areas of dense planting will also help. Males constantly display to females, spreading their

vibeLine.casual: A pocket-sized firework crossed with a guppy.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia wingei. Tiny, vivid livebearer; hybridizes freely with guppies.

flavorText.casual: Smaller than a guppy but somehow even flashier, with males that look like walking neon paint jobs. They're hardy, lively, and breed constantly. Keep them away from guppies if you want pure Endlers.
flavorText.pro: Poecilia wingei is a small, intensely colored livebearer for hard water. It interbreeds readily with guppies, so isolate to preserve pure strains. Prolific and active in groups.

diet.fooditems: Feeds chiefly on floating or suspended zooplankton, phytoplankton, and invertebrates in nature, and in the aquarium must be offered items of a suitable size. Ideally much of the diet should comprise live foods such as Daphnia , Moina , Artemia nauplii, micro worm, etc., although small/crushed floati

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: This species is an egg-scatterer exhibiting no parental care and will consume its own eggs and fry given the opportunity. Since all aquarium fish appear to derive from a single collection (see ‘Distribution’) there are also some inbreeding issues which can include reduced fertility, life span, and a high rate of deformity in fry. Spawning is more likely in temperatures towards the upper end of the range suggested above, with females capable of depositing a few eggs daily for a period of several

vibeLine.casual: Tiny rainbow relative with a forked yellow tail 🌿
vibeLine.pro: Pseudomugil furcatus. New Guinean pseudomugilid; forked caudal, neutral to alkaline water.

flavorText.casual: A small, active fish from New Guinea that looks like a miniature rainbowfish — because it basically is one. Males display with spread fins and that distinctive forked yellow tail. Easy to keep in planted nano tanks with gentle flow. Groups of eight-plus bring out the best behaviour.
flavorText.pro: Pseudomugil furcatus is a New Guinean pseudomugilid reaching 5 cm, distinguished by its forked caudal fin with yellow margins. Egg-scatterer among fine vegetation; males display actively in the presence of rivals. Prefers neutral to slightly alkaline water (pH 7–8, 24–28°C). Suitable for planted community tanks with similarly-sized peaceful species.

diet.fooditems: Feeds chiefly on floating or suspended zooplankton, phytoplankton, and invertebrates in nature, and in the aquarium must be offered items of a suitable size. Ideally much of the diet should comprise live foods such as Daphnia , Moina , Artemia nauplii, micro worm, etc., although small/crushed floati

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: This species is an egg-scatterer exhibiting no parental care and will consume its own eggs and fry given the opportunity. Spawning is more likely in temperatures towards the upper end of the range suggested above, with females capable of depositing a few eggs daily for a period of several days, these being attached to aquatic vegetation or other substrate by adhesive filaments. An individual male may also mate with multiple females during a single day, and spawning usually continues throughout d

vibeLine.casual: Males flash elaborate threadfin displays — tiny fish, big performance.
vibeLine.pro: Pseudomugil gertrudae. Delicate pseudomugilid; males exhibit elaborate filamentous fin extensions during display.

flavorText.casual: Barely three centimetres long, but males put on a show that punches way above their size — spreading elongated fin threads during courtship displays. Needs soft, slightly acidic water and a mature planted tank. A nano showstopper for patient keepers.
flavorText.pro: Pseudomugil gertrudae is a diminutive pseudomugilid (2.5–3 cm) in which males develop elongated filamentous extensions on dorsal and anal fins used in display. Prefers soft to neutral water (pH 6–7.5, 24–28°C). Egg-scatterer on fine-leaved vegetation. Short-lived (1–2 years); maintain breeding colonies to sustain populations. Best in species-only nano setups.

diet.fooditems: Micro predator — feeds on tiny insects, zooplankton, and biofilm in nature. In aquaria: micro pellets, crushed flake, live baby brine shrimp, micro worms.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer — deposits adhesive eggs on fine-leaved plants (java moss) over several days
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Vivid red-orange nano fish — a recent discovery from New Guinea.
vibeLine.pro: Pseudomugil luminatus. Recently described pseudomugilid; vivid red-orange coloration, soft acidic water preferred.

flavorText.casual: Described to science only recently and immediately popular for good reason — males glow red-orange in a way few nano fish can match. Needs soft, slightly acidic water and a mature planted tank. Small groups in a dedicated nano setup bring out the best colour and behaviour.
flavorText.pro: Pseudomugil luminatus is a recently described pseudomugilid from Papua New Guinea exhibiting intense red-orange body coloration in males. Small (2–3 cm); best maintained in species-specific nano setups with soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6–7.5, 24–28°C). Egg-scatterer on fine-leaved plants. Short-lived; maintain breeding colonies for population continuity.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified); others (unidentified terrestrial insects, unidentified ants); plants (unidentified diatoms); zoobenthos (unidentified dipterans, unidentified chironomid larva, unidentified ephemeropteran larva)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Australian native that handles fresh through to brackish without fuss.
vibeLine.pro: Pseudomugil signifer. Australian pseudomugilid; euryhaline, tolerates fresh to brackish conditions.

flavorText.casual: A hardy little Australian native that thrives across a surprisingly wide range of salinities — fresh through to full brackish. Males develop bright blue eyes and display with spread fins. Easy to breed, easy to keep, and works well in subtropical or tropical setups.
flavorText.pro: Pseudomugil signifer is an Australian pseudomugilid with broad salinity tolerance (fresh to brackish). Reaches 5–6 cm. Males exhibit neon-blue eye coloration and fin displays during courtship. Egg-scatterer on fine vegetation; breeds readily. Accepts pH 6.5–7.5 and 20–26°C. Suitable for subtropical community tanks or brackish biotopes. Multiple geographic populations exist.

diet.fooditems: Likely to feed chiefly on small invertebrates and other zooplankton in nature. In the aquarium it will accept dried foods of a suitable size but should be offered regular meals of small live and frozen fare such as Daphnia , Artemia and suchlike.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Naturally deposits eggs among aquatic vegetation, filamentous algae, or on the underside of broad leaves, but in the aquarium synthetic spawning mops can also be used if you prefer. Apparently the most favourable results are obtained when a group of adults is maintained together as a group and the plants or spawning medium checked for eggs on a daily basis. Temperature also appears important, with the fish failing to spawn at values in excess of 77°F/25°C, while general hardness should be at lea

vibeLine.casual: An unusual blue-bodied barb from Lake Inle that likes cooler water.
vibeLine.pro: Sawbwa resplendens. Lake Inle endemic; unique scaleless barb, cooler harder water required.

flavorText.casual: A strange little barb with a metallic blue body and red-tipped nose and tail. From Lake Inle in Myanmar, it prefers cooler (21–25°C), slightly harder, alkaline-leaning water — the opposite of most small tropicals. Worth the specific setup.
flavorText.pro: Sawbwa resplendens is a scaleless Lake Inle barb requiring cooler (21–25°C), moderately hard, neutral to slightly alkaline water. Males display metallic blue body with red caudal and snout tips. Egg-scattering spawner; breeds in groups over fine plants. Not compatible with warm, soft-water communities.

diet.fooditems: plants (unidentified algae); zoobenthos (benthic invertebrates)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Sewellia spp. are arguably the easiest of all loach species to breed in aquaria and success has been achieved with several species . Initial reports m
reproduction.comments: Sewellia spp. are arguably the easiest of all loach species to breed in aquaria and success has been achieved with several species . Initial reports mostly described recovering fry that had been sucked into external canister filters in hill stream-style arrangements such as that described above (see ‘Maintenance’), or discovery of small numbers surviving in mature community set-ups. However subsequent experiments have shown that they can be bred in numbers in very simple quarters using groups of

vibeLine.casual: The flat suction-cup fish built for fast-flowing cool water.
vibeLine.pro: Sewellia lineolata. Rheophilic hillstream loach; needs high flow and cool oxygen.

flavorText.casual: Flat like a tiny stingray and designed for rushing water, they cling to rocks with their whole body. They need strong current, cool temps, and lots of oxygen. Not a typical tropical setup, but a stunning specialist choice.
flavorText.pro: Sewellia lineolata is a rheophilic hillstream loach requiring high flow, cool well-oxygenated water (18–24°C), and biofilm-covered surfaces to graze. Not suited for standard tropical tanks. Keep in groups on smooth rocks with strong turnover.

diet.fooditems: Presumably feeds on small aquatic crustaceans, worms, insect larvae and other zooplankton in nature. It can be a little picky in the aquarium and may not accept dried foods although in some cases they will learn to take them over time. At any rate they should always be offered regular meals of small

reproduction.spawningTrait: This species has not been bred often in the hobby which is probably down to the fact it does not appreciate fluctuating water conditions, making it di
reproduction.comments: This species has not been bred often in the hobby which is probably down to the fact it does not appreciate fluctuating water conditions, making it difficult to arrange separate spawning /rearing tanks. Reports do exist, however, and the greatest successes have been when the adult fish are maintained alone as a good-sized group in heavily-planted, mature aquaria. The pH should ideally be below 6.0 with other parameters within the ranges suggested here, the most important factor being that they r

vibeLine.casual: Tiny jewel-toned nano fish that needs very soft, acidic water.
vibeLine.pro: Sundadanio axelrodi. Blackwater obligate nano; requires pH below 6.5 consistently.

flavorText.casual: Stunning colours packed into a fish barely over a centimetre. The catch: they need very soft, acidic blackwater to survive and show colour. Not for a generic community tank — set up a dedicated blackwater nano and they'll reward you.
flavorText.pro: Sundadanio axelrodi is a blackwater-obligate micro-fish requiring pH 5.0–6.5 and very low hardness. Multiple colour forms exist. Feed micro-foods; fry are extremely small. Egg-scatterer in leaf litter. High mortality if introduced to neutral or hard water. Best in species-focused tannin-stained setups.

diet.fooditems: Will usually accept dried food but much prefers small live and frozen stuff, such as bloodworm , daphnia , brineshrimp etc. The fish will also show much better colouration and come into spawning condition far quicker on this kind of diet.

reproduction.comments: Easily bred under the right conditions. The fish tend to spawn in caves, so the provision of these is essential. Short lenths of plastic piping work well as these can easily be removed by placing a tumb over each end and lifting the whole thing clear. Clumps of live plants are also useful to provide extra cover for the fish. In order to obtain a breeding pair it’s best to buy a group of 6-8 young fish and allow them to pair off naturally. Feed the group on a good diet of live and frozen foods, c

vibeLine.casual: The tiny, colorful gudgeon with a peacock-like eye spot.
vibeLine.pro: Tateurndina ocellicauda. Peaceful nano gudgeon; cave-spawner with male fry care.

flavorText.casual: A small, strikingly colorful fish with bright bands and a distinctive eye-spot on the tail. Peaceful and easy in a planted nano setup. Males guard eggs in small caves, making them a fascinating breeding project.
flavorText.pro: Tateurndina ocellicauda is a peaceful nano gudgeon where males guard eggs inside caves or tubes until hatching. Keep pairs or small groups in a planted tank. Accepts a range of parameters; a rewarding and easily bred species.

diet.fooditems: Remaining close to the shore, it feeds primarily on insects; a study (Netto-Ferreira et al, 2007) determined that 99.6% of the diet of this fish consists of insects, and the majority (87.6%) of these were terrestrial. Sometimes a little reluctant to take dried foods initially, but it will usually le

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer — jumping breeders in the wild. Rarely bred in captivity. Need covered tank
reproduction.comments: Unrecorded.

vibeLine.casual: The largest hatchetfish — powerful jumper, needs zero lid gaps.
vibeLine.pro: Thoracocharax stellatus. Largest hatchetfish; most powerful jumper, sealed lids mandatory.

flavorText.casual: The biggest of the hatchetfish family with the most powerful leap. Surface-only and a spectacular escape artist. If there's a gap, they'll find it. Keep them in a group with floating plants for cover and absolutely no openings in the lid.
flavorText.pro: Thoracocharax stellatus is the largest commonly available hatchetfish (up to 8 cm), with exceptional jumping force. Obligate surface dweller; requires sealed lids and floating plant cover. Egg-scattering spawner; rarely bred in home aquaria. Less demanding on water chemistry than Carnegiella but still favours soft, slightly acidic water.

diet.fooditems: Likely to feed on insects and other small invertebrates in the wild. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they are recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or chironomid larvae (bloodworm) to ensure development of opt

reproduction.comments: Bubble nester. Soft water with a slightly raised temperature is said to induce reproductive behaviour. The aquarium should have the tightest-fitting cover possible (some breeders use clingfilm/plastic wrap) because the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air, without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. The pair need not be separated prior to spawning. The male tends to construct his nest within a raft of surface vegetation, under an overhang or in a cavity. Once the n

vibeLine.casual: Larger croaking gourami — same audible purring, more presence in-tank.
vibeLine.pro: Trichopsis schalleri. Largest Trichopsis species; produces audible croaking via pectoral mechanism.

flavorText.casual: Like its smaller cousin T. pumila, this one makes actual sounds you can hear — a purring croak produced by its pectoral fins. Bigger-bodied (up to 6 cm) and a bit bolder. Peaceful, does well in planted tanks with quiet tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Trichopsis schalleri is the largest Trichopsis species (~6 cm), sharing the genus's acoustic signalling via modified pectoral fin tendons. Soft, slightly acidic water preferred. Bubblenester; male builds small nest and guards fry. Keep in pairs or small groups with ample plant cover and minimal surface agitation.

diet.fooditems: This species is a generalised detrivore, so offer a varied diet comprising dried, frozen and live foods including sinking tablets, wafers, slices of fruit and vegetables plus meatier fare such as bloodworm , earthworms, prawns, cockles and lancefish. In nature it is reputed to occur close to settlem

reproduction.comments: Has been achieved but little information is available. Apparently a cave spawner with the male responsible for guarding and tending the eggs.

vibeLine.casual: Starts small, ends at a metre — will outgrow your tank.
vibeLine.pro: Acanthicus adonis. Reaches 100 cm; heavily spined predatory loricariid needing enormous housing.

flavorText.casual: Sold as cute dark juveniles with white spots. Grows to nearly a metre. Covered in sharp spines, eats meatier foods than most plecos, and will need a tank measured in thousands of litres eventually. Research before buying — this is a lifetime commitment to a tank the size of a car.
flavorText.pro: Acanthicus adonis reaches up to 100 cm in the wild and 60–80 cm in captivity, requiring aquaria of 1,000+ litres at adult size. Heavily armoured with odontodes; omnivorous tending carnivorous. Not a typical algae-grazing loricariid. Cave-spawner in the wild. Juveniles are commonly sold without adequate size warnings — responsible retailers should disclose adult dimensions.

diet.fooditems: Micro predator feeding on small invertebrates, insect larvae, and crustaceans in leaf litter zones. In aquaria: live/frozen daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworm, micro pellets.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Extreme blackwater specialist — needs pH below 5.5, not for beginners.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma diplotaenia. Obligate blackwater species; requires pH 4.5–5.5, minimal hardness, advanced husbandry.

flavorText.casual: This is not a standard apisto. It demands water so acidic that most equipment and tankmates can't handle it — pH 4.5 to 5.5, essentially zero hardness. Stunning fish, but maintaining those extreme parameters long-term is serious work. Research thoroughly before committing.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma diplotaenia is an obligate blackwater specialist requiring pH 4.5–5.5 and near-zero general hardness — among the most extreme water chemistry demands in the genus. Cave-brooding; females guard fry. Wild-caught specimens dominate trade due to limited captive breeding success. RO water and botanical extracts (leaves, peat) essential to maintain acidic, tannin-stained conditions. Not compatible with most common community fish.

diet.fooditems: Apistogramma spp. are chiefly carnivorous and feed mostly on benthic invertebrates in nature. In the aquarium live and frozen foods such as Artemia , Daphnia and chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ) should be offered regularly although most species will also learn to accept dried alternatives with pellet
