reproduction.comments: Substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor. The female is mostly responsible for post- spawning care of eggs and fry and in smaller aquaria the male may need to be removed as she may become hyper-aggressive.

vibeLine.casual: Blackwater specialist with a bold striped pattern — not beginner territory.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma mendezi. Blackwater-obligate apisto; striking melanistic patterning, demands pH 5–6, soft water.

flavorText.casual: A visually striking apisto with bold dark patterning, but it comes from blackwater habitats and means it. pH 5–6, near-zero hardness, and stable conditions are mandatory. Rewards the effort with genuinely impressive display, but this is advanced fishkeeping.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma mendezi is a blackwater specialist requiring pH 5–6 and minimal hardness. Distinguished by bold melanistic banding and intense male coloration. Cave-brooder; females exhibit strong brood defence with yellow maternal coloration. RO water with tannin supplementation recommended. Wild-caught imports predominate. Sensitive to water quality deterioration; maintain low organic load.

diet.fooditems: Nocturnal carnivore hunting small invertebrates, worms, and insect larvae. In aquaria: frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, live blackworms, sinking carnivore pellets.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Weakly electric nocturnal hunter — navigates total darkness using self-generated fields.
vibeLine.pro: Apteronotus leptorhynchus. Weakly electric gymnotiform; electrolocation for navigation, nocturnal predator.

flavorText.casual: Generates a weak electric field and "reads" distortions in it to navigate and hunt in complete darkness — like biological sonar but with electricity. Strictly nocturnal and shy, hiding in tubes or driftwood during the day. Needs dim lighting, hiding spots, and frozen or live foods. Sensitive to water quality.
flavorText.pro: Apteronotus leptorhynchus is a weakly electric gymnotiform generating continuous wave-type electric organ discharge (EOD) for electrolocation and communication. Nocturnal; requires dark refuges (tubes, driftwood) during photoperiod. pH 6–7.5, 23–28°C. Sensitive to water quality fluctuations and medications. Feed frozen/live foods — rarely accepts pellets. Conspecific aggression occurs via EOD jamming. Less commonly kept than Apteronotus albifrons but similar care requirements.

diet.fooditems: Gut analyses of wild specimens revealed the diet to be composed chiefly of algae, particularly diatoms and filamentous genera such as Spirogyra? alongside smaller amounts of invertebrates such as chironomids and bryozoans. In the aquarium aufwuchs should thus be allowed to colonise all surfaces exce

reproduction.spawningTrait: The possibly conspecific L081 has been bred on a single occasion. Any successful attempt is likely to depend on providing a large tank, well-oxygenate
reproduction.comments: The possibly conspecific L081 has been bred on a single occasion. Any successful attempt is likely to depend on providing a large tank, well-oxygenated water and an excellent diet.

vibeLine.casual: The black pleco dusted with bright golden spots.
vibeLine.pro: Baryancistrus xanthellus. Demanding pleco; needs warmth, oxygen, and pristine water.

flavorText.casual: Jet black with glowing gold spots and fin tips, it's a stunner that's also fussy. It wants warm, oxygen-rich, immaculate water and plenty of grazing. Not a starter pleco, but gorgeous when thriving.
flavorText.pro: Baryancistrus xanthellus needs warm, highly oxygenated, pristine water and a mixed algae-and-protein diet. Sensitive to nitrate and low oxygen, hence its advanced rating. Territorial; provide caves and broken sightlines.

diet.fooditems: Obligate piscivore — feeds almost exclusively on small fish and large invertebrates. In aquaria: live feeder fish (guppies/mollies), frozen silversides, shrimp. May accept pellets after training.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Predatory livebearer shaped like a pike — eats fish, including siblings.
vibeLine.pro: Belonesox belizanus. Predatory poeciliid; pike-like morphology, piscivorous, consumes conspecific fry.

flavorText.casual: This doesn't behave like any livebearer you've kept before. Pike-shaped head, toothy mouth, and it eats other fish — including its own offspring and tankmates. Fascinating predator for a species-only setup but absolutely not a community fish. Needs live or fresh foods.
flavorText.pro: Belonesox belizanus is a piscivorous poeciliid reaching 15–20 cm (females larger), with an elongated pike-like body and toothed jaws. Consumes fish up to half its body length, including conspecific fry. Species-only setup essential. Hard, alkaline to brackish water tolerated. Livebearer; large fry born fully capable of swimming. Maintain singly or as monitored pairs — females may attack smaller males.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on insects, other small invertebrates and zooplankton in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they’re recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or bloodworm regularly to ensure development of opt

reproduction.comments: Paternal mouthbrooder. Organise a separate tank for breeding purposes unless the fish are already being maintained alone, setting this up as suggested above. The tank should have the tightest-fitting cover you can find (some breeders use clingfilm instead to ensure no gaps) as the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. Following a protracted courtship, eggs and milt are released during an ’embrace’ typical of anabantoids in

vibeLine.casual: Orange-red mouthbrooder with crisp white fin edges — soft water only.
vibeLine.pro: Betta albimarginata. Paternal mouthbrooder; diagnostic white fin margins, soft acidic conditions required.

flavorText.casual: One of the most visually striking mouthbrooding bettas — warm orange body with clean white margins on the fins. Males carry eggs for about two weeks. Needs soft, acidic blackwater conditions. Peaceful by betta standards; pairs work well.
flavorText.pro: Betta albimarginata is a paternal mouthbrooder distinguished by white margins on caudal and anal fins. Requires very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5). Males incubate for approximately 12–14 days. Maintain as pairs or trios in blackwater setups with leaf litter and botanicals. Relatively tractable wild betta for dedicated soft-water keepers.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on insects, other small invertebrates and zooplankton in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they’re recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or bloodworm regularly to ensure development of opt

The tank should have the tightest-fitting cover you can find (some breeders use clingfilm instead to ensure no gaps) as the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. Following a protracted courtship, eggs and milt are released during an ’embrace’ typical of anabantoids in

vibeLine.casual: Deep-red mouthbrooder named for its snakehead-shaped head profile.
vibeLine.pro: Betta channoides. Paternal mouthbrooder; snakehead-like morphology, requires very soft acidic water.

flavorText.casual: Named for its elongated, snakehead-like head shape. Males are deep brick-red and mouthbrood for about two weeks. Needs very soft, acidic water (pH 5–6.5) — blackwater conditions with tannins and leaf litter. Not a beginner wild betta.
flavorText.pro: Betta channoides is a paternal mouthbrooder requiring very soft, acidic water (pH 5–6.5, TDS under 50). Males display intense red colouration and incubate eggs orally for 12–14 days. Maintain in pairs in densely structured blackwater setups with leaf litter. Sensitive to hard or alkaline water.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they are recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or chironomid larvae (bloodworm) regularly to ensure developmen

reproduction.comments: Bubble nester. It is particularly important to provide plenty of cover for the female, and empty camera film canisters or lengths of plastic tubing can be used to offer potential nesting sites. Floating plants may be incorporated into the nest if present. The aquarium should have the tightest-fitting cover possible (some breeders use clingfilm/plastic wrap) because the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. The pair need no

vibeLine.casual: The crescent-tail betta that actually lives up to "peaceful" — mostly.
vibeLine.pro: Betta imbellis. Bubblenester; significantly reduced aggression relative to B. splendens, crescent caudal.

flavorText.casual: Often called the "crescent betta" for the red-rimmed crescent marking on its tail. Genuinely less aggressive than splendens — males can sometimes coexist in large, well-planted tanks (no guarantees, though). Still a bubblenester, still jumps.
flavorText.pro: Betta imbellis is a bubblenesting species within the B. splendens complex, exhibiting markedly reduced male-male aggression. Males may coexist in densely planted, spacious aquaria, though monitoring is advisable. Readily hybridises with B. splendens — lineage integrity important for conservation. Breeds like B. splendens; male guards nest.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on insects and other small invertebrates/ zooplankton in nature, plus perhaps the shrimp that share its habitat. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they’re recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or

reproduction.comments: Paternal mouthbrooder. Ideally organise a separate tank for breeding purposes, unless the fish are already being maintained alone, setting this up as suggested above. The tank should have the tightest-fitting cover you can find (some breeders use clingfilm instead to ensure no gaps) as the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. Though not always possible, the ideal way to obtain a compatible pair for breeding purposes is to

vibeLine.casual: Rarest wild betta — enormous mouth for brooding, extremely demanding care.
vibeLine.pro: Betta macrostoma. Critically rare mouthbrooding Betta; extreme soft-acid requirements, high acquisition cost.

flavorText.casual: Among the most spectacular and expensive wild bettas in the hobby — males are flame-red with that oversized mouth used to brood eggs and fry. Demands extremely soft, acidic water (pH 5–6) and pristine conditions. Not forgiving of mistakes. A grail fish for serious Betta keepers who've mastered the basics years ago.
flavorText.pro: Betta macrostoma is among the rarest and most demanding wild Betta species — paternal mouthbrooder with a disproportionately large buccal cavity for fry incubation. Requires pH 5–6, near-zero hardness, and minimal bacterial load. 22–26°C. Males display intense red-orange coloration. Extremely sensitive to water chemistry fluctuations. High acquisition cost reflects scarcity and captive breeding difficulty. Species-only setups with botanical conditioning (leaves, peat) essential. Not a beginner Betta.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on insects and other small invertebrates/ zooplankton in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they’re recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or bloodworm regularly to ensure development of opt

reproduction.comments: Bubble-nester. Organise a separate tank for breeding purposes unless the fish are already being maintained alone, setting this up as suggested above. The tank should have the tightest-fitting cover you can find (some breeders use clingfilm instead to ensure no gaps) as the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. The pair need not be separated prior to spawning . The male may construct the nest in a tube or canister, under a

vibeLine.casual: Wild betta from brackish swamps that handles salt others cannot.
vibeLine.pro: Betta mahachaiensis. Brackish-tolerant wild bubblenester; pH 7–8.5, unique estuarine Betta species.

flavorText.casual: A wild bubblenesting betta that naturally lives in slightly brackish, alkaline water near Thailand's coast. Dark-bodied with green-blue iridescence. One of very few bettas that tolerates (and arguably prefers) a pinch of salt in the tank. Still a jumper.
flavorText.pro: Betta mahachaiensis is a bubblenesting species from coastal nipa palm swamps near Bangkok, tolerating brackish conditions (SG up to ~1.005) and pH 7–8.5. Iridescent green-blue scaling on a dark body. Males territorial; maintain singly or in well-structured species tanks. Conservation concern due to habitat loss — captive breeding encouraged.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they are recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or chironomid larvae (bloodworm) regularly to ensure developmen

reproduction.comments: Paternal mouthbrooder. Ideally organise a separate aquarium for breeding purposes, unless the fish are already being maintained alone. The aquarium should have the tightest-fitting cover possible (some breeders use clingfilm/plastic wrap) because the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. Following a protracted courtship, eggs and milt are released during an ’embrace’ typical of osphronemids, with the male wrapped around th

vibeLine.casual: Subtle mouthbrooding betta that thrives in cooler, calmer water conditions.
vibeLine.pro: Betta picta. Cooler-water paternal mouthbrooder (22–26°C); peaceful, group-tolerant wild Betta species.

flavorText.casual: Not flashy like splendens — this is a muted, earthy betta with understated charm. Males mouthbrood, and the species tolerates cooler water (22–26°C) than most bettas. Peaceful enough to keep in groups. A thinking person's betta.
flavorText.pro: Betta picta is a paternal mouthbrooder preferring cooler temperatures (22–26°C) than most congeners. Relatively peaceful; can be maintained in groups with adequate cover. Males incubate for approximately 12 days. Soft to neutral water. One of the earliest described Betta species (1846), often confused with B. taeniata complex.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they are recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or chironomid larvae (bloodworm) regularly to ensure developmen

reproduction.comments: Paternal mouthbrooder. Ideally organise a separate aquarium for breeding purposes, unless the fish are already being maintained alone. The aquarium should have the tightest-fitting cover possible (some breeders use clingfilm/plastic wrap) because the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. Following a protracted courtship, eggs and milt are released during an ’embrace’ typical of osphronemids, with the male wrapped around th

vibeLine.casual: A mouthbrooding wild betta — the male carries eggs in his jaw.
vibeLine.pro: Betta pugnax. Paternal mouthbrooder; males incubate eggs orally for 10–14 days.

flavorText.casual: Completely different reproductive strategy from pet-store bettas. Males hold fertilised eggs in their mouths for about two weeks, not eating until the fry are released. Calmer and more social than splendens — can sometimes be kept in pairs or groups.
flavorText.pro: Betta pugnax is a paternal mouthbrooder, with males incubating clutches orally for 10–14 days. Unlike the B. splendens complex, this species is relatively tolerant of conspecifics and can be maintained in pairs or small groups. Requires soft, acidic water with heavy cover. Jumps readily — secure lids essential.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they are recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or chironomid larvae (bloodworm) regularly to ensure developmen

reproduction.comments: Paternal mouthbrooder. Ideally organise a separate aquarium for breeding purposes, unless the fish are already being maintained alone. The aquarium should have the tightest-fitting cover possible (some breeders use clingfilm/plastic wrap) because the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. Following a protracted courtship, eggs and milt are released during an ’embrace’ typical of osphronemids, with the male wrapped around th

vibeLine.casual: Limestone-cave mouthbrooder that actually prefers harder, more alkaline water.
vibeLine.pro: Betta simplex. Karst-habitat mouthbrooder; unusually requires neutral to alkaline, harder water conditions.

flavorText.casual: One of the few wild bettas that does well in harder, more alkaline water — it comes from limestone karst pools in southern Thailand. Males mouthbrood like B. pugnax. A good choice if your tap water runs hard and you still want a wild betta.
flavorText.pro: Betta simplex is a paternal mouthbrooder endemic to limestone karst habitats in Krabi province, Thailand. Unusually for Betta, it thrives in pH 7–8 and moderately hard water. Males incubate eggs orally. Maintain in pairs or trios with abundant cover and structured hardscape mimicking karst environments.

diet.fooditems: Likely to prey on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates in nature. Captive fish will normally accept dried products once they are recognised as edible, but should be offered plenty of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia , Artemia or chironomid larvae (bloodworm) regularly to ensure developmen

reproduction.comments: Bubble nester. It is particularly important to provide plenty of cover for the female, and empty camera film canisters or lengths of plastic tubing can be used to offer potential nesting sites. Floating plants may be incorporated into the nest if present. The aquarium should have the tightest-fitting cover possible (some breeders use clingfilm/plastic wrap) because the fry need access to a layer of warm, humid air without which development of the labyrinth organ can be impaired. The pair need no

vibeLine.casual: Wild bubblenester covered in iridescent green — not your pet-store betta.
vibeLine.pro: Betta smaragdina. Wild bubblenesting species; iridescent green scaling, less aggressive than splendens.

flavorText.casual: A wild betta species — not the long-finned pet-store fish. Males shimmer with green iridescence over a dark body. Still a bubblenester and still territorial with other males, but generally less rage-fueled than splendens. Needs a lid — they jump.
flavorText.pro: Betta smaragdina is a wild bubblenesting species from the B. splendens complex, displaying intense green iridescence. Less aggressive than B. splendens but males still cannot be housed together. Breeds readily; male builds bubble nest and guards fry. Hybridises easily with B. splendens — keep lineages pure.

diet.fooditems: Stomach contents of dissected wild specimens were found to be composed largely of small invertebrates including chironomid larvae and ostracods. The captive diet should thus contain a variety of small live and/or frozen chironomid larvae ( bloodworm ), Tubifex , Artemia , mosquito larvae , etc. Some

reproduction.comments: Biparental substrate spawner which normally lays its eggs in crevices or cavities among the décor, with the underside of submerged leaves or small caves normally preferred. Ripe females are noticeable by the development of pinkish colouration around the abdominal region while the ventral fins become bluish although courtship is initiated by territorial males. The eggs normally hatch within 3-5 days with the larvae either remaining attached to the roof of the spawning site or moved into a shallow

vibeLine.casual: Extreme blackwater micro-cichlid — demands acidic soft water below pH 6.
vibeLine.pro: Biotoecus opercularis. Obligate blackwater dwarf cichlid; pH 5–6, minimal hardness, advanced maintenance.

flavorText.casual: One of the most demanding dwarf cichlids in the hobby. Requires extremely soft, acidic water — pH 5 to 6, near-zero hardness — and won't thrive outside these parameters. Tiny, delicate, and intolerant of water chemistry shortcuts. Research-level commitment required.
flavorText.pro: Biotoecus opercularis is a diminutive blackwater cichlid (4–5 cm) requiring pH 5–6 and near-zero general hardness — highly sensitive to elevated mineral content. Sand-dwelling; males maintain small territories on open substrate. RO water with botanical conditioning (leaves, peat) essential. 25–30°C. Rarely bred in captivity due to extreme water chemistry requirements. Not suitable for community tanks or beginners.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unidentified); zooplankton (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Aggressive freshwater puffer with glowing red eyes — males fight.
vibeLine.pro: Carinotetraodon lorteti. Aggressive freshwater puffer; pronounced sexual dimorphism, intense male territoriality.

flavorText.casual: Those red eyes aren't just for show — this puffer backs them up with serious aggression. Males are fiercely territorial and will attack anything that enters their space, including other puffers. Smaller than most puffers but compensates with attitude. Needs dense planting and broken sightlines to manage aggression.
flavorText.pro: Carinotetraodon lorteti is a small freshwater puffer (5–6 cm) with pronounced sexual dimorphism — males exhibit red eye coloration and intense territorial aggression. pH 6–7.5, 24–28°C. Males cannot cohabitate; maintain single males with multiple females in densely planted tanks. Continuous tooth growth requires hard-shelled food. Less social than C. travancoricus; species-only setups recommended. Ambush predator; provide dense vegetation.

diet.fooditems: Tetraodontids lack true teeth, the jawbone itself being modified into four fused toothlike structures. These grow continuously at a surprising rate, so offer regular meals of shelled invertebrates such as snails, small crab legs, cockles, etc., in order to maintain them at a reasonable length. There

reproduction.comments: Unrecorded. The congener C. lorteti is known to scatter its eggs among vegetation, with the male guarding the brood during incubation.

vibeLine.casual: Rare Borneo puffer — striped, aggressive, and seldom available.
vibeLine.pro: Carinotetraodon salivator. Rare Bornean puffer; striped patterning, aggressive, limited captive availability.

flavorText.casual: Even rarer than its redeye cousin — a Borneo endemic that barely shows up in the trade. Same puffer attitude dialled up further. Striped pattern distinguishes it from other Carinotetraodon species. Requires the same hard-shelled diet for dental maintenance and should be kept species-only due to aggression levels.
flavorText.pro: Carinotetraodon salivator is a rarely imported Bornean endemic puffer distinguished by longitudinal striping. Highly aggressive — exceeds C. lorteti in territorial behaviour. pH 6–7, 24–28°C. Requires hard-shelled prey for continuous dental wear. Limited captive breeding data available; most specimens wild-caught. Species-only maintenance recommended. Dense planting and visual barriers essential. Advanced species requiring prior puffer-keeping experience.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified small cyprinids, unidentified bettas)

reproduction.spawningTrait: This species has been bred in aquaria. A group of 4 fish spawned in a length of 3″ diameter tubing, with a male fish guarding the eggs over the 3 day
reproduction.comments: This species has been bred in aquaria. A group of 4 fish spawned in a length of 3″ diameter tubing, with a male fish guarding the eggs over the 3 day incubation period , after which no further parental care was observed. The fry became free swimming after 7 days. Presumably they were raised on small live foods.

vibeLine.casual: Ambush predator with a mouth wider than its body — motionless.
vibeLine.pro: Chaca chaca. Extreme ambush predator; enormous gape, near-zero locomotion, swallows fish whole.

flavorText.casual: Sits motionless on the substrate for days, looking like a piece of bark. Then something swims too close to that cavernous mouth and disappears. Eats fish up to two-thirds its own body length. Not a community fish — a living trap you feed on a schedule.
flavorText.pro: Chaca chaca is an extreme ambush predator exhibiting near-total immobility, relying on crypsis and an enormous gape to engulf prey. Capable of consuming fish up to two-thirds its own body length. Maintain singly in species-specific setups with dim lighting and minimal current. Feed sparingly on live or fresh-dead fish. Rarely bred; egg-depositor.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified worms); zooplankton (unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Large jumping characin that leaps to grab food above water.
vibeLine.pro: Copeina guttata. Large characin; jumps above water to capture prey.

flavorText.casual: Related to the famous splash tetra but bigger and bolder — will jump clear of the water to grab food or insects above the surface. Males are colourful with red-spotted fins. Needs a tight-fitting lid because these jumps aren't subtle. Active, mid-level schooling fish that does well in groups.
flavorText.pro: Copeina guttata is a large characin (10–12 cm) exhibiting aerial feeding behaviour — capable of leaping above the water surface to capture terrestrial prey. Males display red-spotted finnage during breeding. Tight-fitting lids essential. pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C. Maintain in groups of 5+. Active swimmer requiring spacious, well-covered tanks. Related to Copella spp. (splash tetras) but lacks their leaf-spawning specialization. Accepts most prepared and live foods readily.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans, unspecified worms)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Jumps out of the water to lay eggs on overhanging leaves.
vibeLine.pro: Copella arnoldi. Remarkable aerial spawner; deposits eggs above waterline on leaves.

flavorText.casual: One of the most unusual spawning behaviors in freshwater fish — the pair leaps together to stick eggs onto a leaf above the water, then the male splashes them to keep them moist until they hatch. You need overhanging cover and a secure lid (they jump).
flavorText.pro: Copella arnoldi exhibits unique aerial oviposition: pairs leap to deposit adhesive eggs on overhanging surfaces, with males subsequently splashing water onto the clutch until hatching. Requires overhanging vegetation or tilted surfaces and a tight-fitting lid. Soft, acidic water preferred. Males develop extended dorsal finnage.

diet.fooditems: Tank bred specimens will happily take most foods, but wild caught fish may demand live or at the least frozen foods. A variety of foods is vital, as it has been reported that hole-in-the-head disease can be caught by poorly fed Pikes. Feed live and frozen foods regularly, even to fish which will acc

reproduction.comments: It has been noted by a number of hobbyists that spawning Crenicichla Compressiceps may be very difficult without target fish in the aquarium . Target fish serve as targets for cichlids aggression and, in so doing, help diffuse intraspecific aggression and cement pair bonds. Target fish for cichlids may range from other individuals of the same species , to other cichlids, to even some non-cichlids. Condition a pair of Compressiceps by feeding high quality live or frozen foods. As females ripen, t

vibeLine.casual: Small pike cichlid — predatory instincts in a manageable ten-centimetre body.
vibeLine.pro: Crenicichla compressiceps. Dwarf crenicichline; predatory, pair-bonding cave-spawner, manageable size under 10 cm.

flavorText.casual: All the predatory intensity of a pike cichlid compressed into a body that stays around 10 cm. Still eats anything it can swallow, still territorial during spawning, but it fits in a tank that a full-sized Crenicichla never would. Keep in pairs with robust tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Crenicichla compressiceps is a dwarf crenicichline reaching ~10 cm, retaining the predatory behaviour of larger congeners in a manageable size. Piscivorous toward smaller fish. Cave-spawning pair-bonder; both parents defend territory aggressively during breeding. Soft to neutral, slightly acidic water (pH 6–7, 22–27°C). Provide caves and sight breaks. Feed live and frozen meaty foods.

diet.fooditems: Piscivorous predator feeding on small fish and large invertebrates. In aquaria: live/frozen fish, prawns, earthworms, high-quality carnivore pellets.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Large ambush predator — if it fits, it gets eaten.
vibeLine.pro: Crenicichla marmorata. Large creniciclid pike cichlid; obligate piscivore, ambush predation strategy.

flavorText.casual: This is a serious predatory fish that will eat anything it can physically fit in its mouth. Grows large, hunts by ambush, and needs a tank built around its needs — not something you add to an existing community. Experienced keepers only. Plan tankmates very carefully.
flavorText.pro: Crenicichla marmorata is a large pike cichlid (30+ cm) and obligate piscivore employing ambush predation from concealed positions. Requires spacious, well-filtered tanks with hiding spots (driftwood, rock caves). Soft to neutral water (pH 6–7.5, 24–28°C). Extremely aggressive toward inappropriate tankmates. House with robust fish too large to ingest. Not suitable for standard community setups. Advanced husbandry required.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified); plants (unidentified plants); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Unrecorded in captivity. Observations by local fishermen in the Kapuas River suggest it to be non-migratory, spawning in April and May, with no parent
reproduction.comments: Unrecorded in captivity. Observations by local fishermen in the Kapuas River suggest it to be non-migratory, spawning in April and May, with no parental care.

vibeLine.casual: Bold black tiger bars on a golden predator — grows large.
vibeLine.pro: Datnioides microlepis. Large datnoid; bold vertical barring, brackish-tolerant predator, significant space requirements.

flavorText.casual: Those thick black vertical bars on gold make this one of the most visually striking predatory fish available. Grows large and needs a tank to match — we're talking 500L minimum for adults. Brackish-tolerant but adapts to freshwater. Shy initially, but once settled it dominates the tank with presence alone.
flavorText.pro: Datnioides microlepis is a large predatory datnoid (30+ cm) with bold black vertical bars on golden-yellow base coloration. Brackish-tolerant; adapts to freshwater but benefits from low-level salt addition (SG 1.002–1.005). pH 6.5–7.5, 22–28°C. Requires spacious tanks (500L+). Shy and reclusive when juvenile — provide cover. Piscivorous; feed appropriately sized live/frozen prey transitioning to pellets. Slow grower; long-lived (10+ years).

diet.fooditems: An efficient, largely piscivorous, predator with highly protrusible mouthparts. In the aquarium, juveniles can be offered chironomid larvae (bloodworm), small earthworms, chopped prawn, and suchlike, while adults will accept strips of fish flesh, whole prawns, mussels, live river shrimp, larger eart

reproduction.spawningTrait: Unrecorded. Captive breeding efforts are apparently underway in Thailand and Cambodia, but with little success to date.
reproduction.comments: Unrecorded. Captive breeding efforts are apparently underway in Thailand and Cambodia, but with little success to date.

vibeLine.casual: Even larger tiger fish — same bold bars, bigger tank.
vibeLine.pro: Datnioides pulcher. Largest Datnioides; exceeds D. microlepis in adult size.

flavorText.casual: Same genus, same stunning tiger bars, but this one gets even bigger than its Indonesian cousin. Adults are imposing fish that need enormous tanks and deep pockets for the food bill. Rarer in the trade and often commands a premium price. Trophy predator for dedicated large-fish keepers.
flavorText.pro: Datnioides pulcher is the largest Datnioides species, exceeding D. microlepis in maximum size (40+ cm). Bold vertical barring identical in pattern but broader. Brackish-tolerant predator; same care parameters as D. microlepis but with increased space requirements (600L+ minimum). pH 6.5–7.5, 22–28°C. Less frequently available in trade; commands higher prices. Same slow growth, long lifespan, and shy juvenile behaviour. Advanced predator husbandry required.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore feeding on insects, algae, plant matter, and small invertebrates in the wild. In aquaria: quality flakes/pellets, frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, blanched spinach.

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: The sleek torpedo barb with a red racing stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Dawkinsia denisonii. Active, cool-water schooling barb; needs swimming length.

flavorText.casual: Fast, streamlined, and boldly marked with a red lateral stripe and a flash of gold on the nose. They're active schoolers that need a long tank and cooler water. Stunning in a group but not for small setups.
flavorText.pro: Dawkinsia denisonii is a subtropical barb preferring cooler, well-oxygenated water (15–25°C). Needs a long tank for its active, schooling nature. Keep six or more. An IUCN-listed species; buy captive-bred when possible.

diet.fooditems: Checkerboards will probably accept dry foods, especially sinking cichlid pellets. If that is the case, feed a good quality sinking pellet as staple. Vary their diet with vegetarian or spirulina flakes and plenty of frozen foods. To bring Checkerboards into breeding condition, live foods are highly r

reproduction.comments: Dicrossus Filamentosa are relatively easy to breed if you can provide them with premium water conditions. This means very acidic , soft, highly quality water. RO water is nearly mandatory for breeding these fish, unless your tap water is around pH 4.5.

vibeLine.casual: Extreme blackwater dwarf cichlid — pH below 6 is non-negotiable.
vibeLine.pro: Dicrossus filamentosus. Obligate blackwater cichlid; pH 4.5–6, near-zero hardness, advanced maintenance only.

flavorText.casual: The checkerboard pattern along the flanks is distinctive, but the real story is the water chemistry. This fish needs pH 4.5–6 and virtually zero hardness — conditions that require RO water and careful management. Not a community fish for tap water tanks. Stunning when conditions are right.
flavorText.pro: Dicrossus filamentosus is an obligate blackwater dwarf cichlid requiring pH 4.5–6.0 and near-zero hardness. Substrate-spawner; both parents participate in brood care. Males develop lyrate caudal extensions. Extremely sensitive to dissolved minerals and organic waste — pristine, tannin-stained water mandatory. RO with botanical acidification required in most regions. Not compatible with standard community fish due to extreme parameter requirements.

diet.fooditems: others (ants); zoobenthos (beetles, mayflies)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Small nocturnal catfish wearing a honeycomb pattern over its body.
vibeLine.pro: Duringlanis perugiae. Small auchenipterid; honeycomb patterning, nocturnal, peaceful in community setups.

flavorText.casual: A compact catfish with a distinctive honeycomb or reticulated pattern on its body. Nocturnal and secretive — provide driftwood caves and dim lighting. Peaceful and won't bother tankmates, but you'll mostly see it at feeding time or after dark.
flavorText.pro: Duringlanis perugiae is a small auchenipterid catfish (~8 cm) with distinctive reticulated patterning. Nocturnal; requires refuge sites (driftwood hollows, caves). Omnivorous; feed sinking foods after lights-out. Peaceful in community settings. Internal fertiliser — females store sperm and deposit fertilised eggs on substrate. Soft to neutral water preferred.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified, unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Snake-shaped escape artist — will find any gap in your lid.
vibeLine.pro: Erpetoichthys calabaricus. Polypterid reedfish; atmospheric air-breather, notorious aquarium escape behaviour.

flavorText.casual: Looks like a snake, moves like a snake, and escapes like a snake. If there's a gap in your lid — even one you think is too small — this fish will find it and end up on your floor. Breathes atmospheric air so it'll survive briefly outside water. Seal every single opening. Peaceful, nocturnal, and surprisingly social with its own kind.
flavorText.pro: Erpetoichthys calabaricus is a polypterid reedfish with elongate, serpentiform body and functional lungs for atmospheric respiration. Notorious escape artist — exploits any lid gap due to combination of flexibility, air-breathing capability, and exploratory behaviour. pH 6.5–7.5, 22–28°C. Nocturnal predator of small invertebrates and fish. Seal all tank openings completely. Gregarious; maintain in groups of 3+. Reaches 35+ cm. Peaceful with tankmates exceeding gape size.

diet.fooditems: nekton (fish scales); zoobenthos (unidentified)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: A scale-eating predator — species-only school or nothing. 🦷
vibeLine.pro: Exodon paradoxus. Obligate lepidophage; large conspecific schools only.

flavorText.casual: This one actively rips scales off tankmates. Not nippy — predatory. The only way to keep them is a big group of their own kind in a large tank, where aggression spreads thin. Fascinating but absolutely not community-safe.
flavorText.pro: Exodon paradoxus is an obligate scale-eater unsafe with any heterospecific tankmates. Maintain in schools of 12+ in spacious tanks to diffuse intraspecific aggression. Feeding is straightforward — they take pellets, frozen, and live foods readily. Breeding is egg-scattering; rarely achieved in captivity due to conspecific predation on eggs.

diet.fooditems: zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified worms)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Not thought to have been achieved in captivity. One scientific study suggests that being kept in the confines of an aquarium causes the sexually-defin
reproduction.comments: Not thought to have been achieved in captivity. One scientific study suggests that being kept in the confines of an aquarium causes the sexually-defining patterns of electric impulses to become less clearly defined or even reversed. This means the fish cannot recognise members of the opposite sex , and inhibits spawning .

vibeLine.casual: Uses electric fields to "see" — that trunk senses, not breathes.
vibeLine.pro: Gnathonemus petersii. Weakly electric mormyrid; chin appendage for electrolocation, highly sensitive species.

flavorText.casual: That trunk-like chin extension isn't a nose — it's a sensory organ packed with electroreceptors. Generates weak electric fields and reads distortions to navigate, find food, and communicate with others of its kind. Extremely sensitive to water quality and medications. Shy, nocturnal, and needs sandy substrate to probe for food.
flavorText.pro: Gnathonemus petersii is a mormyrid generating pulse-type electric organ discharge for electrolocation and communication. The elongated chin (Schnauzenorgan) contains dense electroreceptor arrays for substrate-probing prey detection. pH 6–7.2, 22–28°C. Extremely sensitive to copper, medications, and water chemistry instability. Requires sandy substrate, dim lighting, and dark refuges. Conspecifics communicate via EOD; maintain singly or in groups of 5+ to prevent bullying pairs. Advanced species.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unspecified finfishes); zoobenthos (unspecified insects, unspecified crustaceans)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Metre-long electric predator — do not buy unless you own a pond.
vibeLine.pro: Gymnarchus niloticus. Massive electric knifefish exceeding 100cm; predatory, requires pond-scale housing.

flavorText.casual: Grows over a metre long, generates electric fields, eats other fish, and needs housing most people simply cannot provide. This is a pond fish sold to aquarium keepers who don't know better. If you don't have a 2000L+ system or an indoor pond, walk away. Impressive animal — terrible impulse purchase.
flavorText.pro: Gymnarchus niloticus is an African electric knifefish exceeding 100 cm and generating continuous wave-type EOD for electrolocation. Obligate piscivore. Requires pond-scale housing (2000L+ minimum for adults) — wholly inappropriate for standard aquaria despite juvenile availability in trade. pH 6.5–7.5, 23–28°C. Species-only; highly aggressive toward conspecifics and heterospecifics. Powerful predator capable of consuming substantial prey items. Do not acquire without dedicated infrastructure.

diet.fooditems: detritus (unidentified vegetative detritus); nekton (astyanax, ctenobrycon, odontostilbe); plants (unidentified hard fruit & seeds, unidentified)

reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Banded electric knifefish — territorial nocturnal predator with attitude.
vibeLine.pro: Gymnotus carapo. Weakly electric gymnotiform; nocturnal predator, pronounced territorial aggression toward conspecifics.

flavorText.casual: Generates weak electric fields for navigation and communication, hunts at night, and does not appreciate company — especially its own kind. Banded pattern along a knife-shaped body. Hides in tubes or dense cover during the day and emerges after lights-out to feed. Territorial enough that one per tank is the usual rule.
flavorText.pro: Gymnotus carapo is a weakly electric gymnotiform generating pulse-type EOD for electrolocation and territorial communication. Nocturnal ambush predator; provide tubes and dense cover as daytime refuges. pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C. Aggressively territorial toward conspecifics — house singly unless tank is very large with visual barriers. Reaches 30–40 cm. Feeds on live/frozen invertebrates and small fish. Moderate care difficulty but requires understanding of electric fish husbandry.

diet.fooditems: Unfussy, and will accept most live, frozen and dried foods. Try to make sure it receives some greenstuffs in its diet, as it is reported to feed quite heavily on plant matter in nature. To find high quality fish foods, click here .

reproduction.comments: Not thought to have been bred in the hobby. It’s probably an egg -scatterer.

vibeLine.casual: Fast schooling characin with a forked red tail — needs space.
vibeLine.pro: Hemiodus gracilis. Active schooling characin; forked red tail, needs space.

flavorText.casual: A torpedo-shaped schooling fish that needs room to run — fast, active, and always on the move. The forked red tail catches the eye when a group of them streams across a long tank. Peaceful but skittish; keep in groups of six or more with plenty of open swimming space and avoid loud, aggressive tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Hemiodus gracilis is an active schooling characin reaching 15+ cm, characterised by its forked red caudal fin. Requires spacious, elongated tanks for continuous swimming behaviour. pH 6–7, 23–27°C. Maintain in groups of 6+ — solitary specimens become stressed and reclusive. Peaceful but flighty; pair with calm, similarly-sized tankmates. Omnivorous; accepts prepared and live foods. Strong jumper; secure lid required. Benefits from moderate current.

diet.fooditems: Homaloptera spp. are specialised grazers feeding on biofilm , small crustaceans, insect larvae and other invertebrates. In captivity some sinking dried foods may be accepted but regular meals of live or frozen Daphnia , Artemia , bloodworm , etc., are essential for the maintenance of good health, an

reproduction.spawningTrait: Presumably a seasonal spawner in nature but nothing has been recorded in aquaria.
reproduction.comments: Presumably a seasonal spawner in nature but nothing has been recorded in aquaria.

vibeLine.casual: Hillstream specialist that needs serious current and cooler temperatures.
vibeLine.pro: Homaloptera orthogoniata. Rheophilic hillstream loach; demands high flow and cooler water.

flavorText.casual: A flat-bodied hillstream loach that grips rocks in fast flow. Needs a dedicated hillstream setup with strong powerheads, cool water (20–24°C), and clean, oxygen-rich conditions. Not for standard tropical tanks — this is a specialist commitment.
flavorText.pro: Homaloptera orthogoniata is an obligate rheophile requiring high-flow, well-oxygenated water at 20–24°C. Feeds on biofilm and aufwuchs from rock surfaces. Advanced husbandry — sensitive to warm, low-oxygen conditions. Not compatible with standard community aquaria. Rarely bred in captivity; egg-depositor on stones in current.

diet.fooditems: Except when juvenile , these prefer a meaty diet. This can consist of live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , chopped mussel and prawn and sinking dried foods. Algae and other vegetable matter is not often taken, but the occasional slice of cucumber or courgette might be nibbled at.

reproduction.comments: Quite often bred in the hobby. It will sometimes spawn in the community tank if conditions are to its liking. If you want to raise a full brood of these, and exercise a degree of control over the situation, it’s best to set up a separate spawning tank . The best way to obtain a pair is to buy a group of at least 6 fish and grow them on together, so something around 36″ x 12″ x 12″ will be needed (along with a lot of cash!). If the fish are sexable, try to achieve a ratio of 2-3 females per male.

vibeLine.casual: The striking black-and-white jewel of the pleco world.
vibeLine.pro: Hypancistrus zebra. Prized warmwater pleco; protected, carnivorous, parameter-sensitive.

flavorText.casual: Boldly striped like its namesake and highly sought after, this little pleco is a true showpiece. It needs warm, soft, spotless water and unlike most plecos prefers meaty foods. A rewarding fish for careful keepers.
flavorText.pro: Hypancistrus zebra demands warm (26-30°C), soft, pristine water and a carnivorous diet, not algae. A CITES-protected species; buy only captive-bred stock. A cave-brooder; males guard eggs in tight tubes.

diet.fooditems: nekton (unidentified); zoobenthos (unidentified, unidentified prawns)

reproduction.spawningTrait: Open-water egg scatterer no parental care
reproduction.comments: Information arriving soon

vibeLine.casual: Larger transparent catfish — same ghostly look, needs a proper school.
vibeLine.pro: Kryptopterus bicirrhis. Larger glass catfish species (12–15 cm); translucent, schooling, midwater swimmer.

flavorText.casual: Similar transparent body to the more common K. vitreolus but grows noticeably larger (12–15 cm). Needs a school of six-plus and gentle flow — singles or pairs stress and fade. Peaceful midwater fish that looks unreal in a planted tank.
flavorText.pro: Kryptopterus bicirrhis is the larger species frequently confused with K. vitreolus in trade, reaching 12–15 cm. Obligate schooler requiring groups of six-plus; isolated specimens exhibit chronic stress. Midwater swimmer; prefers gentle current and subdued lighting. Egg-scatterer; rarely bred in captivity. Sensitive to poor water quality and abrupt parameter swings.

diet.fooditems: Unfussy. It relishes live and frozen foods such as bloodworm , earthworms etc., as well as sinking dried foods. Add food after lights out to ensure it receives its share.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Not thought to have been achieved in aquaria. Fertilisation occurs internally.
reproduction.comments: Not thought to have been achieved in aquaria. Fertilisation occurs internally.

vibeLine.casual: Striking spotted predator that hunts at night and hides all day.
vibeLine.pro: Liosomadoras oncinus. Predatory auchenipterid; jaguar-spotted, nocturnal, consumes smaller fish readily.

flavorText.casual: One of the most visually striking catfish available — bold black-on-gold jaguar spots. But it's a nocturnal predator that will eat anything it can swallow. Beautiful to own, but plan tankmates accordingly and don't keep with small fish.
flavorText.pro: Liosomadoras oncinus is a predatory auchenipterid reaching 15–20 cm with distinctive melanistic spotting. Strictly nocturnal; requires caves and dim conditions. Consumes fish and invertebrates small enough to ingest. Internal fertilisation typical for auchenipterids. Maintain with robust, similarly-sized tankmates. Soft to neutral, warm water.

ecology.comments: Native to clearwater creeks and heavily vegetated margins of the lower Ucayali basin in Peru. Highly gregarious and social.
ecology.biotope: Clearwater creeks and heavily vegetated margins of the lower Ucayali basin

diet.fooditems: Strict biofilm and specialized soft algae grazer. Requires constant grazing surfaces or organic sinking supplements.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Strict biofilm and specialized soft algae grazer. Requires constant grazing surfaces or organic sinking supplements.

reproduction.comments: Adhesive egg layer on exposed plant substrates. Continuous breeder under highly pristine, mature conditions.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny striped vacuum that silently judges your algae situation.
vibeLine.pro: Otocinclus cocama. Highly sensitive to poor acclimation and requires established biofilm.

flavorText.casual: Looks adorable. Is actually a full-time employee. Will work tirelessly as long as there’s enough biofilm. Otherwise they will dramatically stare at you through the glass.
flavorText.pro: Prone to starvation in brand new tanks. Best introduced into mature, biofilm-rich systems. Excellent at controlling soft diatoms but not a replacement for proper husbandry.

ecology.comments: Highly adaptable freshwater dwarf shrimp. Appreciates leaf litter.
ecology.biotope: Slow-flowing streams and ponds in Taiwan, heavily vegetated with moss.
ecology.socialBehavior: Extremely peaceful. Appreciates being kept in groups.

diet.fooditems: biofilm, detritus, algae
diet.feedingPlaybook: Grazes constantly on biofilm and organic matter. Supplement with high-quality shrimp pellets or blanched vegetables.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense vegetation, mosses, and fine crevices.
reproduction.comments: Carries eggs under abdomen (berried) until they hatch into fully formed miniature shrimp.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny red janitors that make your tank feel complete.
vibeLine.pro: Neocaridina davidi. Hardy invertebrate that helps control biofilm and detritus.

flavorText.casual: Cute, busy, and surprisingly good at cleaning. They breed easily once they settle in.
flavorText.pro: Excellent cleanup crew for mature tanks. Sensitive to copper and sudden parameter changes. Best kept in groups with plenty of hiding places and biofilm.

ecology.comments: Neotenic salamander that remains aquatic throughout life. Requires cool, clean water.
ecology.biotope: Lake Xochimilco in Mexico. High altitude, cold, slow-moving water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Should be housed alone or in large tanks with same-sized axolotls. Will eat small tank mates.

diet.fooditems: worms, insects, small fish
diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed earthworms, bloodworms, or specialized axolotl pellets. Remove uneaten food quickly.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Broad-leafed plants or decorations to attach eggs.
reproduction.comments: Lays hundreds of jelly-coated eggs on plants. Cannibalistic if parents are not separated.

vibeLine.casual: The walking smile that turned the hobby into a cult.
vibeLine.pro: Ambystoma mexicanum. Cool-water amphibian that requires pristine, cold water and careful handling.

flavorText.casual: Weird, wonderful, and way more popular than they have any right to be. They look like they’re always smiling at you.
flavorText.pro: Requires cool, clean, well-cycled water (16–20°C). Sensitive to poor water quality and temperature spikes. Best kept alone or with similarly sized individuals.

hiddenLore: Axolotls are famous in Minecraft for their cute colors and healing powers. Searching 'minecraft' or 'cute' reveals their hidden pixelated aura.

ecology.comments: Often considered a pest but actually beneficial — eats decomposing plant matter, leftover food, and biofilm. Reproduces rapidly. Cannot damage healthy plants.
ecology.biotope: Cosmopolitan — found in almost any freshwater habitat worldwide. Extremely adaptable to diverse water conditions.
ecology.socialBehavior: Harmless detritivore. Populations self-regulate based on available food. Indicator of overfeeding if population explodes.

vibeLine.casual: The uninvited guest that showed up on a plant — actually helpful.
vibeLine.pro: Physella acuta. Cosmopolitan pulmonate; prolific hitchhiker, efficient detritus and biofilm consumer.

flavorText.casual: You didn't buy it — it arrived on a plant and now there are fifty. Before you panic, consider that they're eating decaying matter, leftover food, and biofilm you didn't know was there. Population tracks food supply: overfeed your tank and they boom; cut back and they stabilise. Not a pest if you control inputs.
flavorText.pro: Physella acuta is a cosmopolitan pulmonate snail and the most common aquarium hitchhiker, typically introduced on live plants. Prolific asexual and sexual reproduction; population density directly correlates with available organic matter. Efficient detritus processor and biofilm grazer. Tolerates 15–28°C, pH 6.5–8. Population control achieved through feeding reduction rather than removal. Serves as indicator species for overfeeding. Harmless to living plant tissue.

ecology.comments: One of the hardiest aquarium plants. Attaches to hardscape with rhizoids. Trim regularly to prevent lower portions dying from light deprivation.
ecology.biotope: Southeast Asian streams attached to rocks and driftwood in shaded areas. Extremely adaptable moss that thrives in low to moderate light.
ecology.socialBehavior: Provides shelter for shrimp fry and small fish. Excellent biofilm grazing surface.

vibeLine.casual: The unkillable moss — grows anywhere, on anything, always.
vibeLine.pro: Taxiphyllum barbieri. Ubiquitous moss; extremely hardy, attaches to anything.

flavorText.casual: If this moss dies, something is seriously wrong with your tank. It grows in any light, any temperature, any water chemistry, on any surface. Perfect fry cover, shrimp grazing ground, and general-purpose green texture. Trim it or it becomes a tangled mess — but it won't die on you.
flavorText.pro: Taxiphyllum barbieri is the most widely cultivated aquarium moss and among the hardiest aquatic plants available. Tolerates pH 5–8, 15–30°C, and lighting from low to high. Attaches to hardscape, substrate, and equipment indiscriminately. Propagates by fragmentation — any piece re-establishes growth. Provides fry refuge and biofilm grazing surface for shrimp. Requires periodic trimming to prevent interior die-off from light exclusion. No CO₂ or fertilization required.

ecology.comments: Easy stem plant that develops red coloration under high light. Propagates by cuttings. Needs moderate light minimum; intense light for red color.
ecology.biotope: North American wetlands, stream margins, and shallow ponds. Grows emergent or submersed in soft to moderately hard water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Fast-growing stem plant that provides mid-to-background cover and nutrient export.

vibeLine.casual: Easy red-green stem plant — more light means redder leaves.
vibeLine.pro: Ludwigia repens. Hardy stem plant; red colour intensifies with light.

flavorText.casual: One of the easiest ways to get red colour into a planted tank without advanced gear. Under low light it stays olive-green; crank up the intensity and leaves flush red-orange on top. Grows fast, propagates from cuttings, and tolerates a wide range of conditions. Trim and replant tops for bushier growth.
flavorText.pro: Ludwigia repens is an onagraceous stem plant exhibiting light-dependent anthocyanin production — submersed leaves transition from green to red-orange under high PAR values. Iron supplementation enhances red coloration. Propagates by stem cuttings; replant tops for branching. pH 6–8, 20–28°C. Moderate to high light for red coloration; grows acceptably under low light in green form. CO₂ beneficial but not required. One of the most accessible red-spectrum plants for beginners.

ecology.comments: Needs calcium for shell health (KH 3+ recommended). Lays pink egg clusters above the waterline. Multiple color varieties (gold, blue, ivory, purple). Sensitive to copper.
ecology.biotope: South American rivers and wetlands (Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay). Prefers calm waters with vegetation.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful algae and detritus grazer. Does NOT eat healthy plants (unlike other Pomacea species). Good community member.

vibeLine.casual: Big colourful snail that does not eat your live plants.
vibeLine.pro: Pomacea bridgesii. Large snail; does not eat live plants, multiple colour morphs.

flavorText.casual: Despite what you've heard about apple snails destroying planted tanks — this species doesn't eat live plants. It grazes algae, biofilm, and decaying matter only. Comes in gold, blue, purple, ivory, and more. Lays pink egg clutches above the waterline. Needs calcium-rich water for shell health.
flavorText.pro: Pomacea bridgesii is a large ampullariid snail (5–6 cm) that — critically — does not consume living plant tissue, distinguishing it from destructive Pomacea congeners. Grazes biofilm, algae, and decaying organic matter. Deposits pink egg clutches above the waterline; lower the water level 5+ cm to provide laying space. Requires calcium supplementation (pH 7–8, KH 4+) for shell integrity. Multiple colour morphs: gold, blue, purple, ivory, magenta.

ecology.comments: Livebearing snail that reproduces prolifically. Spends daylight buried in substrate. Excellent for planted tanks — turns over substrate without uprooting plants.
ecology.biotope: Native to East Africa and Southeast Asia but now cosmopolitan. Burrows in sandy/muddy substrates in rivers and lakes.
ecology.socialBehavior: Nocturnal burrower that aerates substrate. Prevents anaerobic gas pockets. Populations indicate tank health.

vibeLine.casual: Burrows through substrate all night — aerating sand is its whole job.
vibeLine.pro: Melanoides tuberculata. Burrowing thiarid; aerates substrate, parthenogenetic reproduction, nocturnal detritivore.

flavorText.casual: You'll barely see them during the day — they're buried in your substrate, tunnelling through sand and preventing anaerobic dead zones. Reproduces asexually, so one snail becomes many. Whether that's a feature or a problem depends on how much you're feeding. Excellent for planted tanks with sand substrates.
flavorText.pro: Melanoides tuberculata is a burrowing thiarid snail that aerates substrate through nocturnal tunnelling activity, preventing anaerobic pocket formation in sand beds. Reproduces via parthenogenesis — a single individual establishes a population. Population density correlates with organic load; population explosions indicate overfeeding. Tolerates 18–28°C, pH 7–8. Beneficial in planted tanks with fine substrates. Cannot be fully eradicated once established.

ecology.comments: Doubles biomass every 2-3 days under good conditions. Excellent nitrate/phosphate export. Must be thinned regularly or it will blanket the entire surface.
ecology.biotope: Cosmopolitan floating plant found on still or slow-moving freshwater worldwide. Extremely fast reproducing.
ecology.socialBehavior: Provides shade, reduces algae by outcompeting for nutrients. Cover for surface-dwelling fry. Can block light to plants below if not managed.

vibeLine.casual: Once it's in your tank, it's in your tank forever 😅
vibeLine.pro: Lemna minor. Prolific floater; extreme nitrate uptake, effectively impossible to remove.

flavorText.casual: Let's be honest — if you introduce duckweed, you will never fully remove it. Ever. A single frond missed during removal repopulates the surface within weeks. That said, it's one of the most powerful nitrate sponges available, grows under any light, and provides surface cover. Just go in with open eyes.
flavorText.pro: Lemna minor is a floating lemnaceous plant with extraordinary vegetative reproduction rate — doubling time under optimal conditions is 2–3 days. Effectively impossible to eradicate once introduced; a single surviving frond re-establishes full coverage. Extremely efficient nitrate and phosphate uptake; useful for nutrient export via regular harvesting. pH 5–9, 10–30°C. Blocks light to submerged plants if unmanaged. Consider containment rings to restrict coverage area.

ecology.comments: Extremely slow grower — takes months to carpet. Prefers stable water chemistry; susceptible to crypt melt when transplanted. Low to moderate light.
ecology.biotope: Sri Lankan streams and riverbanks, growing in shallow water under partial shade. The smallest Cryptocoryne species.
ecology.socialBehavior: Slow-growing foreground carpet plant. Undisturbed once established.

vibeLine.casual: Smallest crypt and slowest grower — patience is the whole game.
vibeLine.pro: Cryptocoryne parva. Smallest crypt; extremely slow, foreground carpet with patience.

flavorText.casual: If you want a crypt carpet, this is your only option — but you'll be waiting months to years for it to fill in. The smallest Cryptocoryne species and possibly the slowest-growing commonly sold plant. Root tabs help. Don't move it once planted — crypts melt when disturbed and this one recovers slowly.
flavorText.pro: Cryptocoryne parva is the smallest species in the genus (3–5 cm) and among the slowest-growing aquarium plants available. Forms foreground carpets over extended timeframes (6–18 months to fill). Unlike larger congeners, does not tolerate low light well — moderate lighting required. Root feeder; substrate fertilization essential. pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C. Prone to crypt melt when transplanted; recovery is slow. CO₂ supplementation improves growth rate but remains very slow regardless.

ecology.comments: One of the easiest aquarium plants. Attach to driftwood or rocks with thread/glue. Propagates via adventitious plantlets on leaves. Tolerates very low light.
ecology.biotope: Southeast Asian streams and waterfalls, growing attached to rocks and wood in shaded, fast-flowing water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Hardy epiphyte that provides cover at all tank levels. Rhizome must not be buried.

vibeLine.casual: Classic epiphyte — never bury the roots, it'll rot.
vibeLine.pro: Leptochilus pteropus. Epiphytic fern; attach to hardscape, propagates from leaf plantlets.

flavorText.casual: Same core rule as Anubias — attach to rock or wood, never plant in substrate. The black wiry roots are for gripping, not feeding. Propagates by growing baby plantlets right on the leaves, which is oddly charming. Tolerates low light, no CO₂ needed, and handles a huge range of parameters.
flavorText.pro: Leptochilus pteropus (syn. Microsorum pteropus) is an epiphytic polypodiaceous fern — attach rhizome and roots to hardscape; substrate burial causes rhizome rot. Propagates via adventitious plantlets produced on mature leaf margins and from the rhizome. Low to moderate light; CO₂ not required. pH 6–7.5, 20–28°C. Multiple cultivars available (narrow leaf, Windeløv, trident). Extremely hardy once established. Older leaves develop black sporangia — normal reproductive structures, not disease.

ecology.comments: Heavy root feeder — requires nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs. Can grow very large (30-50cm). Moderate to high light. Propagates via runners or adventitious plantlets.
ecology.biotope: South American rivers, marshes, and floodplains. Grows partially or fully submerged in nutrient-rich substrates.
ecology.socialBehavior: Large rosette plant serving as centerpiece. Provides broad-leaf cover for egg-laying species.

vibeLine.casual: The classic centrepiece plant — big, rooted, and hungry for nutrients.
vibeLine.pro: Echinodorus spp. Large rosette plant; heavy root feeder requiring nutrient-rich substrate.

flavorText.casual: Been a staple of planted tanks since the beginning for good reason — dramatic size, easy care, and a root system that anchors solidly in substrate. Feeds primarily through its roots, so nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs matter more than water column dosing. Gets big — plan your layout around it.
flavorText.pro: Echinodorus spp. are large rosette-forming plants and primary root feeders — nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or supplemented sand with root tabs) is essential for sustained growth. Reaches 30–50 cm depending on species/cultivar. Moderate to high light. Propagates via adventitious plantlets on emergent flower stalks. pH 6.5–7.5, 22–28°C. Prune older outer leaves to encourage new growth. Some species produce emersed leaves if allowed to breach the surface.

ecology.comments: Very slow grower but nearly indestructible. Attach to hardscape — never bury the rhizome. Low light tolerant. Prone to algae on old leaves due to slow growth rate.
ecology.biotope: West African rivers and streams, growing on rocks and wood in shaded areas with moderate current.
ecology.socialBehavior: Extremely hardy epiphyte. Broad leaves used by many cichlids as egg-laying substrate.

vibeLine.casual: Attach to rock or wood — never bury the rhizome, ever.
vibeLine.pro: Anubias barteri. Epiphytic aroid; attach to hardscape, never bury rhizome, low-light tolerant.

flavorText.casual: The one rule you absolutely cannot break — don't bury the thick green rhizome in substrate or it rots. Tie or glue it to rock or driftwood and forget about it. Grows slowly, tolerates low light, and is basically indestructible. Algae on the leaves is its only real enemy.
flavorText.pro: Anubias barteri is an epiphytic aroid — attach the rhizome to hardscape using thread, glue, or wedging. Burying the rhizome causes rot and death. Extremely slow-growing; low to moderate light sufficient (excess light promotes algae colonization on leaves). Propagates by rhizome division. pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C. Tolerates a wide range of conditions including low CO₂. Prone to green spot and black beard algae on older leaves due to slow growth rate.

ecology.comments: Fast grower that absorbs excess nutrients. Trailing roots can reach 30cm. Needs open top or space between lid and water. Propagates by runners.
ecology.biotope: Central and South American still waters, ponds, and slow rivers. Free-floating rosette with trailing roots.
ecology.socialBehavior: Provides shade and cover for surface-shy fish. Long roots serve as fry refuge. Competes with algae for nutrients.

vibeLine.casual: Floating plant with dangling roots — instant shade and fry cover.
vibeLine.pro: Limnobium laevigatum. Floating plant; surface shade, nutrient uptake via pendant roots.

flavorText.casual: Drop it on the surface and let it do its thing — round leaves float on top while roots dangle below providing cover for shy fish and fry. Grows fast under decent light, absorbs excess nutrients, and shades out algae below. Trim regularly or it'll block all your light.
flavorText.pro: Limnobium laevigatum is a floating hydrocharitaceous plant producing rosettes of round spongy leaves with pendant root systems. Rapid growth under moderate to high light; effective nutrient export through regular harvesting. Provides surface shade reducing algae growth below. Propagates via stolons producing daughter rosettes. pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C. Manage coverage to prevent excessive light blocking — remove excess weekly. Incompatible with strong surface agitation.

ecology.comments: Very easy grower. Sends out runners prolifically. Prefers moderate light and hard water (needs calcium). Can grow to surface and trail along it. Trim by pulling entire leaves, not cutting.
ecology.biotope: Cosmopolitan — found in temperate to tropical freshwater streams and lakes across Europe, Asia, Africa, and Australia.
ecology.socialBehavior: Tall background plant forming dense grass-like stands. Runner propagation creates natural-looking meadows.

vibeLine.casual: Classic background grass — runners spread fast, easy to grow.
vibeLine.pro: Vallisneria spiralis. Common vallisneriid; aggressive runner propagation, hardy background grass, root feeder.

flavorText.casual: The original background plant — long grass-like leaves that sway in the current, spreading by runners until it fills every available space. Virtually impossible to kill. Pull excess runners regularly or your entire substrate becomes a Vallisneria mat. Root tabs help but aren't strictly necessary.
flavorText.pro: Vallisneria spiralis is a common vallisneriid and one of the oldest plants in the aquarium hobby. Propagates aggressively via runners; regular thinning required to manage spread. Root feeder; tolerates low-tech conditions without CO₂ or intense lighting. pH 6.5–8.5, 18–28°C. Sensitive to glutaraldehyde-based algaecides (melts). Provides current-responsive movement and background density. Hardy across a wide range of water chemistries including hard, alkaline conditions.

ecology.comments: Hardy once established but prone to crypt melt when first planted or when parameters shift. Low to moderate light. Propagates by runners. Leave undisturbed once settled.
ecology.biotope: Sri Lankan streams growing in sandy/muddy substrates under moderate shade. Highly variable leaf color and shape.
ecology.socialBehavior: Mid-ground rosette plant providing natural cover. Multiple color variants available (green, brown, red, tropica).

vibeLine.casual: The easy crypt — multiple colours, low light, minimal fuss.
vibeLine.pro: Cryptocoryne wendtii. Hardy crypt; multiple colour forms, low-light tolerant.

flavorText.casual: The default recommendation when someone asks for an easy midground plant. Comes in green, brown, red, and bronze colour forms. Tolerates low light, doesn't need CO₂, feeds through roots. May melt when first planted — leave it alone and it grows back. Basically bulletproof once established.
flavorText.pro: Cryptocoryne wendtii is a Sri Lankan species and the most commonly cultivated Cryptocoryne, available in green, brown, red, and bronze forms. Tolerates low light and absence of CO₂ supplementation. Root feeder; benefits from nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs. pH 6–8, 22–28°C. Prone to crypt melt during acclimatization — do not remove; new growth emerges from intact root system. Propagates via runners. Established specimens are extremely hardy.

ecology.comments: Grows very tall (60-100cm+). Needs nutrient-rich substrate. Moderate to high light. Spreads aggressively via runners. Sometimes called Jungle Val.
ecology.biotope: North American lakes and rivers. Larger species growing in sandy substrates with moderate current.
ecology.socialBehavior: Tall, wide-leaved background plant forming dense stands. Provides excellent cover for taller tanks.

vibeLine.casual: Tall grass that sends runners everywhere — fills a background fast.
vibeLine.pro: Vallisneria americana. Large background grass; rapid runner propagation, reaches surface.

flavorText.casual: If you need a background filled in quickly, this delivers. Long ribbon-like leaves reach the surface and bend along it, creating a flowing canopy effect. Spreads aggressively via runners — you'll be thinning it out regularly. Easy, fast, and tolerant of most conditions. Just give it root tabs and stand back.
flavorText.pro: Vallisneria americana is a large vallisneriid reaching 100+ cm, suitable exclusively as a background plant. Propagates aggressively via runners; expect regular thinning to prevent overcrowding. Root feeder; benefits from substrate fertilization. pH 6.5–8.5, 18–28°C. Tolerates hard water well. Leaves float along surface creating a canopy effect. Incompatible with Excel/glutaraldehyde-based algaecides (Vallisneria sensitivity). Provides excellent cover for surface-dwelling fish.

ecology.comments: Slow to moderate grower with distinctive upward growth habit. Low to moderate light. Attach to hardscape. Trim to maintain flame shape.
ecology.biotope: Cultivar of Asian origin (exact provenance unclear). Grows upward in a flame-like pattern unlike typical mosses.
ecology.socialBehavior: Decorative midground moss for attaching to driftwood or rocks. Provides shelter for shrimp and fry.

vibeLine.casual: Grows straight up like green flames — striking on driftwood.
vibeLine.pro: Taxiphyllum sp. flame. Upward-growing moss; vertical frond orientation, attaches to hardscape.

flavorText.casual: Unlike most mosses that creep outward, this one grows vertically — fronds point straight up giving a flickering flame effect. Looks best attached to vertical driftwood surfaces or the tops of rocks. Trim the tops regularly to keep the flame shape defined and prevent it from flopping over.
flavorText.pro: Taxiphyllum sp. 'Flame' is distinguished by its vertical growth orientation — fronds extend upward rather than laterally, producing a flame-like visual effect. Attach to hardscape using thread or glue. Low to moderate light sufficient; CO₂ supplementation enhances density. pH 5.5–7.5, 20–28°C. Trim upper growth to maintain shape and prevent top-heavy collapse. Propagates by fragmentation. Best displayed on vertical surfaces or elevated hardscape features.

ecology.comments: One of the fastest-growing aquarium plants. Leaves change shape dramatically based on light (broad in low, lacy/feathered in high). Great for new tank cycling. Propagate by cuttings.
ecology.biotope: Southeast Asian marshes, rice paddies, and stream margins. Grows emergent or fully submerged.
ecology.socialBehavior: Fast-growing stem plant excellent for nutrient export. Feathery submerged leaves provide fry cover.

vibeLine.casual: Fast-growing lacy stem plant that fills empty space quickly.
vibeLine.pro: Hygrophila difformis. Fast-growing Acanthaceae stem plant; heterophyllous, lacy submerged leaf morphology.

flavorText.casual: One of those plants that grows so fast you'll be trimming it weekly — which is exactly what you want when starting a new tank and need something to outcompete algae. Submerged leaves develop that distinctive lacy, deeply-cut shape. Undemanding; handles most water conditions and moderate light. Propagates from cuttings effortlessly.
flavorText.pro: Hygrophila difformis is a fast-growing Acanthaceae stem plant exhibiting heterophylly — submerged leaves develop deeply pinnate, lacy morphology distinct from emersed form. Nutrient-hungry; effective at outcompeting algae in establishing tanks. Tolerates pH 6.5–7.5, 24–28°C, moderate to high lighting. Propagates readily from stem cuttings. Requires regular pruning to prevent shading of lower plants. Suitable for background placement. Undemanding regarding CO₂ supplementation but responds positively.

ecology.comments: Among the best algae-eating snails. Cannot breed in freshwater (larvae need brackish). Multiple species and patterns (zebra, tiger, olive). Needs calcium for shell health.
ecology.biotope: Tropical coastal and brackish rivers in Southeast Asia, Africa, and the Pacific. Migrate between fresh and brackish water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Excellent algae grazer. Peaceful. Deposits white sesame-seed-like eggs on hard surfaces but these will not hatch in freshwater.

vibeLine.casual: Best algae-grazing snail available — and can't overpopulate your tank.
vibeLine.pro: Neritina spp. Best algae-grazing snail; cannot breed in freshwater.

flavorText.casual: The gold standard for snail-based algae control. Grazes hard surfaces clean and — the real selling point — cannot breed in freshwater. Eggs require brackish water to hatch, so no population explosions. The downside: those white sesame-seed-sized eggs get deposited on rocks and wood and don't go away easily.
flavorText.pro: Neritina spp. are the most effective algae-grazing gastropods available for freshwater aquaria. Eggs require brackish or marine conditions for larval development — overpopulation impossible in freshwater. However, females deposit conspicuous white calcareous egg capsules on hardscape that persist indefinitely. pH 7–8.5, 22–28°C. Multiple species and shell patterns available (zebra, tiger, horned). Require adequate biofilm/algae — may starve in pristine tanks. Escape artists; tight-fitting lid recommended.

ecology.comments: Slow to moderate grower with triangular branching fronds. Attach to driftwood or mesh pads. Low to moderate light. Trim outer growth to maintain shape.
ecology.biotope: Southeast Asian streams growing on rocks in shaded, humid conditions. Branching pattern resembles Christmas tree shape.
ecology.socialBehavior: Decorative moss for hardscape. Denser than Java Moss. Good shrimp grazing surface.

vibeLine.casual: Branching moss that actually looks like tiny Christmas trees — festive texture.
vibeLine.pro: Vesicularia montagnei. Branching moss; triangular frond pattern, attaches to hardscape.

flavorText.casual: The branching pattern genuinely looks like miniature Christmas trees — one of the more visually structured mosses available. Attach to driftwood or mesh and let it fill in. Moderate growth rate; trim regularly to keep the shape tidy and prevent inner die-off from light deprivation.
flavorText.pro: Vesicularia montagnei produces triangular, branching fronds giving a characteristic conifer-like appearance. Attach to hardscape or mesh using thread or glue. Moderate growth rate under low to moderate light; CO₂ supplementation enhances density but is not required. pH 5.5–7.5, 20–28°C. Trim outer growth regularly to prevent interior browning from light exclusion. Propagates by fragmentation. Provides excellent fry cover and biofilm grazing surfaces for shrimp.

ecology.comments: Grows very slowly. Attach rhizome to rocks or wood — never bury. Tolerates low light. Leaves may develop iridescent sheen. Flowers underwater. Sensitive to parameter swings.
ecology.biotope: Borneo island streams and waterfalls, growing on rocks in fast-flowing, clear water. Rheophytic epiphyte.
ecology.socialBehavior: Slow-growing collector plant attached to hardscape. Many species/varieties with different leaf colors and textures.

vibeLine.casual: Borneo's answer to Anubias — dozens of forms, all epiphytic.
vibeLine.pro: Bucephalandra spp. Borneo endemic epiphyte; many forms, slow, attaches to hardscape.

flavorText.casual: Same concept as Anubias — attach to rock or wood, never bury the rhizome — but from Borneo with way more variety.
