Dozens of named forms with different leaf shapes, sizes, and iridescent colours. Slow-growing and low-demand, but pricier because many are wild-collected with limited supply.
flavorText.pro: Bucephalandra spp. are epiphytic aroids endemic to Borneo's stream habitats. Attach rhizome to hardscape; burial causes rot. Extremely slow growth rate. Low to moderate light; tolerates low CO₂. Numerous commercially named forms vary in leaf morphology, size, and iridescence. pH 5.5–7.5, 22–28°C. Propagates by rhizome division. Wild-collection pressure is a concern for some forms — seek tissue-cultured or propagated stock where available. Submerged flowers are characteristic of the genus.

ecology.comments: Flat-coiled shell. Available in many color morphs (red, blue, pink, leopard). Hermaphroditic — any two can breed. Great cleanup crew but can overpopulate.
ecology.biotope: Americas — widespread in ponds, ditches, and slow streams. Highly adaptable to any freshwater.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful scavenger eating algae, biofilm, decaying matter. Populations boom with excess food.

vibeLine.casual: Flat-coiled cleanup crew that multiplies when you overfeed.
vibeLine.pro: Planorbella duryi. Planorbid snail; population tracks feeding, efficient biofilm grazer.

flavorText.casual: That flat spiral shell is distinctive — and you'll see plenty of them if your tank has excess food or decaying matter. Good cleaners that eat algae, biofilm, and dead leaves. Population self-regulates based on food supply. Some keepers love them; others consider them pests. Control your feeding and they stay manageable.
flavorText.pro: Planorbella duryi is a planorbid pulmonate with a flat-coiled shell, functioning as an efficient biofilm and detritus grazer. Hermaphroditic; reproduces prolifically when organic matter is abundant. Population directly correlates with food availability — reduce feeding to control numbers. Tolerates 18–28°C, pH 7–8. Available in red, blue, and leopard colour morphs. Harmless to healthy plant tissue. Serves as a biological indicator of tank hygiene.

ecology.comments: Same care as Anubias barteri but stays small (5-10cm). Extremely slow grower. Low light tolerant. Popular for nano tanks and shrimp tanks.
ecology.biotope: West African streams, same habitat as Anubias barteri but smaller variety. Grows on rocks and submerged wood.
ecology.socialBehavior: Compact epiphyte ideal for foreground or midground on hardscape. Hardy and versatile.

vibeLine.casual: Tiny version of Anubias — same rules, foreground size 🌱
vibeLine.pro: Anubias barteri var. nana. Dwarf epiphyte; same care, foreground size.

flavorText.casual: Everything that applies to regular Anubias applies here — attach to hardscape, never bury the rhizome, low light is fine. Just smaller. Leaves stay compact enough for foreground placement or tucking into crevices on driftwood. Same slow growth, same algae vulnerability, same bulletproof resilience.
flavorText.pro: Anubias barteri var. nana is the dwarf cultivar of A. barteri, reaching 5–10 cm height. Identical culture requirements — epiphytic attachment to hardscape, rhizome must never be buried. Low to moderate light; slow growth rate makes it susceptible to algae colonization. Propagates by rhizome division. pH 6–7.5, 22–28°C. Excellent for foreground hardscape accents. CO₂ supplementation not required but accelerates growth marginally.

ecology.comments: Cannot breed in freshwater — larvae need brackish water. Excellent algae control. Grows to 5cm. May eat soft mosses if underfed. Hardy once acclimated but sensitive to copper.
ecology.biotope: Japanese mountain streams (Amano shrimp named after Takashi Amano). Clear, fast-flowing water over gravel.
ecology.socialBehavior: The premiere algae-eating shrimp. Peaceful, social, best in groups of 10+. Larger than most dwarf shrimp — less vulnerable to predation.

vibeLine.casual: Best algae-eating shrimp around — but won't breed in freshwater.
vibeLine.pro: Caridina multidentata. Premier freshwater algae consumer; larvae require brackish water for development.

flavorText.casual: If you want algae gone, these are the workhorses. A group will strip a tank of hair algae, film algae, and soft green algae faster than any other shrimp. The catch — they can't breed in your freshwater tank. Larvae need brackish water, so what you buy is what you've got. Copper-sensitive; avoid medications containing it.
flavorText.pro: Caridina multidentata is the most effective freshwater algae-consuming shrimp, capable of controlling filamentous and biofilm algae in planted tanks. Larvae require brackish conditions for metamorphosis — reproduction impossible in freshwater aquaria. Reaches 5–6 cm. Tolerates pH 6.5–7.5 and 20–28°C. Highly sensitive to copper and heavy metals; avoid copper-based medications. Maintain in groups of 5+ for effective algae control. Wild-caught and commercially bred specimens available.

ecology.comments: Inexpensive and often sold as feeder shrimp but makes a good cleanup crew. Fully breeds in freshwater. Short-lived (1-2 years). Hardy and undemanding.
ecology.biotope: Eastern North American freshwater streams, ponds, and vegetated shallows. Tolerant of wide parameter range.
ecology.socialBehavior: Transparent scavenger and algae grazer. Peaceful but may be eaten by larger fish. Group of 10+ recommended.

vibeLine.casual: Cheap, see-through, and surprisingly good at cleaning up leftovers.
vibeLine.pro: Palaemonetes paludosus. Transparent palaemonid; economical cleanup crew, often sold as feeder stock.

flavorText.casual: Usually sold as feeders for a few cents each, but they're perfectly capable tank cleaners in their own right. Completely transparent — you can watch food move through their digestive tract. Hardy, tolerant of a wide range of conditions, and useful for mopping up uneaten food in community tanks. Copper-sensitive like all shrimp.
flavorText.pro: Palaemonetes paludosus is a transparent palaemonid shrimp commonly sold as feeder stock but functional as cleanup crew. Tolerates 18–28°C and pH 7–8. Reaches 4–5 cm. Omnivorous scavenger consuming detritus, uneaten food, and biofilm. Sensitive to copper-based medications and sudden parameter shifts. Short-lived (1–2 years). Breeds in freshwater unlike Amano shrimp — larvae do not require brackish conditions. Inexpensive and replaceable.

ecology.comments: Moderate grower. Round succulent-like leaves. Grows well in low to high light. Can grow emergent. Propagate by cuttings. Also known as Brahmi in traditional medicine.
ecology.biotope: Pantropical wetland herb found in marshes, stream banks, and shallow water across India, Africa, and the Americas.
ecology.socialBehavior: Upright stem plant for mid-to-background. Compact leaf pattern provides moderate cover.

vibeLine.casual: Slow-growing medicinal stem plant with neat round little leaves.
vibeLine.pro: Bacopa monnieri. Slow-growing stem plant; round leaves, undemanding.

flavorText.casual: Grows slowly and neatly — no weekly hacking required. Round, fleshy leaves marching up a straight stem give it a tidy, orderly look in the midground. Tough enough to grow in low light without CO₂, just don't expect rapid growth. The same plant is used in Ayurvedic medicine under the name Brahmi. Forgiving and predictable.
flavorText.pro: Bacopa monnieri is a slow-growing Plantaginaceae stem plant with round, succulent leaves. Emersed form is the source of Brahmi in traditional Ayurvedic medicine. Tolerates wide parameters: pH 6–8, 22–28°C, low to moderate lighting. CO₂ supplementation optional but accelerates growth. Propagates from stem cuttings. Compact growth habit suitable for midground placement. One of the most undemanding stem plants available — tolerates neglect better than most Hygrophila species.

ecology.comments: The Opal morph is a selectively bred color variant of A. borellii. One of the hardiest and most peaceful Apistogramma species. Prefers soft, acidic water but adapts to moderate hardness.
ecology.biotope: South American streams and tributaries in southern Brazil, Argentina, and Paraguay. Leaf-litter zones with tannin-stained water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Harem-forming dwarf cichlid. Keep one male with 2-3 females. Males claim small territories but aggression is low compared to other Apistogramma.

diet.fooditems: Small invertebrates, insect larvae, worms; in aquarium: frozen/live foods (daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworm), sinking micro pellets

vibeLine.casual: Cool-tolerant apisto in opal blue — one of the hardier options.
vibeLine.pro: Apistogramma borellii Opal. Opal-blue colour form; unusually cool-tolerant for Apistogramma, pH 6–7.5.

flavorText.casual: One of the hardiest and most temperature-tolerant apistos — handles 20°C without complaint, which most congeners can't. The opal form is a blue colour variant that looks washed in soft light. A reasonable entry point to apistos if your water isn't extremely hard.
flavorText.pro: Apistogramma borellii Opal is a selectively bred colour form exhibiting opal-blue body coloration. Unusually tolerant of cooler temperatures (20–26°C) for the genus. Accepts pH 6–7.5 and moderate hardness — among the least demanding Apistogramma. Cave-brooder; females develop yellow brood-care coloration. Suitable as an entry species for Apistogramma husbandry.

ecology.comments: Often confused with Petitella georgiae (false rummy nose). Requires stable, mature tank with soft acidic water for best color. Sensitive to ammonia/nitrite.
ecology.biotope: Lower Amazon basin and Orinoco River tributaries. Blackwater streams with soft, acidic conditions and leaf litter.
ecology.socialBehavior: Tight-schooling tetra — must be kept in groups of 10+. Red nose coloration fades when stressed or water quality drops, making them excellent indicator fish.

diet.fooditems: Small invertebrates, zooplankton; in aquarium: micro pellets, flakes, frozen daphnia and brine shrimp

vibeLine.casual: The "true" rummy nose — tight school, red face, striped tail.
vibeLine.pro: Hemigrammus rhodostomus. True rummy nose; tighter schooling than Petitella georgiensis.

flavorText.casual: This is the original rummy-nose species (not the more common Petitella sold under the same name). Famous for incredibly tight schooling. The intensity of the red nose fades when stressed, so it doubles as a water-quality indicator.
flavorText.pro: Hemigrammus rhodostomus is the "true" rummy-nose, distinguished from the more widely traded Petitella georgiensis by subtle morphological differences. Demands soft, acidic, stable water for full coloration. Tight obligate schooler — keep ten-plus. Egg-scattering spawner; very soft water and dim lighting required for breeding success.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer in soft acidic water. No parental care

ecology.comments: Reaches 20-25cm. Requires strong current and oxygenation. Herbivorous tendency — may nibble soft-leaved plants. Jump-proof lid essential.
ecology.biotope: Rio Tapajós basin, Brazil. Fast-flowing clear water rivers over rocky substrates.
ecology.socialBehavior: Active swimmer. Can be nippy toward slow-moving tankmates. Keep singly or in groups of 6+ to distribute aggression.

vibeLine.casual: Large headstanding schooler from the Tapajos — needs swimming room.
vibeLine.pro: Leporinus vanzoi. Large anostomid from Rio Tapajos; schooling headstander requiring spacious tanks.

flavorText.casual: Swims at an angle with head pointed downward — classic headstander posture. Gets big enough that you'll need a seriously long tank to house a school properly. From the clear waters of the Rio Tapajos. Active swimmer that won't do well in cramped quarters or with overly aggressive tankmates. Group of five or more.
flavorText.pro: Leporinus vanzoi is a large anostomid (20+ cm) from the Rio Tapajos basin exhibiting characteristic head-down swimming posture. Schooling species requiring groups of 5+ in elongated tanks (150+ cm). pH 6–7.5, 20–25°C. Omnivorous with herbivorous tendency — may damage soft-leaved plants. Active swimmer requiring substantial open swimming space. Tankmates should be robust and non-aggressive. Intra-group hierarchy establishes with adequate numbers.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore with herbivorous tendency — algae, plant matter, seeds, small invertebrates. In aquaria: spirulina flakes, blanched vegetables, occasional frozen foods.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer — rarely bred in captivity

ecology.comments: Now reclassified as Corydoras agassizii by some authorities. Grows larger than typical Corydoras (7-8cm). Needs sand substrate to protect barbels.
ecology.biotope: Upper Amazon tributaries in Peru and Brazil. Sandy-bottomed streams with moderate current.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful schooling catfish. Keep in groups of 5+. Larger and deeper-bodied than most Corydoras but same behavior.

vibeLine.casual: The deep-bodied green cory that looks armored and hefty.
vibeLine.pro: Brochis agassizii. Larger, deeper-bodied relative of Corydoras; peaceful bottom-dweller.

flavorText.casual: Chunkier and deeper-bodied than typical corys, with a green sheen on their plates. Peaceful and hardy, they potter around the bottom just like their slimmer cousins. Give them sand and a group.
flavorText.pro: Brochis agassizii is a larger, deeper-bodied armored catfish closely related to Corydoras. Peaceful in groups over smooth sand. An egg-depositing spawner; keep six-plus. Sometimes reclassified within Corydoras.

diet.fooditems: Benthic omnivore sifting substrate for worms and insect larvae. In aquaria: sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp.

ecology.comments: Grows to 90cm+ and lives 15-20 years. Notorious jumper — requires heavy, sealed lid. Not a community fish. Needs pristine water quality. True monster fish commitment.
ecology.biotope: Amazon basin floodplains and slow rivers. Surface-dwelling predator in tannin-stained blackwater.
ecology.socialBehavior: Solitary surface predator. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics. Will eat any fish that fits in its mouth. Needs massive open swimming space.

vibeLine.casual: Jumps clear out of tanks — grows a metre, needs enormous housing.
vibeLine.pro: Osteoglossum bicirrhosum. Surface predator exceeding 60cm; powerful jumper, requires pond-scale adult housing.

flavorText.casual: Famous for leaping out of the water to snatch insects and birds off branches in the wild — and it will do the same in your living room if the lid isn't locked down. Grows 60–100 cm and needs housing most keepers can't provide. Juveniles are cheap and everywhere; responsible adults are in dedicated setups with reinforced lids.
flavorText.pro: Osteoglossum bicirrhosum is a surface-dwelling osteoglossid reaching 60–100 cm. Powerful jumper capable of clearing significant distances above water — reinforced, weighted lids mandatory. Juvenile availability belies extreme adult space requirements (1500L+ minimum). pH 6–7, 24–30°C. Obligate predator feeding on fish, insects, and terrestrial prey at the surface. Barbels on lower jaw serve as sensory organs. Long-lived (15+ years). Do not acquire without permanent large-scale housing plan.

diet.fooditems: Surface predator — fish, large insects, spiders. In aquaria: market shrimp, smelt, earthworms, carnivore pellets.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Paternal mouthbrooder — male incubates eggs in mouth 6-8 weeks

ecology.comments: Hardy and adaptable. Scissor-like tail movement is distinctive. Grows larger than most rasboras (13cm). Prefers slightly acidic soft water but very adaptable.
ecology.biotope: Southeast Asian streams and rivers in Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo. Clear to tannin-stained water with moderate flow.
ecology.socialBehavior: Active midwater schooling fish. Keep in groups of 8+. Peaceful but active — can stress slow-moving tankmates.

vibeLine.casual: The big, active rasbora whose forked tail flashes open and shut.
vibeLine.pro: Rasbora trilineata. Large active cyprinid (15 cm); needs ample swimming space.

flavorText.casual: Much bigger than most rasboras — up to 15 cm. Named for the deeply forked tail that opens and closes like scissors as they swim. Active and fast, they need a long tank with room to cruise. Peaceful despite the size.
flavorText.pro: Rasbora trilineata is one of the larger rasboras, reaching 15 cm, and requires spacious aquaria with open swimming areas. Hardy and tolerant of a range of conditions. Egg-scattering spawner; prolific when conditioned. Keep in schools of six-plus. Often underestimated in adult size at point of sale.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore — insects, zooplankton, small worms. In aquaria: flakes, micro pellets, frozen daphnia, brine shrimp.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants. No parental care

ecology.comments: Tiny (4cm) and extremely peaceful. Thread-like fin extensions are fragile — no fin-nippers allowed. Needs gentle filtration and dense planting. Micro foods essential.
ecology.biotope: Northern Australia and New Guinea. Slow-moving, heavily vegetated swamps and stream margins.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful, delicate schooling fish. Males display elaborate elongated fins to rivals. Keep in groups of 8+ with gentle tankmates only.

vibeLine.casual: The delicate little rainbow with impossibly long fin threads.
vibeLine.pro: Iriatherina werneri. Fragile nano rainbowfish; males display trailing filaments.

flavorText.casual: Males grow incredible long, filament-like fin extensions that trail behind them like silk ribbons. They're tiny, delicate, and need very gentle company — anything pushy will outcompete or nip them.
flavorText.pro: Iriatherina werneri is a small, fragile rainbowfish whose males develop extreme fin filaments. Needs calm, planted nano conditions with equally gentle tankmates. Males display to each other; an egg-depositor among fine plants.

diet.fooditems: Micro predator — tiny insects, zooplankton. In aquaria: baby brine shrimp, micro worms, crushed flake.

reproduction.spawningTrait: Egg scatterer in java moss over several days

ecology.comments: Grows to 12-15cm. Requires fine sand substrate (essential for feeding behavior). Sensitive to water quality — needs regular water changes. Peaceful community cichlid.
ecology.biotope: Amazon and Orinoco basin. Sandy-bottomed slow rivers and lagoons with submerged wood.
ecology.socialBehavior: Peaceful eartheater cichlid. Keep in groups of 5+. Sifts sand through gills constantly. Less territorial than most South American cichlids.

vibeLine.casual: Peaceful eartheater-relative with a pointed snout for sifting sand.
vibeLine.pro: Biotodoma cupido. Peaceful geophagine cichlid; sand-sifting behaviour, pointed rostrum, community-compatible temperament.

flavorText.casual: One of the mellower South American cichlids — sifts sand through its pointed snout looking for micro-organisms and doesn't bother its neighbours. Works well in community setups with tetras, corydoras, and other non-aggressive species. Subtle coloration that rewards close observation rather than demanding attention from across the room.
flavorText.pro: Biotodoma cupido is a peaceful geophagine cichlid reaching 10–12 cm with a characteristic pointed rostrum adapted for substrate-sifting. Sand-dweller requiring fine substrate to prevent buccal damage. pH 6–7.5, 20–25°C. Among the most community-compatible South American cichlids — minimal territorial aggression outside breeding. Maintain in groups or pairs with dither fish. Subtle iridescent coloration. Biparental substrate spawner. Suitable for mixed Amazonian community tanks with calm tankmates.

diet.fooditems: Eartheater — sifts sand for invertebrates. In aquaria: frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, fine sinking pellets.

ecology.comments: Males develop intense coloration with maturity. Needs swimming space and moderate current. Hardy once established. Prefers slightly alkaline water (pH 7-8).
ecology.biotope: Lake Tebera region, Papua New Guinea. Clear highland lake and its tributaries.
ecology.socialBehavior: Active schooling rainbowfish. Keep in groups of 6+. Males display vibrant colors when competing. Peaceful community fish.

vibeLine.casual: Iridescent New Guinean rainbowfish that colours up over time with patience.
vibeLine.pro: Chilatherina axelrodi. New Guinean melanotaeniid; develops coloration slowly, needs neutral soft water.

flavorText.casual: A New Guinea rainbowfish that looks dull in the bag but develops shimmering blues and silvers with maturity and good conditions. Needs patience — colour comes over months, not days. Active schooler that does well in groups of six-plus.
flavorText.pro: Chilatherina axelrodi is a New Guinean melanotaeniid reaching 10–12 cm, with progressive coloration development over several months. Males display more intensely in the presence of conspecific rivals. Soft to neutral water preferred. Egg-scatterer among fine vegetation; breeds readily when conditioned. Keep in groups to encourage display behaviour.

diet.fooditems: Omnivore — insects, algae, invertebrates. In aquaria: quality flake, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia.
